name: abs
description: Computes the absolute value of a numeric input, representing its distance from zero. For example, the absolute value of -5 is 5.
short_description: Computes the absolute value (distance from zero) of a number.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The numeric value for which to compute the absolute value.

name: acos
description: Computes the inverse cosine (arccosine) of a number. The input value must be between -1 and 1. The function returns the corresponding angle in radians.
short_description: Computes the inverse cosine of a number, returning the angle in radians.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The numeric value for which to compute the inverse cosine. The value must be between -1 and 1, inclusive.

name: acosh
description: Computes the inverse hyperbolic cosine of a given number.
short_description: Computes the inverse hyperbolic cosine of a number.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The number for which to compute the inverse hyperbolic cosine.

name: add
description: An alias for the `+` operator, performing addition. It can be used for numeric addition, adding an interval to a date/time, adding a number of days to a date, adding two intervals together, or concatenating two lists.
short_description: Performs addition on numeric or temporal values, or concatenates lists (alias for `+`).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The first number.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The second number.

parameters.name: temporal
parameters.description: The date, time, or timestamp.

parameters.name: interval
parameters.description: The interval to add.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The starting date.

parameters.name: days
parameters.description: The number of days to add.

parameters.name: interval1
parameters.description: The first interval.

parameters.name: interval2
parameters.description: The second interval.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The date part.

parameters.name: time
parameters.description: The time part.

parameters.name: list1
parameters.description: The first list.

parameters.name: list2
parameters.description: The second list.

name: add_parquet_key
description: Adds a named encryption key to the current session for reading and writing encrypted Parquet files. The key is stored in-memory and can be 128, 192, or 256 bits. The key value can be provided as a raw string or a base64 encoded string.
short_description: Adds a named encryption key for reading and writing encrypted Parquet files.

parameters.name: key_name
parameters.description: The name to assign to the encryption key.

parameters.name: key_value
parameters.description: The encryption key value, which can be 128, 192, or 256 bits. It can be provided as a raw string or a base64 encoded string.

name: age
description: Calculates the time difference between two timestamps. If two timestamps are provided, it subtracts the second from the first. If only one timestamp is provided, it is subtracted from the current date (`current_date`), not the current timestamp. This is a behavior inherited from PostgreSQL.
short_description: Calculates the time difference between two timestamps, or between a timestamp and the current date.

parameters.name: end_timestamp
parameters.description: The later timestamp (minuend).

parameters.name: start_timestamp
parameters.description: The earlier timestamp (subtrahend).

parameters.name: timestamp
parameters.description: The timestamp to calculate the age of, relative to the current date.

name: ago
description: Subtracts a given interval from the current timestamp, effectively calculating a timestamp in the past. This function is equivalent to executing `current_timestamp - interval`.
short_description: Subtracts an interval from the current timestamp

parameters.name: interval
parameters.description: The interval to subtract from the current timestamp.

name: alias
description: Returns the name of the column or expression it is applied to. This is useful for introspecting query structures or for dynamic query generation.
short_description: Returns the name of a column or expression
usage_example: CREATE TABLE tbl(column1 INT); SELECT alias(column1) FROM tbl;

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The column or expression whose name should be returned.

name: any_value
description: An aggregate function that returns the first non-NULL value from the input expression for each group. Since the choice of the 'first' value is arbitrary without an explicit ordering, it is recommended to use an `ORDER BY` clause within the function call to ensure deterministic results.
short_description: Returns the first non-NULL value from a group of input values.

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The input column or expression from which to select a non-NULL value.

name: approx_count_distinct
description: Calculates the approximate number of distinct elements in a set of values using the HyperLogLog algorithm. This is a more memory-efficient alternative to `count(DISTINCT ...)` for very large datasets where an exact count is not required.
short_description: Calculates the approximate number of distinct elements using HyperLogLog.

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The expression or column for which to approximate the count of distinct values.

name: approx_quantile
description: Calculates the approximate quantile for a set of values using the T-Digest algorithm. This is an approximate aggregate function, which means it provides a fast and memory-efficient estimation of the quantile, suitable for large datasets where an exact calculation would be too slow or resource-intensive. It can compute a single quantile or a list of quantiles.
short_description: Calculates the approximate quantile using the T-Digest algorithm.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The column of values for which to calculate the approximate quantile.

parameters.name: pos
parameters.description: The quantile to compute, a float value between 0.0 and 1.0 inclusive. For example, 0.5 for the median.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The column of values for which to calculate the approximate quantiles.

parameters.name: pos
parameters.description: A list of quantile positions to compute, where each is a float value between 0.0 and 1.0 inclusive.

name: approx_top_k
description: Calculates a list of the 'k' approximately most frequent values for a given column using the Filtered Space-Saving algorithm. This is an approximate aggregate function, meaning it provides a fast and memory-efficient way to find top frequent items without guaranteeing perfect accuracy.
short_description: Calculates a list of the k approximately most frequent values.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The column or expression for which to find the most frequent values.

parameters.name: k
parameters.description: The number of top frequent values to return.

name: arg_max_null
description: Finds the row with the maximum value in the second argument and returns the value of the first argument from that row. This function is order-sensitive. Unlike arg_max, it only ignores rows where the second argument (val) is NULL; NULL values in the first argument (arg) are considered.
short_description: Returns the value of the first argument for the row with the maximum value in the second argument.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The expression to be returned from the row with the maximum `val`.

parameters.name: val
parameters.description: The expression over which to find the maximum value. Rows where this expression is `NULL` are ignored.

name: arg_min_null
description: Finds the row with the minimum value for the second argument (`val`) and returns the value of the first argument (`arg`) from that row. This function is order-sensitive. Unlike `arg_min`, it only ignores rows where the `val` expression is NULL; NULLs in the `arg` expression are still considered.
short_description: Returns the value of the first argument for the row with the minimum value in the second argument, ignoring NULLs in the second argument.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The expression to be returned from the row with the minimum `val`.

parameters.name: val
parameters.description: The expression to find the minimum value from. Rows where this expression is NULL are ignored.

name: argmax
description: Finds the row with the maximum value in the second argument and returns the corresponding value from the first argument. If a third argument is provided, it returns a list of the first argument's values for the top n rows, ordered by the second argument in descending order. Rows where either the first or second argument is NULL are ignored.
short_description: Returns the value of an expression for the row with the maximum value in another expression.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The expression to be returned.

parameters.name: val
parameters.description: The expression to find the maximum value of.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The expression to be returned.

parameters.name: val
parameters.description: The expression to find the maximum values of.

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: The number of top values to return.

name: array_agg
description: This aggregate function collects all input values, including NULLs, into a list. The order of elements in the resulting list can be controlled using an `ORDER BY` clause within the function call. To get a list of unique values, the `DISTINCT` keyword can be used. To exclude `NULL` values, a `FILTER` clause is required as `array_agg` does not ignore them by default.
short_description: Collects all input values, including NULLs, for a column or expression into a list.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE items (category VARCHAR, item_name VARCHAR); INSERT INTO items VALUES ('fruit', 'apple'), ('fruit', 'banana'), ('vegetable', 'carrot'), ('fruit', 'apple'); SELECT category, array_agg(DISTINCT item_name ORDER BY item_name) AS items_in_category FROM items GROUP BY category;

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The column or expression whose values will be collected into a list.

name: array_cosine_distance
description: Computes the cosine distance between two arrays. The arrays must be of the same size and their elements cannot be NULL. The function can handle arrays of any size, provided both arguments have the same dimensions.
short_description: Computes the cosine distance between two arrays of the same size

parameters.name: array1
parameters.description: The first array.

parameters.name: array2
parameters.description: The second array.

name: array_cosine_similarity
description: Computes the cosine similarity between two arrays, which is a measure of similarity between two non-zero vectors. This function is commonly used in natural language processing and machine learning to compare vector embeddings. The input arrays must be of the same size and must not contain any NULL elements.
short_description: Computes the cosine similarity between two arrays of the same size.

parameters.name: array1
parameters.description: The first array for comparison.

parameters.name: array2
parameters.description: The second array for comparison. Must be the same size as the first array.

name: array_cross_product
description: Computes the cross product of two arrays. The function requires both input arrays to be of size 3, and their elements cannot be NULL.
short_description: Computes the cross product of two arrays of size 3.

parameters.name: array1
parameters.description: The first input array of size 3.

parameters.name: array2
parameters.description: The second input array of size 3.

name: array_distance
description: Computes the Euclidean distance (L2-norm squared) between two arrays of the same size. The arrays can have any size, but both must be the same size. The array elements cannot be NULL.
short_description: Computes the Euclidean distance between two arrays of the same size.

parameters.name: array1
parameters.description: The first array.

parameters.name: array2
parameters.description: The second array.

name: array_dot_product
description: Computes the inner product (also known as the dot product) between two arrays. The arrays must be of the same size and must not contain NULL elements. This function is an alias for `array_inner_product`.
short_description: Computes the dot product (inner product) of two arrays of the same size.

parameters.name: array1
parameters.description: The first array.

parameters.name: array2
parameters.description: The second array. Must be the same size as the first array.

name: array_grade_up
description: Returns the indices that would sort the input list. This is similar to sorting the list but returns the original 1-based positions of the elements in sorted order.
short_description: Returns the indices that would sort a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The input list.

parameters.name: sort_order
parameters.description: Specifies the sort order. Can be 'ASC' (ascending) or 'DESC' (descending).

parameters.name: null_order
parameters.description: Specifies how NULL values are ordered. Can be 'NULLS FIRST' or 'NULLS LAST'.

name: array_has_any
description: Checks if two lists have any elements in common, returning true if there is at least one overlapping element. NULL values within the lists are ignored during the comparison.
short_description: Checks if two lists have any elements in common

parameters.name: list1
parameters.description: The first list.

parameters.name: list2
parameters.description: The second list.

name: array_negative_inner_product
description: Computes the negative inner product between two arrays of the same size. The array elements cannot be NULL. The arrays can have any size as long as the size is the same for both arguments.
short_description: Computes the negative inner product between two arrays of the same size.

parameters.name: array1
parameters.description: The first array.

parameters.name: array2
parameters.description: The second array.

name: array_pop_back
description: Returns the list without the last element.
short_description: Returns the list without the last element.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list from which to remove the last element.

name: array_pop_front
description: Returns the input list without its first element.
short_description: Returns the list without the first element.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list from which to remove the first element.

name: array_push_front
description: Prepends a given element to the front of a list, returning a new list.
short_description: Prepends an element to the beginning of a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list to which the element will be prepended.

parameters.name: element
parameters.description: The element to prepend to the list.

name: array_sort
description: Sorts the elements of a list. The sort order can be specified as 'ASC' (ascending) or 'DESC' (descending), and the handling of NULL values can be set to 'NULLS FIRST' or 'NULLS LAST'. By default, the sort is ascending with nulls first.
short_description: Sorts the elements of a list, with options for sort order and null handling.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list to be sorted.

parameters.name: sort_order
parameters.description: The sort order. Can be 'ASC' for ascending or 'DESC' for descending.

parameters.name: null_order
parameters.description: Specifies how NULL values are ordered. Can be 'NULLS FIRST' or 'NULLS LAST'.

name: array_to_string
description: Concatenates the elements of a list into a single string, with each element separated by a specified delimiter. The list elements are converted to their string representations before being joined.
short_description: Concatenates list elements into a single string using a specified delimiter.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of elements to concatenate.

parameters.name: delimiter
parameters.description: The string to use as a separator between list elements.

name: array_to_string_comma_default
description: Concatenates the elements of a given list or array into a single string, using a comma as the delimiter. The elements are cast to strings before concatenation.
short_description: Concatenates list/array elements with a comma delimiter.

parameters.name: array
parameters.description: The list or array whose elements will be concatenated.

name: array_value
description: Creates a fixed-size array (ARRAY) from a list of input values. All provided arguments must be of the same data type.
short_description: Creates an ARRAY containing the argument values.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The first value to be included in the array. At least one argument is required.
Additional values to be included in the array. All arguments must be of the same data type.

name: array_where
description: Returns a list containing elements from the value list where the corresponding element in the mask list is true. Both lists must have the same length.
short_description: Filters a list using a corresponding boolean mask list.

parameters.name: value_list
parameters.description: The input list from which to select elements.

parameters.name: mask_list
parameters.description: A list of booleans of the same length as value_list. A true value indicates the corresponding element in value_list should be included in the result.

name: ascii
description: Returns an integer that represents the Unicode code point of the first character of the string.
short_description: Returns the Unicode code point of the first character of a string.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The input string.

name: asin
description: Computes the inverse sine (arcsine) of a numeric value. The input must be in the range [-1, 1], and the function returns the corresponding angle in radians.
short_description: Computes the inverse sine of a number, returning the angle in radians.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The numeric value for which to calculate the inverse sine. Must be between -1 and 1.

name: asinh
description: Computes the inverse hyperbolic sine of a given number. This is the inverse operation of the hyperbolic sine function (sinh).
short_description: Computes the inverse hyperbolic sine of a number.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The numeric value for which to compute the inverse hyperbolic sine.

name: atan2
description: Computes the inverse tangent of the division of the two arguments (y/x), using the signs of both arguments to determine the correct quadrant for the result. The function returns the angle in radians between the positive x-axis and the point (x, y).
short_description: Computes the inverse tangent of (y, x), returning the angle in radians.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The y-coordinate of the point.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The x-coordinate of the point.

name: atan
description: Computes the inverse tangent of a numeric value. The result is the angle in radians whose tangent is the specified number.
short_description: Computes the inverse tangent of a number, returning the angle in radians.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The numeric value for which to calculate the inverse tangent.

name: atanh
description: Computes the inverse hyperbolic tangent of a number.
short_description: Computes the inverse hyperbolic tangent of a number.

name: avg
description: Calculates the average (mean) of all non-null input values. The function is order-sensitive for floating-point calculations to ensure deterministic results.
short_description: Calculates the average (mean) of all non-null values in a group.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE sales(region VARCHAR, amount INTEGER); INSERT INTO sales VALUES ('north', 100), ('north', 150), ('south', 200), ('south', 300); SELECT region, avg(amount) FROM sales GROUP BY region;

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The column of values to average.

name: bar
description: Draws a text-based bar chart. The width of the bar is proportional to the input value `x` relative to the range defined by `min` and `max`. When `x` equals `max`, the bar's width will be equal to the specified `width` in characters.
short_description: Draws a text-based bar chart proportional to a value within a range

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The input value to represent with the bar.

parameters.name: min
parameters.description: The minimum value of the range.

parameters.name: max
parameters.description: The maximum value of the range.

parameters.name: width
parameters.description: The maximum width of the bar in characters.

name: bit_and
description: Computes the bitwise AND of all non-null input values in a group. This operation can be applied to both integer types and bitstrings.
short_description: Computes the bitwise AND of all non-null input values.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The input values (integer or bitstring) to aggregate.

name: bit_count
description: Counts the number of set bits (1s) in a given integer or bitstring. For integers, this is also known as the population count (popcount).
short_description: Counts the number of set bits (1s) in an integer or bitstring.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The integer value to count the set bits of.

parameters.name: bitstring
parameters.description: The bitstring to count the set bits of.

name: bit_or
description: Computes the bitwise OR of all non-null input values. This function can be applied to both integer types and bitstrings. When used with bitstrings, all inputs in a group must have the same length.
short_description: Computes the bitwise OR of all non-null input values.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The input expression to aggregate.

name: bit_position
description: Returns the first starting index of a specified substring within a bitstring. If the substring is not found, it returns zero. The indexing is 1-based, meaning the first (leftmost) bit is at index 1.
short_description: Returns the 1-based starting index of a substring within a bitstring, or 0 if not found.

parameters.name: substring
parameters.description: The substring to search for within the bitstring.

parameters.name: bitstring
parameters.description: The bitstring to search within.

name: bit_xor
description: Computes the bitwise XOR (exclusive OR) of all non-null input values. This function works for both integer types and bitstrings.
short_description: Computes the bitwise XOR for all values in a set.

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The column or expression over which to compute the bitwise XOR. Can be an integer or a bitstring.

name: bitstring
description: Creates a bitstring of a specified length. The input can be a bitstring or a string representation of one. If the specified length is greater than the length of the input bitstring, the result is left-padded with zeros.
short_description: Returns a bitstring of a determined length, left-padding with zeros if necessary.

parameters.name: bitstring
parameters.description: The input bitstring or a string that can be cast to a bitstring.

parameters.name: length
parameters.description: The desired length of the output bitstring.

name: bitstring_agg
description: Aggregates distinct integer values from a column into a single bitstring. For each unique integer in the input, the corresponding bit in the output bitstring is set to 1. The length of the bitstring is determined by the range of the input values (from minimum to maximum). This range can be inferred automatically from the data, or specified explicitly by providing `min` and `max` arguments. When `min` and `max` are provided, all input values must fall within that range.
short_description: Aggregates distinct integer values into a bitstring, setting a bit for each unique value.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The integer values to aggregate.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The integer values to aggregate.

parameters.name: min
parameters.description: The minimum value for the bitstring range.

parameters.name: max
parameters.description: The maximum value for the bitstring range.

name: bool_and
description: Returns true if every input value is true, otherwise it returns false. This function performs a logical AND operation on all boolean values in a group.
short_description: Returns true if all input values in a group are true, otherwise false.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The boolean expression to aggregate.

name: bool_or
description: Returns true if any input value is true, otherwise false.
short_description: Returns true if any input value is true, otherwise false

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The boolean expression to evaluate.

name: broadcast
description: Computes the broadcast address for the address's network.
short_description: Computes the broadcast address for an INET address's network
usage_example: SELECT cidr, broadcast(cidr) AS broadcast FROM (VALUES ('192.168.1.5/24'), ('127.0.0.1'), ('2001:db8:3c4d:15::1a2f:1a2b/96')) AS tbl(cidr);

parameters.name: address
parameters.description: The INET address for which to compute the broadcast address.

name: can_cast_implicitly
description: Checks whether a value of a given source data type can be implicitly cast (automatically converted) to a target data type. This is useful for determining if operations between different types will succeed without an explicit CAST.
short_description: Checks if a value's type can be implicitly cast to a target type.

parameters.name: source_value
parameters.description: The value or column with the source data type.

parameters.name: target_value
parameters.description: The value or column representing the target data type.

name: cardinality
description: Returns the size of the map, which is the number of key-value pairs (entries) it contains.
short_description: Returns the number of entries in a map.

parameters.name: map
parameters.description: The map to get the size of.

name: cast_to_type
description: Casts the first argument to the data type of the second argument. This function is primarily useful in macros, as it allows you to maintain the original data types of columns after performing operations, which is crucial for creating generic macros that can operate on different types.
short_description: Casts an expression to the data type of another expression

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The expression or value to be cast.

parameters.name: target_type_expression
parameters.description: An expression whose data type will be used as the target type for the cast.

name: cbrt
description: Returns the cube root of a number.
short_description: Returns the cube root of a number.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The input number.

name: ceil
description: Rounds a number up to the nearest integer. This function is the counterpart to the floor function, which rounds down.
short_description: Rounds a number up to the nearest integer

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The numeric value to round up.

name: century
description: Extracts the century from a given date, timestamp, or interval. The century is calculated based on the Gregorian calendar. For example, the 20th century includes the years 1901 through 2000.
short_description: Extracts the century from a date, timestamp, or interval.

parameters.name: temporal
parameters.description: The date, timestamp, or interval from which to extract the century.

name: checkpoint
description: Synchronizes the Write-Ahead Log (WAL) with the database file. This operation can be performed on the main database or an optionally specified attached database. The checkpoint process runs without interrupting ongoing transactions.
short_description: Synchronizes the WAL with the database file without interrupting transactions.

parameters.name: database
parameters.description: The name of the database to checkpoint. If omitted, the main database is checkpointed.

name: chr
description: Returns the character corresponding to the given ASCII code value or Unicode code point.
short_description: Returns the character for a given ASCII or Unicode code point.

parameters.name: code_point
parameters.description: The ASCII code value or Unicode code point.

name: col_description
description: Retrieves the comment or description for a specific column in a table. The column is identified by the table's object ID (OID) and the column's number.
short_description: Retrieves the description for a table column
usage_example: SELECT col_description(16401, 2); -- Retrieves description for column 2 of the table with OID 16401.

parameters.name: table_oid
parameters.description: The object ID (OID) of the table containing the column.

parameters.name: column_number
parameters.description: The number of the column for which to retrieve the description.

name: collations
description: Lists all available collations in the database. Collations provide rules for sorting and comparing text, which is essential for localization. By default, DuckDB includes `NOCASE`, `NOACCENT`, and `NFC`. Additional language- and region-specific collations become available after loading the ICU extension.
short_description: Lists all available collations for sorting and comparing text.

name: concat
description: Concatenates multiple strings or lists. Unlike the `||` operator, this function skips any `NULL` inputs.
short_description: Concatenates multiple strings or lists, skipping NULL values.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The first value to concatenate.
Additional values to concatenate.

name: concat_ws
description: Concatenates multiple values into a single string, using a specified separator. Any NULL input values are skipped and will not be included in the final result.
short_description: Concatenates multiple values into a string with a separator, skipping NULLs.

parameters.name: separator
parameters.description: The string to use as a separator between the concatenated values.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The first value to concatenate. Non-string values will be cast to strings.
Additional values to concatenate.

name: constant_or_null
description: If the second argument is NULL, the function returns NULL. Otherwise, it returns the first argument.
short_description: If the second argument is NULL, returns NULL. Otherwise, returns the first argument.

parameters.name: arg1
parameters.description: The value to return if the second argument is not NULL.

parameters.name: arg2
parameters.description: The value to check for NULL.

name: corr
description: Calculates the Pearson correlation coefficient for a set of number pairs. The function takes two numeric columns as input, representing the dependent (y) and independent (x) variables. It ignores pairs where either value is NULL.
short_description: Calculates the Pearson correlation coefficient for two numeric columns.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE measurements (id INT, value1 DOUBLE, value2 DOUBLE); INSERT INTO measurements VALUES (1, 1.0, 2.0), (2, 2.0, 4.1), (3, 3.0, 5.9); SELECT corr(value2, value1) FROM measurements;

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The dependent variable.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The independent variable.

name: cos
description: Computes the cosine of the input angle, which is specified in radians.
short_description: Computes the cosine of an angle in radians

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The input angle in radians.

name: cosh
description: Computes the hyperbolic cosine of a given number.
short_description: Computes the hyperbolic cosine of a number.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The input number.

name: cot
description: Computes the cotangent of a given number, where the number is interpreted as an angle in radians.
short_description: Computes the cotangent of an angle in radians.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The input number, representing an angle in radians.

name: count
description: The count function is an aggregate function that returns the number of rows. When called without an argument (as `count()` or `count(*)`), it returns the total number of rows in the group. When called with an argument (`count(expression)`), it returns the number of rows for which the expression is not NULL. The `DISTINCT` keyword can be used to count only the unique non-null values of the expression.
short_description: Counts the number of rows or the number of non-null values in an expression.
usage_example: SELECT count(*) AS total_sales, count(DISTINCT region) AS unique_regions FROM sales;

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The expression to evaluate. If this argument is provided, the function counts rows where the expression is not NULL. If omitted (or if `*` is used), the function counts all rows.

name: count_if
description: An aggregate function that counts the number of rows where the input expression is true or a non-zero number. Rows where the expression is false, zero, or NULL are not counted.
short_description: Counts rows where the condition is true or a non-zero number.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE values(i INTEGER); INSERT INTO values VALUES (1), (0), (2), (NULL), (-1); SELECT count_if(i) FROM values;

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The condition to evaluate. Rows are counted if this expression is true or a non-zero number.

name: count_star
description: A special form of the count aggregate function that counts all input rows, regardless of NULL values. It is used to determine the total number of rows within a group or an entire table.
short_description: Counts the total number of rows in a group, including NULLs.

name: covar_pop
description: Calculates the population covariance, a measure of the joint variability of two variables. This version does not include bias correction. It ignores pairs where either input is NULL.
short_description: Calculates the population covariance without bias correction

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The dependent variable.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The independent variable.

name: create_fts_index
description: Creates a Full-Text Search (FTS) index for a specified table. This PRAGMA builds the index in a new schema named after the input table (e.g., an index on 'main.table_name' creates a schema named 'fts_main_table_name'). Various options can be provided to customize the indexing process, such as specifying a stemmer, a list of stopwords, patterns to ignore, and whether to convert text to lowercase or strip accents.
short_description: Creates a Full-Text Search (FTS) index on specified table columns.

parameters.name: input_table
parameters.description: Qualified name of the table to be indexed, e.g., 'table_name' or 'main.table_name'.

parameters.name: input_id
parameters.description: Column name of the document identifier, e.g., 'document_identifier'.

parameters.name: input_values
parameters.description: One or more column names of the text fields to be indexed. Use '*' to index all VARCHAR columns.

parameters.name: stemmer
parameters.description: The type of stemmer to be used. Use 'none' if no stemming is desired.

parameters.name: overwrite
parameters.description: If true, overwrites an existing FTS index on the table.

parameters.name: ignore
parameters.description: A regular expression of patterns to be ignored during tokenization.

parameters.name: stopwords
parameters.description: Qualified name of a table containing a single VARCHAR column of stopwords, or 'none' if no stopwords are to be used. Defaults to 'english' for a pre-defined list.

parameters.name: lower
parameters.description: If true, converts all text to lowercase.

parameters.name: strip_accents
parameters.description: If true, removes accents from characters (e.g., converts 'á' to 'a').

name: create_sort_key
description: Constructs a binary-comparable sort key based on a set of input parameters and sort qualifiers.
short_description: Constructs a binary-comparable sort key from input values and sort qualifiers.

parameters.name: parameters
parameters.description: A variable number of input values and their corresponding sort qualifiers (e.g., 'ASC NULLS FIRST', 'DESC NULLS LAST').

name: cume_dist
description: Calculates the cumulative distribution of a value within a set of values. It is computed as (number of partition rows preceding or peer with current row) / total partition rows. If an ORDER BY clause is specified, the distribution is computed within the frame using the provided ordering instead of the frame ordering.
short_description: The cumulative distribution: (number of partition rows preceding or peer with current row) / total partition rows.
usage_example: SELECT cume_dist() OVER (ORDER BY sales) FROM transactions;

parameters.name: ORDER BY ordering
parameters.description: If an ORDER BY clause is specified, the distribution is computed within the frame using the provided ordering instead of the frame ordering.

name: current_catalog
description: Returns the name of the currently active catalog. The default catalog is 'memory'.
short_description: Returns the name of the currently active catalog.

name: current_database
description: Returns the name of the currently active database.
short_description: Returns the name of the currently active database.

name: current_localtime
description: Returns a TIME value whose GMT bin values correspond to the local time in the current time zone.
short_description: Returns the current local time in the current time zone.

name: current_localtimestamp
description: Returns the current local date and time at the start of the transaction. The returned timestamp's values correspond to the local date and time in the current time zone, but it is of the TIMESTAMP type (timezone-naive).
short_description: Returns the current local date and time at the start of the transaction.

name: current_query
description: Returns the current SQL query as a string.
short_description: Returns the current SQL query as a string.

name: current_schema
description: Returns the name of the currently active schema. The default schema is 'main'.
short_description: Returns the name of the currently active schema.

name: current_schemas
description: Returns a list of schemas. Pass a parameter of true to include implicit schemas.
short_description: Returns a list of schemas, optionally including implicit ones.

parameters.name: include_implicit
parameters.description: If set to true, implicit schemas (like 'pg_catalog') are included in the result.

name: current_setting
description: Returns the current value of a specified configuration setting. This function is used to query the current state of various DuckDB options, such as memory limits, thread counts, or default behaviors.
short_description: Returns the current value of a specified configuration setting.

parameters.name: setting_name
parameters.description: The name of the configuration setting whose value is to be returned.

name: currval
description: Returns the current value of a sequence. Note that the `nextval` function must be called at least once in the current session before `currval` can be used, otherwise an error will be thrown.
short_description: Returns the current value of a sequence, after `nextval` has been called at least once in the session.

parameters.name: sequence_name
parameters.description: The name of the sequence.

name: damerau_levenshtein
description: An extension of Levenshtein distance that also includes transposition of adjacent characters as an allowed edit operation. In other words, it is the minimum number of edit operations (insertions, deletions, substitutions or transpositions) required to change one string to another. Characters of different cases (e.g., `a` and `A`) are considered different.
short_description: Calculates the Damerau-Levenshtein distance between two strings, which is the minimum number of edits (including transpositions) to change one string into the other.

parameters.name: s1
parameters.description: The first string.

parameters.name: s2
parameters.description: The second string.

name: database_list
description: A pragma statement that lists all attached databases. It returns a table with the sequence number, name, and file path for each database.
short_description: Lists all attached databases and their file paths.

name: database_size
description: Returns information about the file and memory size of each database. This pragma can be called directly or used as a table function in a FROM clause.
short_description: Fetches the file and memory size of each database.

name: date_add
description: Adds a specified interval to a date, returning a timestamp. This function is an alternative to using the `+` operator for date arithmetic.
short_description: Adds an interval to a date, returning a timestamp.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The base date to which the interval will be added.

parameters.name: interval
parameters.description: The interval to add to the date.

name: date_diff
description: Calculates the signed number of specified date or time part boundaries crossed between a start and end date/time. The calculation is inclusive of the larger timestamp and exclusive of the smaller one. For example, `date_diff('hour', ... '23:59:59', ... '01:58:00')` returns 2 because the hour boundaries at 00:00 and 01:00 are crossed.
short_description: Calculates the number of date part boundaries between two temporal values.

parameters.name: part
parameters.description: The date or time part to count (e.g., 'year', 'month', 'day', 'hour').

parameters.name: start_temporal
parameters.description: The starting date, time, or timestamp.

parameters.name: end_temporal
parameters.description: The ending date, time, or timestamp.

name: date_part
description: Extracts a specific subfield (like year, month, day) from a temporal value such as a date, time, timestamp, or interval. It can extract a single part, returning a numeric value, or multiple parts at once, returning a struct. This function is equivalent to the EXTRACT function.
short_description: Extracts a subfield from a date, time, timestamp, or interval.

parameters.name: part
parameters.description: The time unit to extract (e.g., 'year', 'month', 'day', 'hour').

parameters.name: temporal
parameters.description: The date, time, timestamp, or interval to extract the part from.

parameters.name: parts
parameters.description: A list of time units to extract (e.g., ['year', 'month', 'day']).

parameters.name: temporal
parameters.description: The date, time, timestamp, or interval to extract the parts from.

name: date_sub
description: Calculates the signed length of the interval between two temporal values, truncated to whole multiples of a specified part. This function counts the number of full units of the specified part between the two timestamps, rather than the number of boundaries crossed (like date_diff).
short_description: Calculates the interval between two temporal values, truncated to a specified part

parameters.name: part
parameters.description: The date or time part to measure the interval in (e.g., 'year', 'month', 'day', 'hour').

parameters.name: start_temporal
parameters.description: The starting date, time, or timestamp.

parameters.name: end_temporal
parameters.description: The ending date, time, or timestamp.

name: date_trunc
description: Truncates a temporal value (date, timestamp, or interval) to a specified precision. For example, truncating a timestamp to the 'hour' will set the minute, second, and microsecond parts to zero. The `part` argument specifies the field to which the input value should be truncated, such as 'year', 'month', 'day', 'hour', etc.
short_description: Truncates a date, timestamp, or interval to a specified precision.

parameters.name: part
parameters.description: The time unit to truncate to (e.g., 'year', 'month', 'day', 'hour', 'minute').

parameters.name: temporal_value
parameters.description: The date, timestamp, or interval to truncate.

name: day
description: Extracts the day of the month (an integer from 1-31) from a temporal value. This function can be applied to DATE, TIMESTAMP, TIMESTAMPTZ, and INTERVAL types.
short_description: Extracts the day of the month from a date, timestamp, or interval.

parameters.name: temporal
parameters.description: The date, timestamp, or interval from which to extract the day.

name: dayname
description: Extracts the full name of the day of the week from a date or timestamp. For example, for a date that falls on a Sunday, it will return the string 'Sunday'.
short_description: The (English) name of the weekday.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The date or timestamp from which to extract the day name.

name: dayofweek
description: Extracts the day of the week from a date, timestamp, or interval. The function returns a numeric value where Sunday is 0 and Saturday is 6.
short_description: Returns the day of the week as an integer (Sunday = 0, Saturday = 6).

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The temporal value (date, timestamp, or interval) from which to extract the day of the week.

name: dayofyear
description: Extracts the day of the year from a date, timestamp, or interval. The day of the year is an integer between 1 and 366, where January 1 is 1.
short_description: Extracts the day of the year (1-366) from a date, timestamp, or interval.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The date, timestamp, or interval from which to extract the day of the year.

name: dbgen
description: Generates TPC-H benchmark data. This function is part of the TPC-H extension and populates the standard TPC-H tables (customer, lineitem, nation, etc.) based on a specified scale factor. Note that this function does not automatically clean up existing tables; you may need to drop them manually before generating new data. The data generation can be parallelized by using the 'children' and 'step' parameters to generate partitions of the data. Setting the scale factor to 0 will generate the schema without any data.
short_description: Generates TPC-H benchmark data at a specified scale factor.

parameters.name: sf
parameters.description: Scale factor for data generation.

parameters.name: children
parameters.description: The total number of partitions to divide the data generation into.

parameters.name: step
parameters.description: Defines the partition to be generated, indexed from 0 to `children` - 1. Must be defined when the `children` argument is defined.

parameters.name: catalog
parameters.description: Target catalog for the generated tables.

parameters.name: suffix
parameters.description: A suffix to append to the names of the generated tables.

parameters.name: overwrite
parameters.description: This parameter is not used.

name: decade
description: Extracts the decade from a date, timestamp, or interval. The decade is calculated by dividing the year component by 10.
short_description: Extracts the decade (year / 10) from a date, timestamp, or interval.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The date, timestamp, or interval from which to extract the decade.

name: decode
description: Converts a BLOB to a VARCHAR. The function will fail if the provided BLOB is not a valid UTF-8 encoded string.
short_description: Converts a BLOB to a VARCHAR string, failing if the BLOB is not valid UTF-8.

parameters.name: blob
parameters.description: The BLOB to be converted.

name: degrees
description: Converts a numeric value representing an angle in radians to its equivalent in degrees.
short_description: Converts an angle from radians to degrees

parameters.name: radians
parameters.description: The angle in radians to be converted.

name: delta_scan
description: Reads data from a Delta Lake table. The function can read from local file paths as well as remote locations like S3 or Azure Blob Storage. It supports various optimizations such as multithreaded scans, filter and projection pushdown, and data skipping based on Parquet metadata and Delta partition information. This function is part of the experimental 'delta' extension.
short_description: Scans a Delta Lake table from a local or remote path.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: The path to the Delta Lake table. Can be a local path or a URL to a remote resource (e.g., 's3://bucket/table', 'az://container/table').

name: dense_rank
description: The rank of the current row without gaps; this function counts peer groups.
short_description: The rank of the current row without gaps; this function counts peer groups.
usage_example: SELECT dense_rank() OVER (PARTITION BY "Plant" ORDER BY "Date") AS rank FROM "Generation History";

name: disable_checkpoint_on_shutdown
description: Disables the automatic running of a `CHECKPOINT` on a clean shutdown. This means the Write-Ahead Log (WAL) will not be deleted, and the database will remain as two separate files (the main database file and the WAL file).
short_description: Prevents running a checkpoint on shutdown.

name: disable_object_cache
description: Disables the object cache, which is used to store items like Parquet metadata to speed up subsequent queries.
short_description: Disables the object cache for Parquet metadata and other objects

name: disable_optimizer
description: Disables the query optimizer for subsequent queries. This can be useful for debugging performance issues but should generally be avoided in production environments. The optimizer can be re-enabled using `PRAGMA enable_optimizer`.
short_description: Disables the query optimizer.

name: disable_profiling
description: Disables query profiling, stopping the collection of performance metrics for subsequent queries. This is the counterpart to `enable_profiling`.
short_description: Disables query profiling

name: disable_progress_bar
description: Disables the progress bar that is displayed for running queries. This is a PRAGMA statement that alters the internal state of the database engine.
short_description: Disables the progress bar for running queries.

name: disable_verification
description: Disables query verification. This pragma is primarily used for development and internal testing purposes.
short_description: Disables query verification (for development).

name: disable_verify_external
description: Disables the verification of external operators. This setting is primarily used for development and internal testing.
short_description: Disables verification of external operators.

name: disable_verify_parallelism
description: Disables forced parallel query processing. This pragma is primarily used for development and internal testing purposes.
short_description: Disables forced parallel query processing (for development/testing)

name: disable_verify_serializer
description: Disables the verification of round-trip capabilities for supported logical plans. This pragma is primarily used for development and internal testing purposes.
short_description: Disables verification of round-trip capabilities for logical plans.

name: divide
description: Performs integer division, which is an alias for the `//` operator. If both inputs are integers, the result is an integer. If either input is a floating-point number, it performs floating-point division.
short_description: Performs integer division (e.g., 5 // 2 = 2)

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The dividend.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The divisor.

name: drop_fts_index
description: Drops a Full-Text Search (FTS) index for the specified table. This pragma is part of the 'fts' extension and is used to remove an index previously created by `PRAGMA create_fts_index`.
short_description: Drops a Full-Text Search (FTS) index for a specified table.

parameters.name: input_table
parameters.description: Qualified name of the table for which to drop the FTS index, e.g., 'table_name' or 'main.table_name'.

name: dsdgen
description: Generates the TPC-DS benchmark dataset. This function is part of the TPC-DS extension and creates the necessary tables and populates them with data according to the specified scale factor. Setting the scale factor to 0 will generate the schema without any data.
short_description: Generates the TPC-DS benchmark dataset at a given scale factor.

parameters.name: sf
parameters.description: The scale factor for data generation. A scale factor of 1 corresponds to approximately 1GB of data. A scale factor of 0 generates only the schema.

name: duckdb_columns
description: The duckdb_columns() function provides metadata about the columns available in the DuckDB instance. It returns a table with detailed information for each column, including its name, data type, position within the table, and other properties. This function is useful for schema exploration and can be queried like any other table. For a more standardized view, consider using information_schema.columns, which is built on top of this function but filters out internal objects.
short_description: Provides metadata about the columns available in the DuckDB instance.
usage_example: SELECT column_name, data_type, comment FROM duckdb_columns() WHERE database_name = 'my_database' AND table_name = 'my_table';

name: duckdb_constraints
description: The duckdb_constraints() function provides metadata about the constraints available in the DuckDB instance. The result set returned by this function can be used like an ordinary table or view, for example, in the FROM clause of a SELECT statement.
short_description: Provides metadata about the constraints in the database.

name: duckdb_databases
description: Lists the databases that are accessible from within the current DuckDB process. This includes the database associated at startup, as well as any databases that were attached later on.
short_description: Lists all accessible databases, including attached ones.

name: duckdb_dependencies
description: Provides metadata about the dependencies available in the DuckDB instance.
short_description: Provides metadata about the dependencies in the DuckDB instance.

name: duckdb_extensions
description: Provides metadata about the extensions available in the DuckDB instance. This function returns a table that lists all available extensions, indicating whether they are currently loaded or installed, their version, installation path, and a brief description of their functionality.
short_description: Provides metadata about available, installed, and loaded DuckDB extensions.
usage_example:
SELECT extension_name, installed, description
FROM duckdb_extensions();

name: duckdb_functions
description: The duckdb_functions() table function provides metadata about the functions and macros available in the DuckDB instance. It returns a table with detailed information for each function, including its schema, name, type (scalar, aggregate, table, etc.), return type, parameter names and types, and whether it's a built-in or user-defined function. This function is useful for inspecting the available functions in the system.
short_description: Provides metadata about the functions and macros available in the DuckDB instance.

name: duckdb_indexes
description: Provides metadata about secondary indexes available in the DuckDB instance. Note that this function only provides metadata about secondary indexes, i.e., those indexes created by explicit CREATE INDEX statements. Primary keys, foreign keys, and UNIQUE constraints are maintained using indexes, but their details are included in the duckdb_constraints() function.
short_description: Provides metadata about secondary indexes in the database.

name: duckdb_keywords
description: The duckdb_keywords() function provides metadata about DuckDB's keywords and reserved words. It returns a table with the keyword's name and its category, which can be 'column_name', 'reserved', 'type_function', or 'unreserved'. This is useful for understanding which words have special meaning in DuckDB's SQL dialect and must be quoted if used as identifiers.
short_description: Provides metadata about DuckDB's keywords and reserved words.

name: duckdb_log_contexts
description: Provides information on the contexts of DuckDB log entries. This table function returns details about different operational contexts, such as connections, transactions, and queries, which can be linked to entries in the `duckdb_logs` table.
short_description: Provides information on the contexts of DuckDB log entries.

name: duckdb_logs
description: Returns a table of DuckDB log entries. The logging behavior, such as the log level and storage location, can be configured using `SET` commands. This function provides access to the logs that have been recorded.
short_description: Returns a table of DuckDB log entries.

name: duckdb_memory
description: The duckdb_memory() function provides metadata about DuckDB's buffer manager.
short_description: Provides metadata about DuckDB's buffer manager.

name: duckdb_optimizers
description: Provides metadata about the optimization rules (e.g., `expression_rewriter`, `filter_pushdown`) available in the DuckDB instance. These can be selectively turned off using `PRAGMA disabled_optimizers`.
short_description: Provides metadata about the available query optimization rules.

name: duckdb_prepared_statements
description: Provides metadata about the prepared statements that exist in the current DuckDB session.
short_description: Provides metadata about prepared statements in the current session.

name: duckdb_proj_compiled_version
description: Returns a text description of the PROJ library version that this instance of DuckDB was compiled against.
short_description: Returns the version of the PROJ library DuckDB was compiled against.

name: duckdb_proj_version
description: Returns a text description of the PROJ library version that is being used by this instance of DuckDB.
short_description: Returns the version of the PROJ library currently in use by DuckDB.

name: duckdb_schemas
description: Provides metadata about the schemas available in the DuckDB instance. The information_schema.schemata system view provides a more standardized way to obtain metadata about database schemas.
short_description: Provides metadata about the schemas available in the DuckDB instance

name: duckdb_secret_types
description: Lists the secret types that are supported in the current DuckDB session. This table function returns details for each secret type, including its name, default provider, and the extension that registered it.
short_description: Lists secret types supported in the current session.

name: duckdb_secrets
description: Provides metadata about the secrets available in the DuckDB instance. Secrets are used to manage credentials for external services and can be created using the CREATE SECRET statement. This function returns a table with details for each configured secret, including its name, type, provider, and scope. By default, sensitive information within the returned secret_string is redacted.
short_description: Lists metadata about the secrets available in the DuckDB instance.

parameters.name: redact
parameters.description: If set to false, sensitive information within the secret_string will not be redacted. Defaults to true.

name: duckdb_sequences
description: Provides metadata about the sequences available in the DuckDB instance. The returned columns correspond to the various options available in the CREATE SEQUENCE statement and will always be filled out, even if they were not explicitly specified. Note that the `last_value` column is NULL if no value was ever drawn from the sequence, and 1 if a value was drawn, not the actual last value. Also, if a sequence cycles, it will restart from the boundary of its range, not necessarily from the specified start value.
short_description: Provides metadata about sequences, such as their start value, increment, and cycle behavior.
usage_example: SELECT sequence_name, start_value, increment_by FROM duckdb_sequences();

name: duckdb_settings
description: The duckdb_settings() table function provides metadata about all available configuration settings in the DuckDB instance. It returns a table containing the name of each setting, its current value, a description of what it does, its expected input data type, and its scope (either LOCAL or GLOBAL). This function is useful for inspecting the current state of the database configuration.
short_description: Returns metadata about the available configuration settings in the DuckDB instance.

name: duckdb_tables
description: Provides metadata about the base tables available in the DuckDB instance. This function returns a table with detailed information for each table, including its database and schema, name, comments, size estimates, and structural details like column and index counts. It is useful for programmatically exploring the database schema.
short_description: Provides metadata about the base tables in the DuckDB instance.
usage_example: SELECT database_name, schema_name, table_name, comment FROM duckdb_tables() WHERE database_name = 'my_db';

name: duckdb_temporary_files
description: Provides metadata about the temporary files DuckDB has written to disk to offload data from memory. This function is primarily intended for debugging and testing purposes.
short_description: Provides metadata about temporary files DuckDB has written to disk.

name: duckdb_types
description: Provides metadata about the data types available in the DuckDB instance. This includes built-in types as well as user-defined types created with the CREATE TYPE statement.
short_description: Provides metadata about the data types available in the DuckDB instance.

name: duckdb_variables
description: Provides metadata about the variables available in the DuckDB instance. This table function returns a result set with columns detailing the name, value, and data type of each defined variable.
short_description: Provides metadata about the variables available in the DuckDB instance.

name: duckdb_views
description: The duckdb_views() table function provides metadata about the views available in the DuckDB instance. The returned result set can be used like a regular table or view. This function returns all views, including those marked as internal. For a filtered list of non-internal views, you can query the duckdb_views view instead.
short_description: Provides metadata about all views, including internal ones, in the DuckDB instance.

name: ducklake_cleanup_old_files
description: Cleans up old files in the specified DuckLake catalog. This function can be configured to perform a dry run, clean up all files regardless of age, or only clean up files older than a specific timestamp.
short_description: Cleans up old files in a DuckLake catalog.

parameters.name: catalog
parameters.description: The name of the DuckLake catalog to clean up.

name: ducklake_expire_snapshots
description: Expires snapshots in a DuckLake catalog. Snapshots can be specified either by a list of version numbers or by a cutoff timestamp, expiring all snapshots older than the given time. Upon success, it returns a table with a single 'Success' column, which will be empty.
short_description: Expires old or specified snapshots in a DuckLake catalog.

parameters.name: catalog
parameters.description: The name of the DuckLake catalog.

parameters.name: older_than
parameters.description: Expire all snapshots created before this timestamp.

parameters.name: versions
parameters.description: A list of specific snapshot versions to expire.

name: ducklake_merge_adjacent_files
description: Merges adjacent files in the storage for a specified DuckLake catalog. Upon success, it returns a table with a single column (`Success`) and 0 rows.
short_description: Merges adjacent files in a DuckLake catalog.

name: ducklake_snapshots
description: Returns the snapshots stored in the DuckLake catalog specified by the catalog name.
short_description: Returns the snapshots stored in a DuckLake catalog

name: ducklake_table_changes
description: Returns the rows that have changed in a given table between two snapshots. The snapshots can be identified by their version numbers or timestamps. The returned table includes all columns from the original table, plus three additional columns: `snapshot_id`, `rowid`, and `change_type` (which can be 'insert' or 'delete') to describe the change.
short_description: Returns the rows changed in a table between two snapshots, identified by version or timestamp.

name: ducklake_table_deletions
description: Returns the rows deleted from a given DuckLake table between two specified snapshots. The snapshots can be identified either by their version numbers or by timestamps.
short_description: Returns rows deleted from a DuckLake table between two snapshots.

parameters.name: catalog
parameters.description: The name of the DuckLake catalog.

parameters.name: schema_name
parameters.description: The name of the schema containing the table.

parameters.name: table_name
parameters.description: The name of the table to query for deletions.

parameters.name: start_snapshot
parameters.description: The starting snapshot, specified as a version number (BIGINT) or a timestamp.

parameters.name: end_snapshot
parameters.description: The ending snapshot, specified as a version number (BIGINT) or a timestamp.

name: ducklake_table_info
description: Returns information on the tables stored in the specified DuckLake catalog. The function provides details such as table identifiers, file counts, and total file sizes for both data and deletion files.
short_description: Returns information on the tables stored in a DuckLake catalog.

name: ducklake_table_insertions
description: Returns the rows inserted in a given table between two specified snapshots. The snapshots can be identified either by their version numbers or by their timestamps.
short_description: Returns rows inserted in a table between two snapshots.

name: embedding
description: Generates vector representations (embeddings) of text directly from SQL. These embeddings capture semantic meaning, enabling powerful similarity search and other natural language processing tasks. The function uses OpenAI's models, either 'text-embedding-3-small' (the default) or 'text-embedding-3-large'. The maximum input size is 2048 characters; larger inputs will be truncated.
short_description: Generates vector representations (embeddings) of text using OpenAI models.
usage_example: SELECT embedding('This is a sample text', model:='text-embedding-3-large') AS text_embedding;

parameters.name: text_input
parameters.description: The text to be converted into an embedding vector.

parameters.name: model
parameters.description: Model type, either 'text-embedding-3-small' (default) or 'text-embedding-3-large'.

name: enable_checkpoint_on_shutdown
description: Enables running a `CHECKPOINT` on successful shutdown. This process also deletes the Write-Ahead Log (WAL), ensuring that only a single, consolidated database file remains after shutdown.
short_description: Runs a CHECKPOINT on successful shutdown, deleting the WAL.

name: enable_logging
description: This function enables and configures DuckDB's logging system. It allows specifying the logging level, storage type (memory, stdout, or file), and other storage-related options like file path and buffer size. This provides a programmatic way to control logging behavior, similar to using SET commands for logging configuration options.
short_description: Enables and configures the logging system with specified level and storage options.

parameters.name: level
parameters.description: The log level which will be recorded in the log.

parameters.name: storage
parameters.description: The storage medium for logs (e.g., 'memory', 'stdout', 'file').

parameters.name: storage_config
parameters.description: Custom configuration for the specified storage type.

parameters.name: storage_path
parameters.description: The file path for log storage when the storage type is 'file'.

parameters.name: storage_normalize
parameters.description: Whether to normalize the storage path.

parameters.name: storage_buffer_size
parameters.description: The buffer size for the log storage.

name: enable_object_cache
description: This is a legacy setting that is documented to do nothing. It was originally intended to enable caching of objects, such as Parquet metadata, in memory to speed up subsequent queries.
short_description: Enables the object cache (legacy setting, does nothing).

name: enable_optimizer
description: Enables the query optimizer. The optimizer is responsible for creating an efficient execution plan for SQL queries. This is the default behavior.
short_description: Enables the query optimizer

name: enable_print_progress_bar
description: Enables the progress bar, which is displayed when running queries. This provides visual feedback on the progress of long-running operations.
short_description: Enables the progress bar for running queries

name: enable_profile
description: Enables query profiling. This is a mandatory first step before any profiling information can be collected. The format of the output can be configured using other profiling-related settings.
short_description: Enables query profiling

name: enable_verification
description: Enables query verification. This function is primarily intended for development and internal testing purposes.
short_description: Enables query verification, mainly for development and internal testing.

name: encode
description: Converts a string to a BLOB, encoding UTF-8 characters into their literal byte representation.
short_description: Converts a string to a BLOB

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to be converted to a BLOB.

name: ends_with
description: Returns true if a string ends with a specified substring. This function is case-sensitive and does not support collations.
short_description: Checks if a string ends with a specified suffix

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to check.

parameters.name: search_string
parameters.description: The suffix to search for at the end of the string.

name: entropy
description: Calculates the log-2 entropy of the input values. It is a measure of the uncertainty or randomness in a set of data.
short_description: Calculates the log-2 entropy of a set of values

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The column of values for which to calculate the entropy.

name: enum_code
description: Returns the numeric value backing the given enum value. ENUMs are internally stored as integers, and this function retrieves that integer representation.
short_description: Returns the numeric value backing a given enum value.
usage_example:
CREATE TYPE mood AS ENUM ('sad', 'ok', 'happy', 'anxious');
SELECT enum_code('happy'::mood);

parameters.name: enum_value
parameters.description: The enum value for which to retrieve the backing numeric code.

name: enum_first
description: Returns the first value of the input enum type. The result depends only on the type of the argument and not on its value.
short_description: Returns the first value of the input enum type.
usage_example:
CREATE TYPE mood AS ENUM ('sad', 'ok', 'happy', 'anxious');
SELECT enum_first(NULL::mood);

parameters.name: enum_value
parameters.description: An enum value. The function uses the type of this value, not the value itself.

name: enum_last
description: Returns the last value of a given ENUM type. The result depends only on the type of the argument and not on its specific value.
short_description: Returns the last value of the input enum type.
usage_example:
CREATE TYPE mood AS ENUM ('sad', 'ok', 'happy', 'anxious');
SELECT enum_last(NULL::mood);

parameters.name: enum
parameters.description: An enum value or NULL of the target enum type. The specific value does not matter, only its type.

name: enum_range
description: Returns all values of the input enum type as an array. The result depends only on the type of the argument and not on its specific value.
short_description: Returns all values of an enum type as an array.
usage_example:
CREATE TYPE mood AS ENUM ('sad', 'ok', 'happy', 'anxious');
SELECT enum_range(NULL::mood);

parameters.name: enum
parameters.description: An enum value or NULL of the target enum type. The specific value does not matter, only its type.

name: enum_range_boundary
description: Returns the range between two given enum values as an array. The values must be of the same enum type. When the first parameter is NULL, the result starts with the first value of the enum type. When the second parameter is NULL, the result ends with the last value of the enum type.
short_description: Returns the range between two enum values as an array.
usage_example:
CREATE TYPE mood AS ENUM ('sad', 'ok', 'happy', 'anxious');
SELECT enum_range_boundary(NULL, 'happy'::mood);

parameters.name: start
parameters.description: The starting enum value of the range. If NULL, the range starts from the first value of the enum type.

parameters.name: end
parameters.description: The ending enum value of the range. If NULL, the range ends with the last value of the enum type.

name: epoch
description: Calculates the total number of seconds since the Unix epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC) for a given temporal value. When the input is an interval, it returns the total number of seconds contained within that interval.
short_description: Returns the total number of seconds since the Unix epoch for a temporal value, or the total seconds in an interval.

parameters.name: temporal
parameters.description: The temporal value (date, timestamp, time, or interval) to convert.

name: epoch_ms
description: This function has two main purposes. When given a temporal value (such as a TIMESTAMP, DATE, or INTERVAL), it returns the total number of milliseconds since the Unix epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC). Conversely, when given a BIGINT value, it interprets it as the number of milliseconds since the epoch and returns the corresponding TIMESTAMP.
short_description: Converts a timestamp to milliseconds since epoch, or vice-versa.

parameters.name: temporal
parameters.description: The temporal value to convert to milliseconds since the epoch.

parameters.name: milliseconds
parameters.description: The total number of milliseconds since the epoch to convert to a timestamp.

name: epoch_ns
description: Converts a temporal value (such as a timestamp, date, time, or interval) into the total number of nanoseconds since the Unix epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC). For DATE and TIMESTAMP types, it represents the number of nanoseconds from the epoch to that point in time. For TIME types, it represents the number of nanoseconds from midnight. For INTERVAL types, it represents the total duration in nanoseconds.
short_description: Converts a temporal value to the total number of nanoseconds since the epoch

parameters.name: temporal
parameters.description: The temporal value to convert.

name: epoch_us
description: Converts a temporal value (timestamp, date, time, or interval) into the total number of microseconds since the epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC). For DATE types, it represents the number of microseconds from the epoch to the beginning of that day. For TIME types, it's the number of microseconds since midnight. For INTERVAL types, it represents the total duration in microseconds.
short_description: Converts a temporal value to the total number of microseconds since the epoch.

parameters.name: temporal
parameters.description: The temporal value to convert.

name: equi_width_bins
description: Returns a list of upper boundaries that partition the interval from a minimum to a maximum value into a specified number of equal-sized subintervals. This is often used to generate boundaries for the `histogram` function. An optional parameter can be set to adjust the minimum, maximum, and bin count to produce more aesthetically pleasing, or 'nice', results.
short_description: Generates a list of upper boundaries for equal-sized bins within a given range.

parameters.name: min
parameters.description: The minimum value of the interval.

parameters.name: max
parameters.description: The maximum value of the interval.

parameters.name: bincount
parameters.description: The number of equal-sized subintervals to partition the interval into.

parameters.name: nice
parameters.description: If true, adjusts min, max, and bincount to produce more aesthetically pleasing results.

name: era
description: Extracts the calendar era from a date, timestamp, or interval. The Gregorian era is returned as 1 for CE/AD and 0 for BCE/BC.
short_description: Extracts the calendar era (CE/AD or BCE/BC) from a temporal value.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The date, timestamp, or interval from which to extract the era.

name: error
description: Throws a user-defined error, causing the query to fail and return the specified message. This function is useful for enforcing custom validation logic within a SQL query.
short_description: Throws a user-defined error message
usage_example: SELECT error('This is a custom error message');

parameters.name: message
parameters.description: The error message to be thrown.

name: even
description: Rounds a number to the next even integer by rounding away from zero.
short_description: Rounds a number to the next even integer, rounding away from zero

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The input number.

name: excel_text
description: Formats a given number according to the rules specified in an Excel-style format string. This function wraps the i18npool library to provide Excel-compatible number formatting.
short_description: Formats a number according to an Excel-style format string.

parameters.name: number
parameters.description: The number to be formatted.

parameters.name: format_string
parameters.description: The Excel-style format string that specifies the formatting rules.

name: exp
description: Computes the exponential function, raising Euler's number (e) to the power of the input value.
short_description: Computes e to the power of x.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The exponent to which e is raised.

name: factorial
description: Computes the factorial of an integer, which is the product of that integer and all positive integers below it. This function is an alternative to using the `!` postfix operator.
short_description: Computes the factorial of an integer.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The integer for which to compute the factorial.

name: favg
description: Calculates the average of a set of numbers using the Kahan summation algorithm. This method is more accurate for floating-point numbers as it reduces numerical error that can occur with standard summation. The result can be affected by the order of the input values.
short_description: Calculates the average using a more accurate floating point summation (Kahan Sum).
usage_example: CREATE TABLE numbers(d DOUBLE); INSERT INTO numbers VALUES (10000.0), (3.14159), (100000000.0), (-10000.0), (-100000000.0), (-3.14159); SELECT favg(d) FROM numbers;

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The column of numbers to average.

name: fdiv
description: Performs integer division (`x // y`) but returns a DOUBLE value.
short_description: Performs integer division and returns a DOUBLE value.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The dividend.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The divisor.

name: fill
