description: Replaces NULL values of `expr` with a linear interpolation based on the closest non-NULL values and the sort values. Both values must support arithmetic and there must be only one ordering key. For missing values at the ends, linear extrapolation is used. Failure to interpolate results in the NULL value being retained.
short_description: Fill in missing values using linear interpolation with ORDER BY as the X-axis.
usage_example: SELECT fill(column) OVER (ORDER BY date) FROM sales;

parameters.name: expr
parameters.description: The expression containing NULL values to be filled. This expression must be of a type that supports arithmetic.

parameters.name: ORDER BY ordering
parameters.description: The ordering key for the interpolation, which acts as the X-axis. There must be only one ordering key, and its type must support arithmetic.

name: filter
description: Constructs a new list containing elements from an input list for which a given lambda function returns true. The lambda function's return value must be castable to a boolean. The returned list is of the same type as the input list. The lambda function can optionally accept a second parameter representing the 1-based index of the element.
short_description: Filters a list based on a lambda function, returning elements for which the function is true.
usage_example: SELECT filter([5, -6, NULL, 7], lambda x: x > 0);

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The input list to be filtered.

parameters.name: lambda
parameters.description: The lambda function to apply as a filter condition. It must return a boolean value. The function can take the list element and, optionally, its 1-based index as parameters.

name: first
description: Returns the first value from a group of rows. This function is order-sensitive, meaning the result depends on the order of rows within the group. For deterministic results, it should be used with an ORDER BY clause inside the function call (e.g., first(column ORDER BY other_column)). Unlike many other aggregate functions, first does not ignore NULL values. This makes it particularly useful for pivoting data with a FILTER clause, as it can correctly handle cases where a CASE WHEN expression would produce NULL.
short_description: Returns the first value in a group, including NULLs. Use an ORDER BY clause for deterministic results.
usage_example: SELECT first(amount ORDER BY date ASC) FROM sales;

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The column or expression from which to get the first value.

name: first_value
description: Returns `expr` evaluated at the row that is the first row (with a non-null value of `expr` if `IGNORE NULLS` is set) of the window frame. If an `ORDER BY` clause is specified, the first row number is computed within the frame using the provided ordering instead of the frame ordering.
short_description: Returns `expr` evaluated at the row that is the first row of the window frame.
usage_example: SELECT first_value(column) OVER (PARTITION BY group_col ORDER BY order_col)

parameters.name: expr
parameters.description: The expression to be evaluated.

parameters.name: ORDER BY ordering
parameters.description: If an `ORDER BY` clause is specified, the first row is determined within the frame using the provided ordering instead of the frame ordering.

parameters.name: IGNORE NULLS
parameters.description: If `IGNORE NULLS` is specified, the function returns the value from the first row with a non-null value of `expr`. The default is `RESPECT NULLS`.

name: flatten
description: Converts a list of lists into a single list by concatenating each sub-list. This function only flattens one level at a time. For example, a list of lists of lists will become a list of lists. The function has specific behavior for empty lists and NULL values: an empty input list returns an empty list, a NULL input returns NULL, and a list containing only NULLs returns an empty list.
short_description: Flattens a nested list by one level

parameters.name: nested_list
parameters.description: The nested list to be flattened.

name: floor
description: Rounds the given numeric value down to the nearest integer.
short_description: Rounds a number down to the nearest integer

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The numeric value to round down.

name: fmod
description: Calculates the floating-point remainder (modulo) of dividing two numbers. This function always returns a DOUBLE value.
short_description: Calculates the floating-point remainder (modulo) of two numbers.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The dividend.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The divisor.

name: force_checkpoint
description: Forces a checkpoint to be performed when the CHECKPOINT statement is called, even if no changes have been made to the database. If a database name is provided, this setting applies only to that specific database. If no database is specified, the setting applies globally.
short_description: Forces a checkpoint even if no changes have been made to the database, optionally for a specific database.

parameters.name: database
parameters.description: The name of the database for which to force checkpoints. If omitted, the setting applies globally.

name: force_checkpoint
description: Forces a checkpoint by synchronizing the Write-Ahead Log (WAL) with the database file, interrupting any active transactions. This function will force a checkpoint even if no changes have been made to the database. The database to checkpoint can be specified optionally.
short_description: Forces a checkpoint by synchronizing the WAL with the database file, interrupting active transactions.

parameters.name: database
parameters.description: The name of the database to checkpoint. If omitted, the current database is used.

name: format
description: Formats a string using a syntax loosely based on the {fmt} open-source formatting library. The function takes a format string with placeholders (e.g., '{}' or '{0}') and a variable number of arguments to substitute into those placeholders.
short_description: Formats a string using the fmt library syntax

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: The format string containing placeholders like {} or positional specifiers like {0}.
A variable number of arguments to be formatted into the string.

name: format_bytes
description: Converts an integer value into a human-readable string representation of bytes, using units based on powers of 2 (e.g., KiB, MiB, GiB).
short_description: Converts an integer to a human-readable byte representation (KiB, MiB, etc.).

parameters.name: integer
parameters.description: The integer value representing the number of bytes.

name: format_pg_type
description: Converts a DuckDB data type name into its corresponding PostgreSQL-compatible type name string. For example, it maps 'INTEGER' to 'int4', 'DOUBLE' to 'float8', and 'BLOB' to 'bytea'. For ENUM types, the specific type name must be provided as the second argument.
short_description: Converts a DuckDB data type name to its PostgreSQL-compatible equivalent.

parameters.name: logical_type
parameters.description: The DuckDB data type name to convert.

parameters.name: type_name
parameters.description: The name of the ENUM type. This is only used when the logical_type is 'ENUM'.

name: formatreadabledecimalsize
description: Converts an integer value into a human-readable string representation of a size, using units based on powers of 10 (e.g., KB, MB, GB).
short_description: Converts an integer size to a human-readable string with decimal units (KB, MB, GB)

parameters.name: integer
parameters.description: The integer value to be formatted.

name: from_base64
description: Converts a base64 encoded string to a character string (BLOB).
short_description: Converts a base64 encoded string to a BLOB

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The base64 encoded string to convert.

name: from_binary
description: Converts a string from its binary representation to a blob.
short_description: Converts a string from binary representation to a blob

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The string in binary representation to convert.

name: from_hex
description: Converts a string value from its hexadecimal representation to a blob. This function is an alias for the `unhex` function.
short_description: Converts a hexadecimal string to a blob

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The hexadecimal string to convert.

name: from_json
description: Transforms a JSON object into nested SQL types like LIST and STRUCT, based on a provided structure definition. This function is an alias for json_transform. The structure argument defines the desired output schema and types. It is possible to extract fewer key/value pairs than are present in the JSON, and it is also possible to extract more; missing keys will be filled with NULL.
short_description: Transforms a JSON object into nested SQL types (LIST, STRUCT) based on a specified structure.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE example (j JSON); INSERT INTO example VALUES ('{"family": "anatidae", "species": ["duck", "goose"], "coolness": 42.42}'), ('{"family": "canidae", "species": ["labrador", "bulldog"], "hair": true}'); SELECT from_json(j, '{"family": "VARCHAR", "coolness": "DOUBLE"}') FROM example;

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object or string to be transformed.

parameters.name: structure
parameters.description: A string defining the target structure and data types. This string should be in the same JSON format as the output of the json_structure function.

name: fsum
description: Calculates the sum of a set of numbers using the Kahan summation algorithm. This method is more accurate for floating-point numbers as it reduces numerical error compared to a standard summation. The result of this function can be affected by the order of the input values.
short_description: Calculates the sum using a more accurate floating point summation (Kahan Sum).

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The column of floating-point numbers to sum.

name: functions
description: The `functions` pragma lists all functions available in the DuckDB instance, including scalar, aggregate, table, pragma, and macro functions. It provides metadata similar to the `duckdb_functions()` table function.
short_description: Lists all available functions and their metadata.

name: gamma
description: Computes the gamma function, which is an interpolation of the factorial of the input value minus one. Fractional inputs are allowed.
short_description: Computes the gamma function (interpolation of the factorial of x - 1).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The input value for the gamma function.

name: gcd
description: Computes the greatest common divisor of two numbers.
short_description: Computes the greatest common divisor of two numbers.

name: generate_series
description: Generates a series of values from a start point to an inclusive stop point, with a defined step. This function can generate series of numbers (BIGINT) or time-based values (TIMESTAMP or TIMESTAMPTZ). Unlike the `range` function, the `stop` value is always included in the result. For numeric series, the function can be called with one, two, or three arguments, with defaults for `start` (0) and `step` (1) being applied. For temporal series, all three arguments (`start`, `stop`, `step`) are required.
short_description: Creates an inclusive list of values from a start to a stop point.

parameters.name: stop
parameters.description: The inclusive upper bound of the series. The series starts at 0 with a step of 1.

parameters.name: start
parameters.description: The inclusive lower bound of the series.

parameters.name: stop
parameters.description: The inclusive upper bound of the series.

parameters.name: step
parameters.description: The increment between values in the series. Defaults to 1.

parameters.name: start
parameters.description: The inclusive lower bound of the series (TIMESTAMP or TIMESTAMPTZ).

parameters.name: stop
parameters.description: The inclusive upper bound of the series (TIMESTAMP or TIMESTAMPTZ).

parameters.name: step
parameters.description: The interval to increment between values in the series.

name: generate_series
description: Generates a table containing a sequence of values from a specified start to a stop point, with a defined step. Unlike the `range` function, the stop value is inclusive. The function can generate series of numbers (integers) or timestamps. For numeric series, if only a stop value is provided, the start defaults to 0 and the step defaults to 1. For timestamp series, all parameters (start, stop, step) must be provided.
short_description: Generates a table with a sequence of values from start to stop (inclusive).

parameters.name: stop
parameters.description: The inclusive upper bound of the series. The start is assumed to be 0 and the step is assumed to be 1.

parameters.name: start
parameters.description: The inclusive lower bound of the series.

parameters.name: stop
parameters.description: The inclusive upper bound of the series.

parameters.name: step
parameters.description: The increment between values in the series.

parameters.name: start
parameters.description: The inclusive lower bound of the series.

parameters.name: stop
parameters.description: The inclusive upper bound of the series.

parameters.name: step
parameters.description: The increment between values in the series.

name: generate_subscripts
description: Generates a set of 1-based indexes (subscripts) for a given dimension of an array. This function returns a column of integers corresponding to the positions of elements in the array's specified dimension. It is often used with `unnest` to track the original position of each element after unnesting.
short_description: Generates a set of 1-based indexes for an array dimension.
usage_example:
SELECT unnest(l) AS x, generate_subscripts(l, 1) AS index
FROM (VALUES ([1, 2, 3]), ([4, 5])) tbl(l);

parameters.name: arr
parameters.description: The input array for which to generate subscripts.

parameters.name: dim
parameters.description: The dimension of the array to generate subscripts for.

name: geomean
description: Calculates the geometric mean of all non-null values in a set. The geometric mean is the n-th root of the product of n numbers. This function is implemented as `exp(avg(ln(arg)))` and will return an error if any input value is zero or negative.
short_description: Calculates the geometric mean of a set of non-null, positive numeric values.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The input column of positive numeric values.

name: get_bit
description: Extracts the nth bit from a bitstring. The first (leftmost) bit is indexed 0.
short_description: Extracts the nth bit from a bitstring (0-indexed)

parameters.name: bitstring
parameters.description: The bitstring from which to extract the bit.

parameters.name: index
parameters.description: The 0-based index of the bit to extract.

name: get_current_time
description: Returns the current time at the start of the current transaction, in the local timezone. This function takes no arguments.
short_description: Returns the current time in the local timezone at the start of the transaction.

name: getvariable
description: Retrieves the value of a SQL-level variable that was previously defined using the SET VARIABLE statement. If the specified variable has not been set, the function returns NULL. The returned value can be of any data type, as variables can store various types such as integers, dates, strings, maps, or lists.
short_description: Retrieves the value of a previously set SQL variable.
usage_example:
SET VARIABLE my_var = 30;
SELECT 20 + getvariable('my_var') AS total;

parameters.name: variable_name
parameters.description: The name of the variable whose value is to be retrieved.

name: glob
description: A table function that returns filenames matching a given search path. The search path may contain glob pattern matching syntax (e.g., '*', '?', '**', '[...]'). The function returns a single-column table named 'file' containing the paths of all matching files.
short_description: Finds and returns filenames that match a given glob pattern.

parameters.name: search_path
parameters.description: The path to search, which may include glob patterns. Can be a single path or a list of paths.

name: greatest
description: Returns the largest value from a list of arguments. The function can handle various data types, including numeric, string, date, and timestamp values. For strings, the comparison is based on lexicographical order, where lowercase characters are considered larger than uppercase characters. For temporal types, it returns the latest value. Collations are not supported.
short_description: Returns the largest value from a list of arguments.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The first value in the list to compare.
One or more subsequent values to compare. All arguments must be of a comparable data type.

name: h3_are_neighbor_cells
description: Checks if two H3 cells are direct neighbors. The function returns true if the cells share an edge, and false otherwise. The cell IDs can be provided as integers or strings.
short_description: Checks if two H3 cell IDs are directly adjacent.

parameters.name: origin_cell
parameters.description: The H3 cell ID of the origin cell.

parameters.name: neighbor_cell
parameters.description: The H3 cell ID of the potential neighbor cell.

name: h3_cell_area
description: Calculates the area of a specific H3 cell. The area is returned in the specified unit.
short_description: Get the area of a cell ID

parameters.name: cell
parameters.description: The H3 cell index.

parameters.name: unit
parameters.description: The unit of area. Supported units are 'km^2' for square kilometers and 'm^2' for square meters.

name: h3_cell_to_boundary_wkb
description: Converts an H3 cell ID to its boundary, represented as a Well-Known Binary (WKB) polygon. The input cell ID can be provided as a BIGINT, UBIGINT, or VARCHAR.
short_description: Converts an H3 cell ID to its boundary as a WKB BLOB

parameters.name: cell_id
parameters.description: The H3 cell ID.

name: h3_cell_to_boundary_wkt
description: Converts an H3 cell ID to its boundary, represented as a Well-Known Text (WKT) polygon. The input cell ID can be provided as a numeric (BIGINT or UBIGINT) or string type.
short_description: Convert H3 cell ID to its boundary as a WKT polygon

parameters.name: cell_id
parameters.description: The H3 cell ID.

name: h3_cell_to_center_child
description: Returns the H3 index of the center child cell for the given parent cell at a specified finer resolution. The child resolution must be greater than the parent's resolution.
short_description: Get the center finer cell for a cell

parameters.name: cell
parameters.description: The parent H3 cell index.

parameters.name: resolution
parameters.description: The desired resolution for the child cell. This must be a finer resolution (a higher integer value) than the parent cell's resolution.

name: h3_cell_to_child_pos
description: Returns the sub-indexing number for a child cell relative to its parent at a specified resolution. This number, from 0 to 6, indicates the position of the child cell within the parent's grid.
short_description: Get a sub-indexing number for a cell inside a parent

parameters.name: child_cell
parameters.description: The H3 index of the child cell.

parameters.name: parent_resolution
parameters.description: The resolution of the parent cell.

name: h3_cell_to_children
description: Returns an array of H3 cell indexes for the children of the given cell index at the specified resolution. The child resolution must be greater than the parent cell's resolution.
short_description: Get finer cells for a cell

parameters.name: cell
parameters.description: The parent H3 cell index.

parameters.name: resolution
parameters.description: The desired resolution for the child cells. This must be a larger integer (finer resolution) than the parent cell's resolution.

name: h3_cell_to_children_size
description: Calculates the number of finer (higher resolution) child cells contained within a given parent H3 cell at a specified child resolution. The child resolution must be greater than the parent cell's resolution.
short_description: Calculates the number of finer child cells for a given H3 cell.

parameters.name: cell
parameters.description: The H3 cell index.

parameters.name: child_resolution
parameters.description: The resolution for the finer child cells. Must be greater than the resolution of the input cell.

name: h3_cell_to_lat
description: Converts an H3 cell ID to its corresponding latitude.
short_description: Convert cell ID to latitude

parameters.name: cell_id
parameters.description: The H3 cell ID.

name: h3_cell_to_latlng

name: histogram
description: Returns a map representing a histogram of the input values. The keys of the map are the buckets (or bins), and the values are the counts of items falling into each bucket. There are two ways to use this function. The first version automatically determines the buckets based on the input data. The second version allows you to specify the upper boundaries for each bin, providing more control over the histogram's structure. For values larger than the highest provided boundary, a special 'other' bin is automatically created.
short_description: Creates a histogram by returning a map of buckets and their corresponding counts.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The column of values to create a histogram from.

parameters.name: boundaries
parameters.description: A list of upper boundaries for the histogram bins. If not provided, buckets are determined automatically. The bins are left-open and right-closed partitions.

name: histogram
description: Computes a histogram over a specified column and returns a table containing the bin ranges, the count of values in each bin, and a visual bar chart representation of the count. This function is a macro that calls the `histogram_values` function and formats its output for display.
short_description: Computes and displays a histogram for a column.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE temps(city VARCHAR, temp_c INT); INSERT INTO temps VALUES ('SF', 15), ('SF', 18), ('SF', 12), ('OAK', 20), ('OAK', 22); SELECT * FROM histogram('temps', 'temp_c', 2);

parameters.name: source
parameters.description: The name of the source table as a string.

parameters.name: col_name
parameters.description: The name of the column to compute the histogram on, as a string.

parameters.name: bin_count
parameters.description: The number of bins to create for the histogram.

parameters.name: technique
parameters.description: The technique used for binning.

name: histogram_exact
description: Returns a map of key-value pairs representing the counts for a specified list of elements. If other elements are present in the data, they are counted in a special 'catch-all' bin. The key for this catch-all bin is data-type dependent and can be identified using the is_histogram_other_bin function.
short_description: Returns a map of key-value pairs representing the counts of specific, requested elements.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE values(val INT); INSERT INTO values VALUES (1), (2), (2), (3), (3), (3), (4); SELECT histogram_exact(val, [1, 3]) FROM values;

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The input column for which to compute the histogram.

parameters.name: elements
parameters.description: A list of specific elements to count within the input column.

name: histogram_values
description: Computes a histogram for a given column from a source table and returns a table containing the upper boundaries of the bins and their respective counts. Different techniques can be specified for creating the bins, such as creating bins of equal width or equal height.
short_description: Returns the upper boundaries of histogram bins and their counts for a column.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE integers(i INTEGER); INSERT INTO integers VALUES (1), (2), (3), (4), (5); SELECT * FROM histogram_values('integers', 'i', bin_count := 2);

parameters.name: source
parameters.description: The name of the source table containing the data.

parameters.name: col_name
parameters.description: The name of the column to compute the histogram on.

parameters.name: bin_count
parameters.description: The number of bins for the histogram.

parameters.name: technique
parameters.description: The binning technique to use. 'auto' uses 'equi-width-nice' for numeric/temporal types and 'sample' for others. 'sample' uses the most frequent values as bins. 'equi-height' creates bins with an equal number of values. 'equi-width' creates bins of equal width. 'equi-width-nice' creates bins of equal width with human-friendly boundaries.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: The input string containing characters to be escaped.

parameters.name: flag
parameters.description: An optional boolean flag. Its specific behavior is not documented.

name: html_unescape
description: Converts HTML character entities within a string back to their corresponding characters. For example, it will convert '&' to '&', '<' to '<', and so on.
short_description: Decodes HTML character entities in a string.

parameters.name: html_string
parameters.description: The string containing HTML entities to be decoded.

name: iceberg_metadata
description: Accesses the metadata of an Iceberg table. This function returns detailed information about the table's structure, including manifests, data files, their status (e.g., ADDED, DELETED), format, and record counts. It requires a path to the Iceberg table and supports several optional parameters to control how metadata is read, such as specifying a particular snapshot by ID or timestamp, or allowing for moved paths.
short_description: Accesses and returns the metadata of an Iceberg table, including manifests and data file information.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: The path to the Iceberg table. When used with an attached Iceberg REST catalog, this can be a fully qualified table name (e.g., 'my_datalake.default.t').

parameters.name: allow_moved_paths
parameters.description: Allows scanning Iceberg tables that are moved.

parameters.name: metadata_compression_codec
parameters.description: Treats metadata files as compressed when set to 'gzip'.

parameters.name: snapshot_from_id
parameters.description: Access snapshot with a specific id.

parameters.name: snapshot_from_timestamp
parameters.description: Access snapshot with a specific timestamp.

parameters.name: version
parameters.description: Provides an explicit version string, hint file or guessing ('?').

parameters.name: version_name_format
parameters.description: Controls how versions are converted to metadata file names.

name: iceberg_scan
description: Scans and reads data from an Apache Iceberg table. The function can take a path to the root of an Iceberg table or a direct path to a specific metadata file. It supports various options to control versioning, snapshot selection, and path resolution, and can read from object stores like S3 when used with the httpfs extension.
short_description: Scans and reads data from an Apache Iceberg table.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: Path to the Iceberg table directory or a specific metadata file.

parameters.name: allow_moved_paths
parameters.description: Allows scanning Iceberg tables that have been moved from their original location.

parameters.name: version
parameters.description: Specifies the metadata version to use. Can be a version number, a path to a version hint file, or '?' to guess the latest version.

parameters.name: version_name_format
parameters.description: A format string or comma-separated list of strings to control how version numbers are converted to metadata file names.

parameters.name: metadata_compression_codec
parameters.description: Specifies the compression codec for metadata files. Set to 'gzip' if metadata files are gzipped.

parameters.name: snapshot_from_id
parameters.description: Accesses the table state from a snapshot with a specific ID.

parameters.name: snapshot_from_timestamp
parameters.description: Accesses the table state from the most recent snapshot at or before a specific timestamp.

name: iceberg_snapshots
description: Visualizes the snapshots in an Iceberg table, showing metadata for each snapshot such as its sequence number, ID, timestamp, and manifest list. This function can be used on a file path to an Iceberg table or on a table within an attached Iceberg REST catalog. It supports various options to handle different metadata naming conventions and versions.
short_description: Visualizes the snapshots in an Iceberg table.

parameters.name: table_path
parameters.description: The path to the Iceberg table directory or a fully qualified table name from an attached Iceberg REST catalog.

parameters.name: version
parameters.description: Specifies the metadata version to use. Can be a version number, a path to a version hint file, or '?' to guess the latest version.

parameters.name: metadata_compression_codec
parameters.description: Specifies the compression codec for metadata files. Set to 'gzip' if metadata files are compressed.

parameters.name: version_name_format
parameters.description: A comma-delimited list of format strings to control how version numbers are converted to metadata file names.

name: iceberg_table_properties
description: Returns all properties of the specified Iceberg table as key-value pairs. This function allows you to inspect the metadata properties associated with an Iceberg table, such as write modes or other custom settings.
short_description: Returns all properties of a specified Iceberg table.

parameters.name: table
parameters.description: The fully qualified name of the Iceberg table.

name: iceberg_to_ducklake
description: Performs a metadata-only copy from an attached Iceberg catalog to a DuckLake database. This allows Iceberg tables to be queried as if they were DuckLake tables. An optional list of tables can be provided to exclude them from the copy.
short_description: Copies metadata from an Iceberg catalog to a DuckLake database.
usage_example:
ATTACH 'ducklake:my_ducklake.ducklake' AS my_ducklake;
CALL iceberg_to_ducklake('iceberg_datalake', 'my_ducklake', skip_tables := ['table_to_skip']);

parameters.name: iceberg_catalog
parameters.description: The alias of the attached Iceberg catalog from which to copy metadata.

parameters.name: ducklake_database
parameters.description: The alias of the attached DuckLake database to which the metadata will be copied.

parameters.name: skip_tables
parameters.description: An optional list of table names to exclude from the metadata copy.

name: icu_calendar_names
description: Returns a table containing the names of all calendars supported by the ICU extension. This function is useful for discovering which calendar systems can be set using the `SET Calendar` command for temporal operations.
short_description: Returns a list of available calendar names supported by the ICU extension.
usage_example: SELECT name FROM icu_calendar_names() ORDER BY 1;

name: icu_sort_key
description: Generates a surrogate sort key for a string, which allows for correct sorting of special characters according to a specific locale. The collator parameter, which specifies the locale, is optional. This function is only available when the ICU extension is installed.
short_description: Generates a sort key for sorting strings with special characters according to a specific locale.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The input string for which to generate the sort key.

parameters.name: collator
parameters.description: The locale identifier (e.g., 'DE' for German) that defines the sorting rules.

name: ilike_escape
description: Checks if a string matches a given pattern case-insensitively, similar to the ILIKE operator, but with a specified escape character. The escape character allows for matching literal wildcard characters ('%' and '_') within the string.
short_description: Performs a case-insensitive LIKE pattern match with a custom escape character

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The input string to check against the pattern.

parameters.name: like_specifier
parameters.description: The pattern to match against the string. It can contain wildcards: '%' for any sequence of characters and '_' for any single character.

parameters.name: escape_character
parameters.description: A single character used to escape the meaning of wildcard characters in the pattern, allowing them to be matched as literal characters.

name: import_database
description: Reloads a database from a directory. The directory must have been previously created using the EXPORT DATABASE command. This command restores the full contents of the database, including schema information, tables, views, and sequences.
short_description: Reloads a database from a directory previously created by EXPORT DATABASE.

parameters.name: source_directory
parameters.description: The directory from which to load the database. This directory must have been created by the EXPORT DATABASE command.

name: instr
description: Returns the location of the first occurrence of a substring within a string, counting from 1. If no match is found, it returns 0.
short_description: Returns the 1-based starting position of the first occurrence of a substring within a string.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to search within.

parameters.name: search_string
parameters.description: The substring to search for.

name: is_histogram_other_bin
description: Checks if a given value represents the special 'catch-all' or 'other' bin in the output of histogram functions like `histogram` and `histogram_exact`. This special bin is used to count elements that do not fall into any of the explicitly defined bins or boundaries.
short_description: Checks if a value is the special 'other' bin used in histogram functions.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The value to check.

name: isfinite
description: Returns true if the input value is finite, and false otherwise. This function can be used with numeric types (like FLOAT) and temporal types (like DATE and TIMESTAMP). A finite value is any value that is not positive infinity, negative infinity, or NaN (Not a Number).
short_description: Checks if a numeric or temporal value is finite.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The value to check for finiteness.

name: isinf
description: Checks if a given value is infinite. This function can be used with floating-point numbers as well as temporal types like dates and timestamps. It returns true if the value represents positive or negative infinity, and false for any other value.
short_description: Checks if a numeric or temporal value is infinite

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The value to check for infinity. Can be a floating-point number, date, or timestamp.

name: isnan
description: Returns true if the floating point value is not a number, false otherwise.
short_description: Checks if a floating-point value is Not a Number (NaN)

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The floating point value to check.

name: isodow
description: Extracts the ISO day of the week from a date, timestamp, or interval. The ISO standard considers Monday as the first day of the week (1) and Sunday as the last (7).
short_description: Extracts the ISO day of the week (Monday=1, Sunday=7).

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The date, timestamp, or interval from which to extract the ISO day of the week.

name: isoyear
description: Extracts the ISO Year number from a date, timestamp, or interval. The ISO year starts on the Monday of the week containing January 4th.
short_description: Extracts the ISO year number from a date, timestamp, or interval.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The date, timestamp, or interval from which to extract the ISO year.

name: jaccard
description: Calculates the Jaccard similarity between two strings. This metric evaluates the similarity between two sets by taking the size of their intersection divided by the size of their union. For strings, the sets are the unique characters in each string. Characters of different cases (e.g., 'a' and 'A') are considered distinct. The function returns a floating-point number between 0 and 1, where 1 indicates identical sets of characters and 0 indicates no common characters.
short_description: Calculates the Jaccard similarity between two strings, returning a value between 0 and 1.

parameters.name: s1
parameters.description: The first string.

parameters.name: s2
parameters.description: The second string.

name: jaro_similarity
description: Calculates the Jaro similarity between two strings, a measure of their similarity. The result is a floating-point number between 0 (no similarity) and 1 (exact match). Characters of different cases are treated as distinct. An optional score cutoff can be provided; if the calculated similarity is below this cutoff, the function returns 0.
short_description: Calculates the Jaro similarity between two strings, returning a value between 0 and 1.

parameters.name: s1
parameters.description: The first string to compare.

parameters.name: s2
parameters.description: The second string to compare.

parameters.name: score_cutoff
parameters.description: If the calculated similarity is below this value, the function returns 0.

name: jaro_winkler_similarity
description: Calculates the Jaro-Winkler similarity between two strings, which is a measure of text similarity. The result is a score between 0 (no similarity) and 1 (identical strings). Characters of different cases are treated as different. An optional score cutoff can be provided, causing any similarity score below the cutoff to be returned as 0.
short_description: Calculates the Jaro-Winkler similarity between two strings, returning a score from 0 to 1.

parameters.name: s1
parameters.description: The first string to compare.

parameters.name: s2
parameters.description: The second string to compare.

parameters.name: score_cutoff
parameters.description: If the calculated similarity is below this value, the function returns 0 instead.

name: json
description: Parses a string containing JSON data or a JSON object and returns it as a minified value of type JSON. This function can be used to validate and canonicalize JSON strings. It is a macro for `json_extract(json, '$')`, which extracts the root of the JSON document.
short_description: Parses and minifies a JSON string or value.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON value or string to be parsed and minified.

name: json_array
description: Creates a JSON array from a variable number of input values. Each argument provided to the function becomes an element in the resulting JSON array.
short_description: Creates a JSON array from the provided values.

parameters.name: values
parameters.description: A variable number of values of any type to be included in the JSON array.

name: json_array_length
description: Returns the number of elements in a JSON array. If the input is not a JSON array, it returns 0. An optional path parameter can be provided to specify the location of the array within a larger JSON document. If a list of paths is given, a list of array lengths is returned.
short_description: Returns the number of elements in a JSON array, optionally at a specified path.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON array, provided as a JSON type or a string.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: An optional path expression (JSONPath or JSON Pointer) to locate the array within the JSON document. If a list of paths is provided, a list of corresponding array lengths is returned.

name: json_contains
description: Checks if a JSON value (`json_needle`) is contained within another JSON value (`json_haystack`). The `json_haystack` can be a JSON object or array. The `json_needle` can be a JSON value, a numeric value, or a string. Note that string needles must be wrapped in double quotes.
short_description: Checks if a JSON value is contained within another JSON value.

parameters.name: json_haystack
parameters.description: The JSON object or array to search within.

parameters.name: json_needle
parameters.description: The JSON value, numeric value, or string to search for. String values must be wrapped in double quotes.

name: json_deserialize_sql
description: Deserializes one or many JSON serialized statements back to an equivalent SQL string. Note that this function does not preserve syntactic sugar, so a statement round-tripped through `json_deserialize_sql(json_serialize_sql(...))` may not be identical to the original statement, but will be semantically equivalent and produce the same output.
short_description: Deserializes a JSON representation of a SQL statement back into a SQL string.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object representing the serialized SQL statement(s).

name: json_each
description: Traverses the top-level of a JSON object or array and returns one row for each element. If the input JSON is not an object or array, a single row for the element itself is returned. An optional path can be provided to specify a different starting point for the traversal within the JSON structure.
short_description: Traverses a JSON object or array, returning one row for each top-level element.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object or array to traverse, provided as a JSON type or a string.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: An optional JSONPath or JSON Pointer string specifying the element to start traversal from.

name: json_execute_serialized_sql
description: Executes a JSON serialized SQL statement. This PRAGMA version executes the statement in the same query context, allowing it to see transaction-local changes. A key limitation is that the serialized JSON must be provided as a constant string, unlike its table function counterpart.
short_description: Executes a JSON serialized SQL statement in the current query context.

parameters.name: json_statement
parameters.description: A constant string containing the JSON representation of the SQL statement to execute.

name: json_execute_serialized_sql
description: Executes a JSON serialized SQL statement and returns the resulting rows. Only one statement at a time is supported. When run inside a transaction, the serialized statements will not see any transaction-local changes as they are executed in a separate query context. The PRAGMA version of this function can be used to execute statements in the same query context.
short_description: Executes a JSON serialized SQL statement and returns the resulting rows.

parameters.name: json_serialized_sql
parameters.description: A JSON string representing the serialized SQL statement to execute.

name: json_exists
description: Checks if a specified JSONPath or JSON Pointer path exists within a JSON object or string. It returns true if the path is found and false otherwise. This function can check for a single path or a list of paths.
short_description: Returns true if the supplied path exists in the JSON, and false otherwise.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE data(j JSON); INSERT INTO data VALUES ('{ "family": "anatidae", "species": [ "duck", "goose" ] }'); SELECT json_exists(j, '$.family'), json_exists(j, '$.nonexistent_key');

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object or a string representation of a JSON object to check.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: The JSONPath or JSON Pointer expression specifying the path to check for existence.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object or a string representation of a JSON object to check.

parameters.name: paths
parameters.description: A list of JSONPath or JSON Pointer expressions to check for existence.

name: json_extract
description: Extracts a JSON value from a JSON object at a specified path. This function supports both JSONPath and JSON Pointer notations for locating elements within the JSON structure. It can extract a single value when given a single path, or multiple values when provided with a list of paths. The `->` operator is a convenient alias for this function.
short_description: Extracts a JSON value from a JSON object at a specified path.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object, can be a JSON type or a string.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: The path to the value to extract, using JSONPath or JSON Pointer notation. An integer can be used to index into an array.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object, can be a JSON type or a string.

parameters.name: paths
parameters.description: A list of paths to the values to extract.

name: json_extract_string
description: Extracts a value from a JSON object at a specified path and returns it as a string (VARCHAR). This is similar to `json_extract`, but it returns the unquoted text value. The path can be specified using JSONPath or JSONPointer notation. If a list of paths is provided, the function returns a list of strings.
short_description: Extracts a string value from a JSON object at a specified path.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object, or a string that can be parsed as JSON.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: The path to the value to be extracted. Can be a single path string (JSONPath or JSONPointer), an integer index for an array, or a list of path strings. If a list of paths is provided, a list of strings is returned.

name: json_group_array
description: An aggregate function that takes all input values from a group and returns them as a single JSON array. Each value is converted to its JSON representation.
short_description: Aggregates values from a column into a single JSON array

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value to aggregate into the JSON array.

name: json_group_object
description: An aggregate function that returns a JSON object composed of all key-value pairs provided in the aggregation. The key is converted to a string, and the value is converted to its corresponding JSON representation.
short_description: Aggregates key-value pairs from multiple rows into a single JSON object

parameters.name: key
parameters.description: The key for the JSON object.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value for the JSON object.

name: json_group_structure
description: An aggregate function that computes the combined structure of all JSON values in a group. It first aggregates all JSON inputs into a JSON array and then determines the structure of that array, effectively merging the structures of individual JSON objects.
short_description: Returns the combined structure of all JSON values in an aggregation.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE example2 (j JSON); INSERT INTO example2 VALUES ('{"family": "anatidae", "species": ["duck", "goose"], "coolness": 42.42}'), ('{"family": "canidae", "species": ["labrador", "bulldog"], "hair": true}'); SELECT json_group_structure(j) FROM example2;

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON value to be aggregated.

name: json_keys
description: Returns the keys of a JSON object as a list of strings. If a path is provided, it returns the keys of the JSON object at that specific path. The path can be specified using either JSON Pointer or JSONPath notation. If a list of paths is provided, the function returns a list of key lists corresponding to each path.
short_description: Returns the keys of a JSON object as a list of strings.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object or its string representation.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: An optional path to a specific object within the JSON, using JSONPath or JSON Pointer notation.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object or its string representation.

parameters.name: paths
parameters.description: A list of paths to specific objects within the JSON.

name: json_merge_patch
description: Merges two JSON documents together. The properties from the second JSON document are applied to the first, effectively updating or adding key-value pairs.
short_description: Merges two JSON documents together.

parameters.name: json1
parameters.description: The first JSON document.

parameters.name: json2
parameters.description: The second JSON document to merge into the first.

name: json_object
description: Creates a JSON object from a list of key-value pairs provided as arguments. The function requires an even number of arguments, where each odd-positioned argument is treated as a key and the following even-positioned argument is its value. Keys are typically strings.
short_description: Creates a JSON object from a list of key-value pairs.

parameters.name: key_value_pairs
parameters.description: A variadic list of key-value pairs. Must be an even number of arguments, where keys are followed by their corresponding values.

name: json_serialize_sql
description: Serializes a set of semicolon-separated SQL select statements into an equivalent list of JSON serialized statements. The function provides several optional boolean parameters to control the serialization output, such as skipping empty or null values, or formatting the JSON.
short_description: Serializes a set of SQL select statements into a JSON representation.
usage_example: SELECT json_serialize_sql('SELECT 1 + 2; SELECT a + b FROM tbl1', skip_default := true, skip_empty := true, skip_null := true);

parameters.name: sql_string
parameters.description: A set of semicolon-separated select statements to be serialized.

parameters.name: skip_empty
parameters.description: If true, skips empty values in the serialized output.

parameters.name: skip_null
parameters.description: If true, skips null values in the serialized output.

parameters.name: skip_default
parameters.description: If true, skips default values in the serialized abstract syntax tree (AST).

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: If true, formats the JSON output for readability.

name: json_structure
description: Analyzes a JSON object or string and returns a new JSON object that describes its structure. For each key, it shows the inferred data type. For arrays, it shows the type of the elements within the array. If the structure is inconsistent (e.g., an array contains elements of incompatible types), it defaults to 'JSON' for that part of the structure.
short_description: Returns the structure of a JSON object, showing keys and inferred data types.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object or string whose structure is to be determined.

name: json_transform_strict
description: Transforms a JSON object into nested types (LISTs and STRUCTs) according to a specified structure definition. This function is strict and will throw an error if a value cannot be cast to the specified type in the structure. The structure argument is a JSON string that defines the desired output structure and types, similar to the format returned by the `json_structure` function. It is possible to extract a subset of the keys present in the JSON; missing keys in the source JSON will result in NULL values in the output.
short_description: Transforms a JSON object into nested types based on a specified structure, throwing an error if type casting fails.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object or string to be transformed.

parameters.name: structure
parameters.description: A JSON string that specifies the desired output structure and data types.

name: json_tree
description: Traverses a JSON object or string in a depth-first fashion, returning one row for each element in the structure. An optional path can be provided to start the traversal from a specific element within the JSON instead of the root.
short_description: Traverses a JSON object in a depth-first manner, returning one row for each element.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE example(j JSON); INSERT INTO example VALUES ('{"family": "anatidae", "species": ["duck", "goose"]}'); SELECT * FROM example, json_tree(j);

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object or string to traverse.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: An optional JSONPath or JSON Pointer string to specify the starting point for traversal.

name: json_type
description: Returns the type of the supplied JSON value. The possible return types are 'ARRAY', 'BIGINT', 'BOOLEAN', 'DOUBLE', 'OBJECT', 'UBIGINT', 'VARCHAR', and 'NULL'. An optional path can be provided to get the type of a specific element within the JSON. If a list of paths is provided, the function returns a list of corresponding types.
short_description: Returns the type of a JSON value (e.g., 'OBJECT', 'ARRAY', 'VARCHAR').

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object or a string representation of it.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: An optional JSONPath or JSON Pointer string to specify the element within the JSON to check.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object or a string representation of it.

parameters.name: paths
parameters.description: A list of JSONPath or JSON Pointer strings to specify the elements within the JSON to check.

name: json_valid
description: Checks if the input string or JSON object is a valid JSON document. It returns true if the input is valid JSON and false otherwise. Unlike other JSON functions, this function does not produce an error when invalid JSON is supplied.
short_description: Checks if the input string or JSON object is valid JSON.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON string or JSON object to validate.

name: json_value
description: Extracts a scalar value from a JSON object at a specified path and returns it as a VARCHAR. The path can be specified using either JSONPath or JSON Pointer notation. If the value at the specified path is not a scalar (e.g., it's an object or an array), the function returns NULL. This function can also extract multiple scalar values by providing a list of paths, in which case it returns a list of VARCHARs.
short_description: Extracts a scalar JSON value from a given path, returning NULL if the value is not a scalar.
usage_example: SELECT json_value('{"a": 1, "b": [2, 3]}', '$.a') AS scalar_val, json_value('{"a": 1, "b": [2, 3]}', '$.b') AS non_scalar_val;

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object, or a string representation of it.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: The path to the value to extract, using JSONPath or JSON Pointer notation.

parameters.name: json
parameters.description: The JSON object, or a string representation of it.

parameters.name: paths
parameters.description: A list of paths to the values to extract.

name: julian
description: Extracts the Julian Day number from a date or timestamp. The Julian Day is the continuous count of days since the beginning of the Julian period.
short_description: Extracts the Julian Day number from a date or timestamp.

parameters.name: temporal
parameters.description: The date or timestamp from which to extract the Julian Day number.

name: kurtosis
description: Computes the excess kurtosis, which is a measure of the 'tailedness' of the probability distribution of a real-valued random variable. This function uses Fisher's definition and includes a bias correction based on the sample size. A normal distribution has a kurtosis of 0. A positive value indicates a 'heavy-tailed' distribution, while a negative value indicates a 'light-tailed' distribution.
short_description: Computes the excess kurtosis (Fisher's definition) with bias correction for a set of values.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The input values for which to compute the kurtosis.

name: kurtosis_pop
description: Calculates the population excess kurtosis, a measure of the 'tailedness' of a probability distribution. This version of the function does not apply bias correction. Excess kurtosis compares the distribution's kurtosis to that of a normal distribution; positive values indicate heavier tails (more outliers), while negative values indicate lighter tails.
short_description: Calculates the population excess kurtosis (a measure of distribution tailedness) without bias correction.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE series(val DOUBLE); INSERT INTO series VALUES (1), (2), (3), (4), (5), (10); SELECT kurtosis_pop(val) FROM series;

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The input column of numeric values.

name: lag
description: Returns the value for an expression evaluated at the row that is a specified number of rows before the current row within its partition. If no such row exists, it returns the specified default value, or NULL if no default is provided. The offset is the number of rows to look back, defaulting to 1. The IGNORE NULLS clause can be used to skip null values in the expression when counting rows.
short_description: Returns the value of an expression from a preceding row in the partition.
usage_example: SELECT amount - lag(amount) OVER (ORDER BY time) FROM sales;

parameters.name: expr
parameters.description: The expression to be evaluated at the preceding row.

parameters.name: offset
parameters.description: The number of rows before the current row from which to obtain the value.

parameters.name: default
parameters.description: The value to return if the offset is outside the partition. Must be the same type as `expr`.

parameters.name: ORDER BY ordering
parameters.description: If specified, the lagged row number is computed within the frame using this ordering instead of the frame ordering.

parameters.name: IGNORE NULLS
parameters.description: Specifies whether to skip null values when counting rows.

name: last
description: Returns the last value within a group, based on the order of rows. For a deterministic result, it is crucial to use an ORDER BY clause within the function call. Unlike most aggregate functions, `last` considers NULL values in its evaluation.
short_description: Returns the last value within an ordered group, including NULLs.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE sales (product TEXT, sale_date DATE, amount INT); INSERT INTO sales VALUES ('A', '2023-01-01', 10), ('A', '2023-01-05', 20), ('B', '2023-01-02', 30); SELECT product, last(amount ORDER BY sale_date) AS last_sale_amount FROM sales GROUP BY product;

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The expression or column from which to get the last value.

name: last_day
description: Calculates the last day of the month for a given date or timestamp. The function returns a DATE value representing the last day of the month of the input.
short_description: Returns the last day of the month for a given date or timestamp.

parameters.name: temporal
parameters.description: The date or timestamp for which to find the last day of the month.

name: last_value
description: Returns `expr` evaluated at the row that is the last row (among rows with a non-null value of `expr` if `IGNORE NULLS` is set) of the window frame. If an `ORDER BY` clause is specified, the last row is determined within the frame using the provided ordering instead of the frame ordering.
short_description: Returns the value of an expression from the last row of the window frame.
usage_example: SELECT amount, last_value(amount) OVER (ORDER BY time) AS last_sale FROM sales;

parameters.name: expr
parameters.description: The expression to evaluate at the last row of the window frame.

parameters.name: ORDER BY ordering
parameters.description: Specifies a different ordering to determine the last row within the frame.

parameters.name: IGNORE NULLS
parameters.description: If specified, the function returns the value from the last row where `expr` is not NULL. The default is `RESPECT NULLS`.

name: lcm
description: Computes the least common multiple of two numbers.
short_description: Computes the least common multiple of two numbers.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The first integer value.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The second integer value.

name: lead
description: Returns the value of an expression evaluated at the row that is a specified number of rows after the current row within its window partition. If no such row exists, it returns a default value. This function is useful for comparing values across consecutive rows.
short_description: Returns `expr` evaluated at the row that is `offset` rows after the current row within the window frame.
usage_example: SELECT time, amount, lead(amount, 1, 0) OVER (ORDER BY time) AS next_amount FROM sales;

parameters.name: expr
parameters.description: The expression to be evaluated at the row that is `offset` rows after the current row.

parameters.name: offset
parameters.description: The number of rows after the current row from which to obtain the value.

parameters.name: default
parameters.description: The value to be returned if the `offset` goes beyond the scope of the window frame. The type must be the same as `expr`.

parameters.name: ORDER BY ordering
parameters.description: If an `ORDER BY` clause is specified, the leading row number is computed within the frame using the provided ordering instead of the frame ordering.

parameters.name: IGNORE NULLS
parameters.description: When specified, `lead` skips null values in the expression when determining the row. The default is `RESPECT NULLS`.

name: least
description: Returns the smallest value from a list of arguments. This function can take any number of arguments (at least two). For strings, the comparison is done lexicographically, where uppercase characters are considered smaller than lowercase characters. Collations are not supported. For temporal types, it returns the earliest value.
short_description: Returns the smallest value from a list of arguments.

parameters.name: arg1
parameters.description: The first value to compare.
One or more subsequent values to compare. All arguments must be of the same data type.

name: left
description: Extracts a specified number of characters from the beginning (left side) of a string.
short_description: Extracts the specified number of characters from the beginning of a string.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string from which to extract characters.

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: The number of characters to extract.

name: left_grapheme
description: Extracts the specified number of grapheme clusters from the beginning (left side) of a string. A grapheme cluster is a user-perceived character, which may be composed of multiple Unicode code points (e.g., an emoji with a skin tone modifier).
short_description: Extracts the left-most grapheme clusters from a string.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string from which to extract the grapheme clusters.

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: The number of grapheme clusters to extract.

name: length
description: Calculates the length of the input, which varies depending on the data type. For strings, it returns the number of characters. For bitstrings, it returns the number of bits (as an alias for `bit_length`). For lists, it returns the number of elements.
short_description: Returns the number of characters in a string, bits in a bitstring, or elements in a list.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: The input string, bitstring, or list for which to determine the length.

name: length_grapheme
description: Calculates the number of grapheme clusters in a given string. A grapheme cluster is a user-perceived character, which may be composed of multiple Unicode code points (e.g., an emoji with a skin tone modifier). This is different from the `length` function, which counts characters (code points).
short_description: Returns the number of grapheme clusters in a string.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The input string.

name: levenshtein
description: Calculates the minimum number of single-character edits (insertions, deletions or substitutions) required to change one string to the other. Characters of different cases (e.g., 'a' and 'A') are considered different.
short_description: Calculates the Levenshtein distance (edit distance) between two strings.

parameters.name: s1
parameters.description: The first string.

parameters.name: s2
parameters.description: The second string.

name: lgamma
description: Computes the log of the gamma function.
short_description: Computes the natural logarithm of the gamma function.

name: like_escape
description: Returns true if the input string matches the specified LIKE pattern using case-sensitive matching. An escape character can be provided to search for literal wildcard characters within the string.
short_description: Checks if a string matches a LIKE pattern with a custom escape character, using case-sensitive matching.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to match.

parameters.name: like_specifier
parameters.description: The LIKE pattern to match against the string. It can include wildcard characters '%' (matches any sequence of zero or more characters) and '_' (matches any single character).

parameters.name: escape_character
parameters.description: The character used to escape wildcard characters in the pattern, allowing them to be treated as literal characters.

name: list_aggregate
description: Executes a specified aggregate function on the elements of a list. The name of the aggregate function is provided as a string. Additional arguments can be passed directly to the aggregate function itself, such as a delimiter for 'string_agg'.
short_description: Executes a specified aggregate function on the elements of a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The input list on which to perform the aggregation.

parameters.name: function_name
parameters.description: The name of the aggregate function to execute, provided as a string (e.g., 'min', 'sum').
Optional, additional arguments that are passed directly to the specified aggregate function. Multiple arguments can be provided.

name: list_any_value
description: Applies the `any_value` aggregate function to the elements of a list. This function returns an arbitrary non-NULL value from the input list. If the list is empty or contains only NULL values, it returns NULL.
short_description: Returns an arbitrary non-NULL value from a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The input list.

name: list_append
description: Appends a given element to the end of a list, returning a new list with the element added.
short_description: Appends an element to the end of a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list to append the element to.

parameters.name: element
parameters.description: The element to append to the list.

name: list_approx_count_distinct
description: Applies the `approx_count_distinct` aggregate function to the elements of a list. This function calculates an approximation of the number of distinct elements within the given list.
short_description: Calculates the approximate number of distinct elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of values.

name: list_avg
description: Applies the `avg` aggregate function to the elements of a list, calculating their average value. This is a convenient shorthand for `list_aggregate(list, 'avg')`.
short_description: Calculates the average of the numeric elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of numbers to average.

name: list_bit_and
description: Applies the `bit_and` aggregate function to the elements of a list, computing the bitwise AND of all non-null elements.
short_description: Computes the bitwise AND of all elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of integer or bitstring values on which to perform the bitwise AND operation.

name: list_bit_or
description: Applies the `bit_or` aggregate function to the elements of a list, computing the bitwise OR of all non-null elements.
short_description: Computes the bitwise OR of all elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of integers on which to perform the bitwise OR operation.

name: list_bit_xor
description: Applies the `bit_xor` aggregate function to the elements of a list. This is a shorthand for `list_aggregate(list, 'bit_xor')`.
short_description: Computes the bitwise XOR of all elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of values to perform the bitwise XOR operation on.

name: list_bool_and
description: Applies the `bool_and` aggregate function to the elements of a list. This function returns true if all elements in the list are true, and false if at least one element is false.
short_description: Performs a logical AND on all boolean elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of boolean values.

name: list_bool_or
description: Applies the `bool_or` aggregate function to the input list. This function returns `true` if any element in the list is `true`, and `false` otherwise. `NULL` values are ignored.
short_description: Computes the logical OR of all boolean values in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of boolean values.

name: list_concat
description: Concatenates lists. `NULL` inputs are skipped. See also operator `||`.
short_description: Concatenates multiple lists into a single list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The first list to concatenate.
Additional lists to concatenate.

name: list_contains
description: Returns true if the list contains the element.
short_description: Checks if a list contains a specific element.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list to search within.

parameters.name: element
parameters.description: The element to search for in the list.

name: list_cosine_distance
description: Computes the cosine distance between two same-sized lists of numbers. Cosine distance is a measure of dissimilarity between two non-zero vectors, calculated as 1 minus the cosine similarity.
short_description: Computes the cosine distance between two same-sized lists of numbers.

parameters.name: list1
parameters.description: The first list of numbers.

parameters.name: list2
parameters.description: The second list of numbers. Must be the same size as the first list.

name: list_cosine_similarity
description: Computes the cosine similarity between two same-sized lists of numbers. Cosine similarity measures the cosine of the angle between two non-zero vectors, with a value of 1 indicating identical direction, 0 indicating orthogonality, and -1 indicating opposite directions.
short_description: Computes the cosine similarity between two same-sized lists.

parameters.name: list1
parameters.description: The first list of numeric values.

parameters.name: list2
parameters.description: The second list of numeric values, which must be the same size as the first list.

name: list_count
description: Applies the `count` aggregate function to the elements of a list, returning the total number of elements.
short_description: Counts the number of elements in a list

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list to count the elements of.

name: list_distance
description: Calculates the Euclidean distance between two points, where the coordinates are provided as two input lists of equal length.
short_description: Calculates the Euclidean distance between two lists of coordinates.

parameters.name: list1
parameters.description: The first list of coordinates.

parameters.name: list2
parameters.description: The second list of coordinates, which must be of the same length as the first list.

name: list_distinct
description: Removes all duplicates and NULL values from a list. This function does not preserve the original order of the elements.
short_description: Removes duplicate and NULL values from a list

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list from which to remove duplicate and NULL values.

name: list_dot_product
description: This is an alias for the `list_inner_product` function. It computes the inner product (also known as the dot product) between two lists of numbers. The lists must have the same size.
short_description: Computes the inner product (dot product) of two lists.

parameters.name: list1
parameters.description: The first list of numbers.

parameters.name: list2
parameters.description: The second list of numbers.

name: list_entropy
description: Applies the aggregate function `entropy` to the elements of a list, calculating the entropy of the values within it.
short_description: Calculates the entropy of the elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of values.

name: list_extract
description: Extracts the element at a specified 1-based position from a list, or a character from a string. This function can also be invoked using the `list[index]` bracket syntax. Negative indices are supported and count from the end of the list.
short_description: Extracts an element from a list or a character from a string at a specified 1-based index.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list or string to extract from.

parameters.name: index
parameters.description: The 1-based index of the element to extract. Negative indices are supported and count from the end of the list.

name: list_first
description: Applies the 'first' aggregate function to the elements of a list, which returns the first element from the input list. If the list is empty, it returns NULL.
short_description: Returns the first element of a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The input list.

name: list_has_all
description: Checks if the first list contains all elements present in the second list. This function ignores NULL values during the comparison.
short_description: Checks if one list contains all elements of another list

parameters.name: list1
parameters.description: The list to be checked.

parameters.name: list2
parameters.description: The list of elements to check for in the first list.

name: list_histogram
description: Applies the `histogram` aggregate function to the elements of a list, creating a frequency map. The resulting map contains each unique element from the list as a key and its frequency (count) as the value.
short_description: Computes a frequency map (histogram) of the elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The input list.

name: list_intersect
description: Returns a list of all the elements that exist in both input lists, without duplicates.
short_description: Returns the intersection of two lists, without duplicates.

parameters.name: list1
parameters.description: The first list.

parameters.name: list2
parameters.description: The second list.

name: list_kurtosis
description: Applies the `kurtosis` aggregate function to the elements of a list. Kurtosis is a measure of the 'tailedness' of the probability distribution of a real-valued random variable. A `NULL` is returned if the list contains fewer than four non-`NULL` elements.
short_description: Computes the kurtosis of the elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of numbers for which to compute the kurtosis.

name: list_kurtosis_pop
description: Applies the `kurtosis_pop` aggregate function to the elements of a list. This calculates the population kurtosis, which is a measure of the 'tailedness' of the probability distribution of the values within the list.
short_description: Calculates the population kurtosis of the elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of numeric values.

name: list_last
description: Applies the aggregate function `last` to the list, which returns the last element of the list.
short_description: Returns the last element of a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list from which to extract the last element.

name: list_mad
description: Applies the `mad` (median absolute deviation) aggregate function to the elements of a list. This calculates the median of the absolute deviations from the data's median.
short_description: Calculates the median absolute deviation (MAD) of the elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of numbers for which to calculate the median absolute deviation.

name: list_max
description: Finds the maximum value within a list by applying the `max` aggregate function to its elements.
short_description: Finds the maximum value in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list from which to find the maximum value.

name: list_median
description: Calculates the median value from the elements of a list by applying the `median` aggregate function.
short_description: Calculates the median value of the elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of values.

name: list_min
description: Applies the `min` aggregate function to the elements of a list, returning the minimum value. This is a convenient shorthand for `list_aggregate(list, 'min')`.
short_description: Finds the minimum value in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of values.

name: list_mode
description: Applies the `mode` aggregate function to the elements of a list, returning the most frequently occurring value. If there is a tie for the most frequent value, one of the tied values is chosen arbitrarily.
short_description: Finds the most frequently occurring value (mode) in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of values.

name: list_negative_inner_product
description: Computes the negative inner product between two same-sized lists. The inner product, also known as the dot product, is the sum of the products of the corresponding entries of the two sequences of numbers. This function returns the negated result of that calculation.
short_description: Computes the negative inner product between two same-sized lists.

parameters.name: list1
parameters.description: The first list of numbers.

parameters.name: list2
parameters.description: The second list of numbers, which must be the same size as the first list.

name: list_position
description: Returns the 1-based index of the first occurrence of an element within a list. If the element is not found in the list, it returns NULL.
short_description: Returns the 1-based index of an element in a list, or NULL if not found.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list to search within.

parameters.name: element
parameters.description: The element to find the position of.

name: list_prepend
description: Prepends a given element to the beginning of a list, returning a new list with the element at the first position.
short_description: Prepends an element to the beginning of a list.

parameters.name: element
parameters.description: The element to prepend to the list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list to which the element will be prepended.

name: list_product
description: Calculates the product of all elements in a list by applying the `product` aggregate function.
short_description: Calculates the product of all elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of numbers for which to calculate the product.

name: list_reduce
description: Reduces all elements of the input list into a single scalar value by executing the lambda function on a running result and the next list element.
