The lambda function takes two arguments: an accumulator and the current list element. An optional initial value can be provided to start the reduction.
short_description: Reduces a list to a single value by applying a lambda function cumulatively.
usage_example: SELECT list_reduce([1, 2, 3], lambda x, y : x + y);

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The input list to reduce.

parameters.name: lambda_function
parameters.description: The lambda function to apply. It takes two arguments: the accumulator and the current element.

parameters.name: initial_value
parameters.description: An optional initial value for the reduction.

name: list_resize
description: Resizes a list to a specified number of elements. If the new size is larger than the current size, the list is padded with a specified value, or NULL if no value is provided. If the new size is smaller, the list is truncated.
short_description: Resizes a list to a specified size, padding or truncating as needed

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list to resize.

parameters.name: size
parameters.description: The new size of the list.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value to pad the list with if the new size is larger. Defaults to NULL.

name: list_reverse
description: Reverses the order of the elements in the input list.
short_description: Reverses the order of elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list to be reversed.

name: list_reverse_sort
description: Sorts the elements of a list in descending (reverse) order. An optional argument can specify the ordering of NULL values, either first or last.
short_description: Sorts the elements of a list in descending (reverse) order.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list to be sorted.

parameters.name: null_order
parameters.description: Specifies how NULL values should be ordered.

name: list_select
description: Returns a new list containing elements from a value list, selected based on a corresponding list of indices.
short_description: Returns a new list containing elements from a value list at specified indices.

parameters.name: value_list
parameters.description: The list from which to select elements.

parameters.name: index_list
parameters.description: A list of 1-based indices indicating which elements to select from the value list.

name: list_sem
description: Applies the `sem` (standard error of the mean) aggregate function to the elements of a list. This calculates the standard error of the mean for all non-NULL values within the input list.
short_description: Calculates the standard error of the mean for the elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of numbers for which to calculate the standard error of the mean.

name: list_skewness
description: Applies the aggregate function `skewness` to the elements of a list. Skewness is a measure of the asymmetry of the probability distribution of a real-valued random variable about its mean.
short_description: Computes the skewness of the elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of numbers for which to calculate the skewness.

name: list_slice
description: Extracts a sublist from a list or a substring from a string based on a starting and ending index. This function is also available through bracket notation (e.g., `my_list[2:4]`). It uses 1-based indexing. An optional step parameter can be provided to skip elements. Negative values for 'begin' and 'end' are accepted and are counted from the end of the list/string. If 'begin' is greater than 'end' (after resolving negative indices), an empty list/string is returned, unless a negative step is provided, which reverses the slice.
short_description: Extracts a sublist or substring using start, end, and optional step indices.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list or string to slice.

parameters.name: begin
parameters.description: The 1-based starting index of the slice. Negative values count from the end.

parameters.name: end
parameters.description: The 1-based ending index of the slice (inclusive). Negative values count from the end.

parameters.name: step
parameters.description: The step size between elements. A negative step reverses the slice. Must be non-zero.

name: list_stddev_pop
description: Calculates the population standard deviation for the elements in a list. This is a convenience function that applies the `stddev_pop` aggregate function to the list's elements.
short_description: Calculates the population standard deviation for all elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of numbers for which to calculate the population standard deviation.

name: list_stddev_samp
description: Calculates the sample standard deviation for the elements in a list. This is equivalent to applying the `stddev_samp` aggregate function to the list's elements.
short_description: Calculates the sample standard deviation of the elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of numbers.

name: list_string_agg
description: Applies the `string_agg` aggregate function to the elements of a list. This concatenates all non-null elements of the list into a single string, separated by a comma.
short_description: Concatenates the elements of a list into a single string using a comma delimiter.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of values to concatenate.

name: list_sum
description: Calculates the sum of the elements in a list by applying the `sum` aggregate function. If the input list is empty, the function returns `NULL`.
short_description: Calculates the sum of all elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of numbers to sum.

name: list_transform
description: Returns a list that is the result of applying a lambda function to each element of the input list. The lambda function can take one parameter (the element) or two parameters (the element and its 1-based index). The return type of the new list is defined by the return type of the lambda function.
short_description: Applies a function to each element in a list, returning a new list of the results.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The input list to be transformed.

parameters.name: lambda_function
parameters.description: The lambda function to apply to each element. It can take one parameter (the element) or two parameters (the element and its 1-based index).

name: list_unique
description: Counts the number of unique non-NULL elements in a list.
short_description: Counts the number of unique non-NULL elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list from which to count the unique elements.

name: list_value
description: Creates a list containing the provided argument values. The function accepts a variable number of arguments, which are combined into a single list. If arguments of different types are provided, they will be cast to a common supertype.
short_description: Creates a list from a variable number of arguments.

parameters.description: A variable number of arguments to be included in the list.

name: list_var_pop
description: Applies the `var_pop` aggregate function to the elements of a list, calculating the population variance.
short_description: Calculates the population variance of the elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of values.

name: list_var_samp
description: Applies the `var_samp` aggregate function to the elements of a list, calculating the sample variance. This is a simplified wrapper around the `list_aggregate` function.
short_description: Computes the sample variance of the elements in a list.

parameters.name: list
parameters.description: The list of values to calculate the sample variance for.

name: list_zip
description: Zips multiple lists into a new list of structs. The length of the resulting list will be that of the longest input list. Its elements are structs containing corresponding elements from each input list, with missing elements replaced by `NULL`. If the optional `truncate` argument is set to true, the resulting list is truncated to the length of the shortest input list.
short_description: Zips multiple lists into a single list of structs.

parameters.name: lists
parameters.description: A variable number of list arguments to be zipped together.

parameters.name: truncate
parameters.description: If set to true, the resulting list is truncated to the length of the shortest input list.

name: ln
description: Computes the natural logarithm (base e) of a given number.
short_description: Computes the natural logarithm of a number

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The number for which to compute the natural logarithm.

name: log2
description: Computes the base-2 logarithm of a given number.
short_description: Computes the base-2 logarithm of a number.

name: log
description: Computes the logarithm of a number. When called with a single argument, it computes the base-10 logarithm. When called with two arguments, it computes the logarithm of the second argument to the base of the first argument.
short_description: Computes the logarithm of a number (base-10 default, or specified base).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The number for which to compute the base-10 logarithm.

parameters.name: base
parameters.description: The base of the logarithm.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The number for which to compute the logarithm.

name: lower
description: Converts a string to lower case.
short_description: Converts a string to lower case.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to convert to lower case.

name: lpad
description: Pads a string with a specified character on the left until it reaches a desired length. If the original string is longer than the target length, it will be truncated on the right.
short_description: Pads a string on the left to a specified length with a given character

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to pad.

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: The target length of the resulting string.

parameters.name: character
parameters.description: The character to use for padding.

name: ltrim
description: Removes any occurrences of the specified characters from the left side of the input string. If the characters to remove are not specified, it defaults to removing leading spaces.
short_description: Removes specified characters or leading whitespace from the left side of a string.
usage_example: SELECT ltrim('    test  ') AS trim_spaces, ltrim('>>>>test<<', '><') AS trim_chars;

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to trim.

parameters.name: characters
parameters.description: The set of characters to remove from the left side of the string. If not provided, whitespace is removed.

name: mad
description: Calculates the median absolute deviation (MAD) for a set of values. MAD is a robust measure of statistical dispersion, defined as the median of the absolute deviations from the data's median: `median(abs(x - median(x)))`. NULL values are ignored. When the input is a temporal type (like DATE or TIMESTAMP), the function returns a positive INTERVAL.
short_description: Calculates the median absolute deviation (MAD), a robust measure of variability.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE stats(val INT); INSERT INTO stats VALUES (1), (1), (2), (2), (4), (6), (9); SELECT mad(val) FROM stats;

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The input column of values.

name: make_date
description: Creates a date value from separate year, month, and day components. The components can be provided as three separate integer arguments or as a single struct containing 'year', 'month', and 'day' fields.
short_description: Creates a date from year, month, and day components.

parameters.name: year
parameters.description: The year component of the date.

parameters.name: month
parameters.description: The month component of the date.

parameters.name: day
parameters.description: The day component of the date.

parameters.name: date_struct
parameters.description: A struct containing the year, month, and day components.

name: make_time
description: Creates a TIME value from its constituent parts: hour, minute, and second. The seconds component can be a fractional value.
short_description: Creates a TIME value from hour, minute, and second components.

parameters.name: hour
parameters.description: The hour component of the time.

parameters.name: minute
parameters.description: The minute component of the time.

parameters.name: seconds
parameters.description: The seconds component of the time, which can include fractional parts.

name: make_timestamp
description: Creates a timestamp value. It can either be constructed from individual date and time components (year, month, day, hour, minute, second) or from a single integer value representing the number of microseconds since the epoch (1970-01-01).
short_description: Creates a timestamp from its component parts or from microseconds since the epoch.

parameters.name: year
parameters.description: The year component of the timestamp.

parameters.name: month
parameters.description: The month component of the timestamp.

parameters.name: day
parameters.description: The day component of the timestamp.

parameters.name: hour
parameters.description: The hour component of the timestamp.

parameters.name: minute
parameters.description: The minute component of the timestamp.

parameters.name: second
parameters.description: The second component of the timestamp, which can include fractional seconds.

parameters.name: microseconds
parameters.description: The total number of microseconds since the epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00+00).

name: make_timestamp_ms
description: Converts a BIGINT representing the number of milliseconds since the epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00+00) into a TIMESTAMP.
short_description: Converts milliseconds since the epoch to a timestamp

parameters.name: milliseconds
parameters.description: The number of milliseconds since the epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00+00).

name: make_timestamp_ns
description: Converts a BIGINT representing the number of nanoseconds since the epoch (1970-01-01 00:00:00+00) into a timestamp with nanosecond precision.
short_description: Converts nanoseconds since the epoch to a timestamp.

parameters.name: nanoseconds
parameters.description: The number of nanoseconds since the epoch.

name: make_timestamptz
description: Creates a timestamp with time zone (TIMESTAMPTZ) from its component parts. It can be constructed from year, month, day, hour, minute, and second values, with an optional time zone. If no time zone is provided, the current session's time zone is used. Alternatively, it can be created from a single integer representing the number of microseconds since the epoch.
short_description: Creates a timestamp with time zone from component parts or from microseconds since the epoch.

parameters.name: timezone
parameters.description: The time zone for the timestamp. If not provided, the current time zone is used.

parameters.name: microseconds
parameters.description: The number of microseconds (µs) since the epoch.

name: map
description: Creates a map data structure. When called with no arguments, it returns an empty map. When called with two list arguments, it creates a map from the provided keys and values. The two lists must have the same length.
short_description: Creates a map from lists of keys and values, or an empty map.
usage_example: SELECT map() AS empty_map, MAP(['key1', 'key2'], [10, 20]) AS populated_map;

parameters.name: keys
parameters.description: A list containing the keys of the map.

parameters.name: values
parameters.description: A list containing the values of the map.

name: map_concat
description: Returns a map created from merging the input maps. On key collision the value is taken from the last map with that key.
short_description: Merges multiple maps into a single map

parameters.name: maps
parameters.description: The first map to merge.
Additional maps to merge.

name: map_contains
description: Checks if a map contains a given key.
short_description: Checks if a map contains a given key.

parameters.name: map
parameters.description: The map to check.

parameters.name: key
parameters.description: The key to look for in the map. The type of the key must match the key type of the map.

name: map_contains_entry
description: Checks if a map contains a specific key-value pair. The function returns true if the map contains the given key and its associated value is the same as the provided value, and false otherwise.
short_description: Checks if a map contains a given key-value pair.

parameters.name: map
parameters.description: The input map.

parameters.name: key
parameters.description: The key of the entry to check.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value of the entry to check.

name: map_contains_value
description: Checks if a map contains a given value.
short_description: Checks if a map contains a given value.

parameters.name: map
parameters.description: The map to check.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value to search for within the map's values.

name: map_entries
description: Returns a list of structs, where each struct contains a key and a value corresponding to a key-value pair in the map.
short_description: Returns a list of key-value structs for each entry in the map.

parameters.name: map
parameters.description: The map from which to extract the entries.

name: map_extract
description: Extracts the value for a given key from a map and returns it as a list. If the key is not found in the map, it returns an empty list. The type of the provided key must match the map's key type, otherwise an error is thrown.
short_description: Extracts the value for a given key from a map, returning it as a list.

parameters.name: map
parameters.description: The map from which to extract the value.

parameters.name: key
parameters.description: The key corresponding to the value to extract. The type of the key must match the map's key type.

name: map_extract_value
description: Extracts the value associated with a specific key from a map. If the key is not found within the map, it returns NULL. An error is thrown if the provided key's data type does not match the map's key type.
short_description: Returns the value for a given key from a map, or NULL if the key is not found.

parameters.name: map
parameters.description: The map from which to extract the value.

parameters.name: key
parameters.description: The key corresponding to the value to be extracted. Its type must match the map's key type.

name: map_from_entries
description: Returns a map created from an array of key-value pair entries. The input must be an array of STRUCTs, where each STRUCT contains two fields representing the key and the value.
short_description: Creates a map from a list of key-value struct entries.

parameters.name: entries
parameters.description: An array of STRUCTs, where each STRUCT represents a key-value pair for the map.

name: map_keys
description: Returns a list containing all the keys from the input map. The order of the keys in the returned list is not guaranteed.
short_description: Return a list of all keys in the map.

parameters.name: map
parameters.description: The map from which to extract the keys.

name: map_values
description: Returns a list containing all the values from the input map.
short_description: Return a list of all values in the map.

parameters.name: map
parameters.description: The map from which to extract the values.

name: max
description: Returns the maximum value from a set of rows. It can either return a single maximum value or a list of the top 'n' values, ordered descending. This function is unaffected by the DISTINCT clause.
short_description: Returns the maximum value from a group of values, or a list of the top N values.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The input column or expression from which to find the maximum value.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The input column or expression from which to find the top values.

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: The number of top values to return.

name: md5
description: Computes the MD5 hash of the input string or blob. The result is returned as a hexadecimal string.
short_description: Computes the MD5 hash of a string or blob.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: The string or blob to be hashed.

name: md5_number
description: Computes the MD5 hash of an input string or blob, returning the result as a 128-bit unsigned integer (UHUGEINT).
short_description: Computes the MD5 hash of a string or blob, returning it as a large unsigned integer.

parameters.name: data
parameters.description: The input string or blob to be hashed.

name: md5_number_lower
description: Computes the MD5 hash of the input string and returns the lower 64 bits (8 bytes) of the hash as an unsigned big integer.
short_description: Returns the lower 64-bit segment of the MD5 hash of a string as an unsigned big integer.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The input string to be hashed.

name: md5_number_upper
description: Computes the MD5 hash of the input string and returns the upper 64-bit (8 bytes) segment of the hash as an unsigned big integer (UBIGINT).
short_description: Returns the upper 64-bit segment of the MD5 hash of a string as a UBIGINT.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The input string to be hashed.

name: md_active_server_connections
description: The md_active_server_connections table function can be used to list all server-side connections that have active transactions.
short_description: Lists all server-side connections that have active transactions.

name: md_all_databases
description: Lists all available databases, including MotherDuck databases, local DuckDB databases, and MotherDuck shares. It returns the alias, attachment status, type (e.g., DuckDB, MotherDuck, MotherDuck share), and the fully qualified name for each database.
short_description: Lists all available databases, including MotherDuck, local DuckDB, and shared databases.

name: md_create_dive
description: Creates a new Dive in your MotherDuck account. This function returns the metadata and initial version information of the created Dive.
short_description: Creates a new Dive in your MotherDuck account.

parameters.name: title
parameters.description: The title of the Dive.

parameters.name: content
parameters.description: The JSX/React component code for the Dive.

parameters.name: description
parameters.description: A brief description of the Dive.

parameters.name: api_version
parameters.description: API version for the Dive format.

name: md_current_client_connection_id
description: A scalar function that returns the unique identifier (UUID) for the current client connection. This can be used in conjunction with other MotherDuck functions to manage connections.
short_description: Returns the UUID of the current client connection.

name: md_current_client_duckdb_id
description: A scalar function that returns the unique identifier (UUID) for the current client DuckDB instance. This can be used in conjunction with other MotherDuck functions, such as `md_active_server_connections()` and `md_interrupt_server_connection()`, to manage connections originating from the same client.
short_description: Returns the UUID of the current client DuckDB instance.
usage_example:
SELECT md_interrupt_server_connection(client_connection_id)
FROM md_active_server_connections()
WHERE client_duckdb_id = md_current_client_duckdb_id()
  AND client_connection_id != md_current_client_connection_id();

name: md_delete_dive
description: Permanently deletes a Dive and all its versions. This action cannot be undone.
short_description: Permanently delete a Dive by ID.

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The unique identifier of the Dive to delete

name: md_describe_database_share
description: Retrieves detailed information about a specific database share. The creator of the share can provide the share name, while a receiver of the share should provide the share URL. The function returns a table containing various attributes of the share, such as its name, URL, source database details, access level, visibility, update mode, and creation timestamp.
short_description: Get details about a specific database share.

parameters.name: share_name_or_url
parameters.description: The name or URL of the share to describe.

name: md_get_dive
description: Retrieves a single Dive by its unique identifier, including the full React component source code for the current version.
short_description: Retrieve a Dive by ID including its full React component content.
usage_example: SELECT title, description, content FROM MD_GET_DIVE(id ='a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890'::UUID);

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The unique identifier of the Dive.

name: md_get_dive_version
description: Retrieves a specific version of a Dive, including the full React component source code. Version numbers are 0-based—the first version of a Dive is version 0.
short_description: Retrieve a specific historical version of a Dive including its content.

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The unique identifier of the Dive

parameters.name: version
parameters.description: The version number to retrieve (0-based)

name: md_interrupt_server_connection
description: Interrupts an active transaction on a server-side connection. This will interrupt and fail or rollback the active transaction, such as a long-running query, but will allow the connection to be used for future transactions and queries. The function returns `true` if the interrupt signal was successfully sent, and `false` if it could not be sent. An interrupt may fail if the connection is already closed, the transaction is committing or rolling back, or the server-side connection could not be found.
short_description: Interrupts an active transaction on a server-side connection.
usage_example:
-- Interrupt all connections where a `CREATE TABLE` query is running
SELECT md_interrupt_server_connection(client_connection_id)
FROM md_active_server_connections()
WHERE client_query ILIKE 'CREATE TABLE%';

parameters.name: client_connection_id
parameters.description: The unique identifier for the client DuckDB connection that initiated the server connection.

name: md_list_database_shares
description: Lists all database shares created by the current user. This table function provides a subset of the information available from the `MD_INFORMATION_SCHEMA.OWNED_SHARES` view. To see shares that have been shared with you by others, query the `MD_INFORMATION_SCHEMA.SHARED_WITH_ME` view instead.
short_description: Lists all database shares created by the current user.
usage_example: SELECT name, url, source_db_name FROM md_list_database_shares();

name: md_list_dive_versions
description: Lists all versions of a specific Dive with pagination support. Each time a Dive's content is updated, a new version is created. This function returns version metadata for each version, but not the actual content of the Dive.
short_description: List all versions of a specific Dive with pagination support.
usage_example: SELECT version, description, created_at FROM MD_LIST_DIVE_VERSIONS(id = 'a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890'::UUID);

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The unique identifier of the Dive

parameters.name: limit
parameters.description: Maximum number of versions to return

parameters.name: offset
parameters.description: Number of versions to skip

name: md_list_dives
description: Lists all Dives in your MotherDuck account, returning metadata for each Dive without the component content. This function supports pagination through optional limit and offset parameters and can also include Dives shared with your organization.
short_description: Lists all Dives in your MotherDuck account with pagination support.

parameters.name: limit
parameters.description: Maximum number of Dives to return.

parameters.name: offset
parameters.description: Number of Dives to skip.

parameters.name: include_org_shares
parameters.description: Include Dives shared with your organization.

name: md_update_dive_content
description: Updates the content of an existing Dive, creating a new version. Each call increments the version number. Previous versions remain accessible.
short_description: Update a Dive's React component code, creating a new version.

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The unique identifier of the Dive to update

parameters.name: content
parameters.description: The new JSX/React component code

parameters.name: description
parameters.description: Version description or commit message

parameters.name: api_version
parameters.description: API version for the Dive format.

name: md_update_dive_metadata
description: Updates the title and/or description of an existing Dive. This does not create a new version—only the metadata is changed. At least one of the title or description parameters should be provided.
short_description: Update a Dive's title or description without creating a new version.

parameters.name: id
parameters.description: The unique identifier of the Dive to update

parameters.name: title
parameters.description: New title for the Dive

parameters.name: description
parameters.description: New description for the Dive

name: median
description: Calculates the middle value of a set, ignoring NULL values. For sets with an even number of values, the function's behavior depends on the data type. For quantitative types like numbers and timestamps, it returns the average of the two middle values. For ordinal types like strings, it returns the lower of the two middle values.
short_description: Calculates the middle value of a set, averaging for even counts of numeric/temporal types.

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The set of values for which to calculate the median.

name: metadata_info
description: Returns information on the metadata store, including the block ID, total blocks, free blocks, and the free list.
short_description: Returns information on the metadata store.

name: microsecond
description: Extracts the sub-minute microsecond component from a temporal value. This is calculated as the seconds part multiplied by 1,000,000 plus the microseconds part.
short_description: Extracts the sub-minute microsecond component from a temporal value.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The temporal value from which to extract the microsecond component.

name: millennium
description: Extracts the millennium part from a temporal value. This function can be used with DATE, TIMESTAMP, TIMESTAMPTZ, and INTERVAL types.
short_description: Extracts the millennium from a date, timestamp, or interval.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The temporal value (date, timestamp, or interval) from which to extract the millennium.

name: millisecond
description: Extracts the sub-minute millisecond component from a temporal value (DATE, TIME, TIMESTAMP, or INTERVAL). This function is one of several dedicated functions for extracting specific parts from temporal types.
short_description: Extracts the sub-minute millisecond component from a temporal value.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The temporal value from which to extract the millisecond part.

name: min
description: Returns the minimum value from a set of values. It can also be used to find the 'n' smallest values, which are returned as a list. When finding the single minimum value, this function is unaffected by the DISTINCT clause. NULL values are ignored in all cases.
short_description: Returns the minimum value, or a list of the n smallest values, from a set.
usage_example:
CREATE TABLE scores(name VARCHAR, score INT); INSERT INTO scores VALUES ('Alice', 80), ('Bob', 95), ('Charlie', 75); SELECT min(score) FROM scores; -- Returns 75
SELECT min(score, 2) FROM scores; -- Returns [75, 80]

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The input column or expression.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The input column or expression.

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: The number of minimum values to return.

name: min_by
description: Finds the row(s) with the minimum value in one column and returns the corresponding value(s) from another column. The two-argument version returns a single value for the row with the absolute minimum. The three-argument version returns a list of values for the bottom 'n' rows, ordered by the specified value column. This function is affected by ordering.
short_description: Returns the value of one column for the row with the minimum value in another column.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE sales(product_name VARCHAR, price DECIMAL, grp VARCHAR); INSERT INTO sales VALUES ('A', 10, 'g1'), ('B', 20, 'g1'), ('C', 5, 'g2'), ('D', 15, 'g2'); SELECT grp, min_by(product_name, price) FROM sales GROUP BY grp;

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The column from which to return a value.

parameters.name: val
parameters.description: The column to use for finding the minimum value.

parameters.name: arg
parameters.description: The column from which to return values.

parameters.name: val
parameters.description: The column to use for finding the minimum values.

parameters.name: n
parameters.description: The number of values to return.

name: minute
description: Extracts the minute component from a temporal value such as a timestamp, time, date, or interval. This is a dedicated function for extracting the minute part, similar to using `date_part('minute', ...)`.
short_description: Extracts the minute component from a temporal value.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The temporal value from which to extract the minute.

name: mod
description: Calculates the remainder of a division, also known as the modulo operation. It is an alias for the `%` operator.
short_description: Calculates the remainder of a division (modulo).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The dividend.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The divisor.

name: mode
description: Returns the most frequent value (the mode) from a set of values. This function is affected by ordering, meaning if there are multiple values with the same highest frequency, the result may be non-deterministic unless an ORDER BY clause is used within the function call. NULL values are ignored.
short_description: Returns the most frequent value in a set of values.

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The input expression to calculate the mode from.

name: month
description: Extracts the month component from a temporal value (date, timestamp, or interval). The result is an integer representing the month of the year, from 1 to 12.
short_description: Extracts the month from a date, timestamp, or interval as an integer.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The temporal value from which to extract the month.

name: monthname
description: Returns the full English name of the month from a given date or timestamp. For example, for a date in September, it will return 'September'.
short_description: The (English) name of the month.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The date or timestamp from which to extract the month name.

name: multiply
description: This function is an alias for the multiplication operator (`*`). It takes two numeric values and returns their product. It can also be used to scale an interval by a numeric value.
short_description: Computes the product of two numbers or scales an interval.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The first numeric value (multiplicand).

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The second numeric value (multiplier).

parameters.name: interval
parameters.description: The interval to be scaled.

parameters.name: number
parameters.description: The numeric value to multiply the interval by.

name: mysql_clear_cache
description: Clears DuckDB's internal cache of schema information for attached MySQL databases. DuckDB caches schema data like table and column names to avoid repeatedly fetching it from MySQL. If the MySQL schema is modified through an external connection (e.g., adding a new column), this cache can become outdated. This function forces a refresh of the schema information.
short_description: Clears DuckDB's internal cache of schema information for attached MySQL databases.

name: mysql_execute
description: Allows running arbitrary SQL queries within an attached MySQL database. Unlike `mysql_query`, this function can execute statements that modify the database schema or content, such as `CREATE TABLE`, `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, or `DELETE`.
short_description: Executes an arbitrary SQL query, including modification statements, on an attached MySQL database.
usage_example:
ATTACH 'host=localhost database=mysql' AS mysqldb (TYPE mysql);
CALL mysql_execute('mysqldb', 'CREATE TABLE my_table (i INTEGER)');

parameters.name: attached_database
parameters.description: The name of the attached MySQL database to execute the query in.

parameters.name: query
parameters.description: The SQL query to execute in the MySQL database. This can include DDL and DML statements.

name: mysql_query
description: The mysql_query table function allows you to run arbitrary read-only SQL queries within an attached MySQL database. It takes the name of the attached database and the SQL query to execute, returning the result of the query as a table. Single-quote strings within the query must be escaped by repeating the single quote twice.
short_description: Runs a read-only SQL query on an attached MySQL database and returns the result.
usage_example:
ATTACH 'host=localhost database=mysql' AS mysqldb (TYPE mysql);
SELECT * FROM mysql_query('mysqldb', 'SELECT * FROM cars LIMIT 3');

parameters.name: attached_database
parameters.description: The name of the attached MySQL database to execute the query in.

parameters.name: query
parameters.description: The SQL query to execute.

name: netmask
description: Computes the network mask for the provided INET address's network.
short_description: Computes the network mask for an IP address's network
usage_example: SELECT cidr, netmask(cidr) FROM (VALUES ('192.168.1.5/24'::INET), ('127.0.0.1'::INET), ('2001:db8:3c4d:15::1a2f:1a2b/96'::INET)) AS tbl(cidr);

parameters.name: address
parameters.description: The INET address for which to compute the network mask.

name: network
description: Returns the network part of the address, zeroing out whatever is to the right of the netmask.
short_description: Returns the network part of an INET address
usage_example: SELECT cidr, network(cidr) AS network FROM (VALUES ('192.168.1.5/24'::INET), ('127.0.0.1'::INET), ('2001:db8:3c4d:15::1a2f:1a2b/96'::INET)) AS tbl(cidr);

parameters.name: address
parameters.description: The INET address.

name: nextafter
description: Returns the next representable floating-point value after the first argument in the direction of the second argument.
short_description: Return the next floating point value after x in the direction of y.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The starting floating-point value.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The floating-point value indicating the direction.

name: nextval
description: Advances the specified sequence and returns the new value. This function is commonly used to generate unique identifiers for columns, such as primary keys. The sequence name is provided as a string argument. The sequence must be created with CREATE SEQUENCE before it can be used.
short_description: Advances a sequence and returns its next value.

parameters.name: sequence_name
parameters.description: The name of the sequence to advance.

name: nfc_normalize
description: Converts a string to its Unicode Normalization Form C (NFC). This is useful for ensuring consistent string comparisons and ordering when text data might contain characters represented in different but equivalent ways, such as a precomposed character versus a base character with a combining mark.
short_description: Converts a string to its Unicode NFC normalized form

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The input string to be normalized.

name: not_ilike_escape
description: Performs a case-insensitive `LIKE` comparison with a custom escape character and returns the inverse of the result. It returns `false` if the string matches the pattern and `true` otherwise. The escape character is used to treat wildcard characters (`%` and `_`) as literals in the pattern.
short_description: Negated case-insensitive LIKE comparison with a custom escape character

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The input string to be checked against the pattern.

parameters.name: like_specifier
parameters.description: The case-insensitive pattern to match. It can include wildcard characters `%` (matches any sequence of characters) and `_` (matches any single character).

parameters.name: escape_character
parameters.description: A single character used to escape the meaning of wildcard characters, allowing them to be matched as literal characters.

name: not_like_escape
description: Performs a case-sensitive check to determine if a string does not match a specified LIKE pattern. It returns `true` if there is no match, and `false` if there is a match. An escape character can be provided to search for wildcard characters literally within the string.
short_description: Case-sensitive check if a string does not match a LIKE pattern with a custom escape character.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to be checked against the pattern.

parameters.name: like_specifier
parameters.description: The LIKE pattern to match against. Wildcards '%' (any sequence of characters) and '_' (any single character) can be used.

parameters.name: escape_character
parameters.description: The character used to escape wildcard characters in the pattern, allowing them to be treated as literal characters.

name: now
description: Returns the current date and time at the start of the current transaction.
short_description: Returns the current timestamp at the start of the transaction.

name: nth_value
description: Returns `expr` evaluated at the nth row of the window frame (counting from 1). If `IGNORE NULLS` is specified, it considers only rows with non-null values for `expr`. If no such row exists, it returns `NULL`. An optional `ORDER BY` clause can be provided to compute the nth row number within the frame using a different ordering than the main window frame's ordering.
short_description: Returns the value of an expression evaluated at the nth row of the window frame.
usage_example: SELECT sale_id, sale_amount, nth_value(sale_amount, 2) OVER (ORDER BY sale_date) AS second_sale_amount FROM sales;

parameters.name: expr
parameters.description: The expression to evaluate at the nth row.

parameters.name: nth
parameters.description: The row number (counting from 1) from which to retrieve the value.

parameters.name: ORDER BY ordering
parameters.description: If specified, the nth row number is computed within the frame using this provided ordering instead of the main window frame's ordering.

parameters.name: IGNORE NULLS
parameters.description: Specifies whether to ignore or respect NULL values in `expr` when determining the row number. The default is `RESPECT NULLS`.

name: ntile
description: An integer ranging from 1 to `num_buckets`, dividing the partition as equally as possible. If an `ORDER BY` clause is specified, the ntile is computed within the frame using the provided ordering instead of the frame ordering.
short_description: An integer ranging from 1 to num_buckets, dividing the partition as equally as possible.
usage_example: SELECT ntile(4) OVER (ORDER BY sales_date) FROM sales;

parameters.name: num_buckets
parameters.description: The number of buckets to divide the partition into.

parameters.name: ORDER BY ordering
parameters.description: If an ORDER BY clause is specified, the ntile is computed within the frame using the provided ordering instead of the frame ordering.

name: nullif
description: Returns NULL if the two specified arguments are equal, otherwise it returns the first argument. This is equivalent to the expression `CASE WHEN a = b THEN NULL ELSE a END`.
short_description: Returns NULL if a = b, otherwise returns a.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: The value to be returned if it is not equal to the second argument.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: The value to compare against the first argument.

name: octet_length
description: Calculates the number of bytes in a BLOB or bitstring.
short_description: Returns the number of bytes in a blob or bitstring.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: The BLOB or bitstring for which to calculate the byte length.

name: parquet_bloom_probe
description: Checks the Bloom filter of a Parquet file to determine which row groups can be excluded when filtering for a specific value in a given column. This function helps in understanding the effectiveness of Bloom filters for query pruning without actually reading the data.
short_description: Probes a Parquet file's Bloom filter to see which row groups can be skipped for a given column and value.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the Parquet file or a list of paths to Parquet files.

parameters.name: column_name
parameters.description: The name of the column whose Bloom filter should be checked.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value to check for in the Bloom filter.

name: parquet_file_metadata
description: The parquet_file_metadata function can be used to query file-level metadata from a Parquet file, such as the format version and the encryption algorithm used.
short_description: Queries file-level metadata from a Parquet file, such as format version and encryption algorithm.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the Parquet file or a list/glob of paths.

name: parquet_kv_metadata
description: Queries the custom key-value metadata from a Parquet file. This function is useful for extracting user-defined metadata that has been embedded in the file.
short_description: Queries custom key-value metadata from a Parquet file.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the Parquet file(s). Can be a single path, a list of paths, or a glob pattern.

name: parquet_metadata
description: Queries the metadata contained within a Parquet file. This function reveals various internal details of the Parquet file, such as statistics for different columns, which can be useful for understanding data distribution and potential for predicate pushdown by skipping row groups.
short_description: Queries the metadata within a Parquet file, revealing internal details like column statistics.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the Parquet file or a list of paths to Parquet files.

name: parquet_schema
description: Queries and returns the internal schema from a Parquet file's metadata. This provides a detailed view of the schema as stored within the file. For a simpler overview of column names and types, using `DESCRIBE` on a `SELECT` from the file is often easier.
short_description: Queries the internal schema from a Parquet file's metadata.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to a Parquet file, or a list of paths/globs.

name: parse_dirname
description: Extracts and returns the top-level directory name from a given file path. The path separator can be specified to handle different operating system conventions.
short_description: Returns the top-level directory name from a file path.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: The file path string.

parameters.name: separator
parameters.description: Specifies the path separator to use. Options are 'system', 'both_slash', 'forward_slash', or 'backslash'.

name: parse_dirpath
description: Returns the head of the path (the pathname until the last slash), similar to Python's `os.path.dirname`. The separator can be specified from a set of options.
short_description: Extracts the directory path from a full file path.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: The file path string to parse.

parameters.name: separator
parameters.description: Specifies the path separator to use.

name: parse_filename
description: Returns the last component of a file path, similar to Python's `os.path.basename` function. It can optionally remove the file extension and allows specifying the path separator.
short_description: Returns the last component (filename) of a path, optionally trimming the extension.

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: The input path string.

parameters.name: trim_extension
parameters.description: If set to true, the file extension will be removed from the result.

parameters.name: separator
parameters.description: Specifies the path separator to use for parsing.

name: parse_path
description: Splits a file path into a list of its components, such as directories and the filename. This function is similar to Python's `pathlib.PurePath.parts`. An optional separator can be specified to handle different path styles.
short_description: Splits a file path into its components (directories and filename).

parameters.name: path
parameters.description: The file path string to be parsed.

parameters.name: separator
parameters.description: The path separator style to use for parsing.

name: percent_rank
description: The relative rank of the current row, calculated as `(rank() - 1) / (total partition rows - 1)`. If an `ORDER BY` clause is specified, the relative rank is computed within the frame using the provided ordering instead of the frame ordering.
short_description: The relative rank of the current row: (rank() - 1) / (total partition rows - 1).
usage_example: SELECT percent_rank() OVER (ORDER BY salary) FROM employees;

parameters.name: ORDER BY ordering
parameters.description: If specified, the relative rank is computed within the frame using the provided ordering instead of the frame ordering.

name: pg_clear_cache
description: Clears DuckDB's internal cache of PostgreSQL schema information, such as table names and column definitions. This is useful when the PostgreSQL schema has been modified by an external connection, ensuring that DuckDB fetches the latest schema on the next query.
short_description: Clears DuckDB's internal cache of PostgreSQL schema information.

name: pg_size_pretty
description: Converts an integer representing a number of bytes into a human-readable string. The units are based on powers of 2 (e.g., KiB, MiB, GiB). This function is an alias for format_bytes.
short_description: Converts an integer byte count to a human-readable string (e.g., 16000 -> '15.6 KiB').

parameters.name: bytes
parameters.description: The number of bytes to format.

name: pg_timezone_names
description: Returns a table containing a list of available time zone names, their abbreviations, and UTC offsets. This function is useful for finding valid time zone identifiers to use with time zone-aware functions and settings.
short_description: Returns a table of available time zone names and their properties.
usage_example: SELECT name, abbrev FROM pg_timezone_names() ORDER BY name;

name: pg_typeof
description: Returns the lower case name of the data type of the result of the expression. This function is provided for PostgreSQL compatibility.
short_description: Returns the lower case name of the data type of an expression

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The expression for which to determine the data type.

name: pi
description: Returns the value of pi.
short_description: Returns the mathematical constant pi.

name: platform
description: Returns an identifier for the platform the current DuckDB executable has been compiled for, e.g., 'osx_arm64'. The format of this identifier matches the platform name as described in the extension loading explainer.
short_description: Returns an identifier for the platform the current DuckDB executable has been compiled for (e.g., 'osx_arm64').

name: postgres_attach
description: This function is deprecated. It attaches a PostgreSQL database, making its tables available for querying in DuckDB. It is recommended to use the `ATTACH` command with `TYPE postgres` instead, as it provides a more robust and feature-rich way to connect to PostgreSQL databases.
short_description: Deprecated. Attaches a PostgreSQL database. Use the ATTACH command instead.

parameters.name: connection_string
parameters.description: A libpq connection string or a PostgreSQL URI.

parameters.name: source_schema
parameters.description: The specific schema within the PostgreSQL database to attach. If not provided, all schemas are attached.

name: postgres_execute
description: Allows running arbitrary queries within a connected PostgreSQL database, including statements that update the schema and content of the database such as DDL and DML statements.
short_description: Executes an arbitrary SQL query in a connected PostgreSQL database.
usage_example:
ATTACH 'dbname=postgresscanner' AS postgres_db (TYPE postgres);
CALL postgres_execute('postgres_db', 'CREATE TABLE my_table (i INTEGER)');

parameters.name: attached_database
parameters.description: The name of the attached PostgreSQL database where the query will be executed.

parameters.name: query
parameters.description: The SQL query to execute. This can include statements that update the schema or content of the database.

name: postgres_query
description: The postgres_query table function allows you to run arbitrary read-only SQL queries within an attached PostgreSQL database. It takes the name of the attached database and the SQL query to execute, and returns the result of the query as a table. Note that single-quote strings within the query must be escaped by repeating the single quote twice.
short_description: Runs a read-only SQL query on an attached PostgreSQL database and returns the result.
usage_example:
ATTACH 'dbname=postgresscanner' AS postgres_db (TYPE postgres);
SELECT * FROM postgres_query('postgres_db', 'SELECT * FROM cars LIMIT 3');

parameters.name: attached_database
parameters.description: The name of the attached PostgreSQL database to execute the query in.

parameters.name: query
parameters.description: The SQL query to execute. Single-quote strings within the query must be escaped by repeating the single quote twice.

parameters.name: use_transaction
parameters.description: Specifies whether to use a transaction for the query.

name: postgres_scan
description: Scans a table from a PostgreSQL database and returns its contents as a table. This function requires a PostgreSQL connection string, a schema name, and a table name. While `postgres_scan` is useful for quick, ad-hoc queries on a single table, the recommended approach for more complex interactions or managing persistent connections is to use the `ATTACH` statement. For executing arbitrary SQL queries against the PostgreSQL database, see the `postgres_query` function.
short_description: Reads data directly from a table in a PostgreSQL database.

parameters.name: dsn
parameters.description: The PostgreSQL connection string, which can be a libpq connection string or a PostgreSQL URI.

parameters.name: schema_name
parameters.description: The name of the schema containing the table.

parameters.name: table_name
parameters.description: The name of the table to scan.

name: pow
description: Raises a base number to the power of an exponent.
short_description: Raises a number to a specified power

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The base number.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The exponent.

name: pragma_collations
description: Returns a table containing a list of all available collations in the database. This is particularly useful after loading the ICU extension to see the added language and region-specific collations.
short_description: Returns a table of all available collations.

name: pragma_database_size
description: Returns information about the file and memory size of each database. This function can be invoked as a PRAGMA statement (`PRAGMA database_size;`) or as a table function (`FROM pragma_database_size()`). The table function form is useful for filtering the results.
short_description: Returns the file and memory size for each database.

name: pragma_platform
description: Returns an identifier for the platform the current DuckDB executable has been compiled for, such as 'osx_arm64'. The format of this identifier matches the platform name as described in the extension loading documentation.
short_description: Returns an identifier for the platform the DuckDB executable was compiled for (e.g., 'osx_arm64').

name: pragma_storage_info
description: Returns detailed storage information for the specified table. This includes metadata about row groups, columns, segments, compression, and persistence, providing insights into the physical layout of the table's data.
short_description: Returns detailed storage information for a given table.

parameters.name: table_name
parameters.description: The name of the table to retrieve storage information for.

name: pragma_table_info
description: Returns detailed information about the columns of a specified table. The information includes the column ID (cid), name, data type, whether the column is marked as NOT NULL, its default value, and whether it is part of the primary key.
short_description: Returns column information for a specific table.

parameters.name: table_name
parameters.description: The name of the table to retrieve information for.

name: pragma_version
description: Returns the version of the DuckDB library. This function can be called using `PRAGMA version;` or `CALL pragma_version();`. The result includes the library name (e.g., 'duckdb') and the version string.
short_description: Shows the DuckDB library version.

name: prefix
description: Returns `true` if a string starts with a specified search string.
short_description: Returns true if a string starts with a specified prefix

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to check.

parameters.name: search_string
parameters.description: The prefix to search for at the beginning of the string.

name: print_md_token
description: Retrieves your MotherDuck authentication token. This token can be stored as an environment variable to avoid having to re-authenticate in every session.
short_description: Retrieves the MotherDuck authentication token.

name: printf
description: Formats a string using C-style printf syntax. It takes a format string as the first argument, followed by a variable number of arguments that are formatted and inserted into the resulting string.
short_description: Formats a string using C-style printf syntax

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: The format string, which can contain format specifiers (e.g., %s, %d) that correspond to the subsequent arguments.
A variable number of arguments to be formatted and inserted into the format string.

name: product
description: Calculates the product of all non-null values in a given set. This function is affected by the order of the input values for floating-point numbers due to the nature of floating-point arithmetic.
short_description: Calculates the product of all non-null numeric values in a set.

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The column of numeric values to calculate the product of.

name: prompt
description: The prompt function allows interaction with Large Language Models (LLMs) directly from SQL to generate both free-form text and structured data. It supports various models like OpenAI's GPT series. The function's return type depends on the parameters provided: it returns a VARCHAR for simple text generation, a specific SQL type when using the 'return_type' parameter, a STRUCT when a schema is defined with the 'struct' parameter, and a JSON value when a 'json_schema' is given.
short_description: Interacts with Large Language Models (LLMs) to generate free-form text or structured data like STRUCTs, JSON, or any specified SQL type.
usage_example: SELECT prompt('John is 30 years old and lives in NYC', return_type := 'STRUCT(name VARCHAR, age INTEGER, city VARCHAR)') AS person_info;

parameters.name: prompt_text
parameters.description: The text input to send to the model.

parameters.name: struct
parameters.description: Defines the output schema as a struct, e.g., `{summary: 'VARCHAR', persons: 'VARCHAR[]'}`. This will result in a STRUCT output. Mutually exclusive with 'return_type' and 'json_schema'.

parameters.name: struct_descr
parameters.description: Provides descriptions for the struct fields that will be added to the model's context, e.g., `{summary: 'a 1 sentence summary of the text', persons: 'an array of all persons mentioned in the text'}`.

parameters.name: model
parameters.description: The model to use for the generation.

parameters.name: temperature
parameters.description: Model temperature value between 0 and 1. Lower values produce more deterministic outputs. Not supported with GPT-5 models; use 'reasoning_effort' instead.

parameters.name: json_schema
parameters.description: A JSON schema string that defines the output structure, causing the function to return a JSON value. Provides more flexibility than the struct/struct_descr parameters. Mutually exclusive with 'return_type' and 'struct'.

parameters.name: return_type
parameters.description: Specifies the exact SQL type to return (e.g., 'INTEGER', 'BOOLEAN', 'DATE', 'VARCHAR[]', 'STRUCT(name VARCHAR, age INTEGER)'). Supports most DuckDB types including primitives, arrays, structs, and enums. Mutually exclusive with 'struct' and 'json_schema'.

parameters.name: reasoning_effort
parameters.description: Controls reasoning depth for GPT-5 models only. Higher effort may improve accuracy for complex tasks. Only available for GPT-5 series models.

name: prompt_explain
description: Analyzes and explains a given SQL query in plain English using MotherDuck AI. This function helps in understanding complex queries, verifying their intent, and learning SQL concepts. It parses the query, analyzes the schema of referenced tables, identifies the operations being performed (like filtering, joining, and aggregating), and translates this technical information into a clear, human-readable explanation. You can optionally provide a list of tables or a dictionary of tables and columns to give the AI more context, which is especially useful in large databases.
short_description: Analyzes and explains a SQL query in plain English using MotherDuck AI.
usage_example: CALL prompt_explain('SELECT COUNT(*) as domain_count, SUBSTRING(SPLIT_PART(url, ''//'', 2), 1, POSITION(''/'' IN SPLIT_PART(url, ''//'', 2)) - 1) as domain FROM hn.hacker_news WHERE url IS NOT NULL GROUP BY domain ORDER BY domain_count DESC LIMIT 10;');

parameters.name: query
parameters.description: The SQL query to explain.

parameters.name: include_tables
parameters.description: An array of table names or a dictionary mapping table names to a list of column names to consider for context. Defaults to all tables in the current database.

name: prompt_fix_line
description: A table function that uses MotherDuck AI to correct specific lines in SQL queries containing syntax or spelling errors. Unlike `prompt_fixup`, which rewrites the entire query, this function targets only the problematic lines, making it faster and more precise for localized errors. It is ideal for fixing minor syntax errors in large queries where preserving the original query structure is important. The function can use a provided error message to help identify the line to correct and returns a table with the line number and the corrected content.
short_description: Corrects specific lines in a SQL query that contain syntax or spelling errors.
usage_example: CALL prompt_fix_line('SEELECT COUNT(*) as domain_count FROM hn.hackers', error='Parser Error: syntax error at or near "SEELECT" LINE 1: SEELECT COUNT(*) as domain_count FROM h... ^');

parameters.name: query
parameters.description: The SQL query that needs correction.

parameters.name: error
parameters.description: The error message from the SQL parser (helps identify the problematic line).

parameters.name: include_tables
parameters.description: Array of table names to consider for context (defaults to all tables in current database).

name: prompt_fixup
description: Allows MotherDuck AI to correct and completely rewrite SQL queries that have logical or severe syntactical issues. It analyzes the problematic query, identifies issues, and generates a corrected version that follows proper SQL syntax and semantics. This is suitable for major syntax problems or logical errors, as opposed to minor typos where `prompt_fix_line` might be more efficient.
short_description: Corrects and completely rewrites SQL queries with logical or severe syntactical issues using AI.
usage_example: CALL prompt_fixup('SELECT user_id, AVG(score) FROM hn.stories GROUP BY score');

parameters.name: query
parameters.description: The SQL query that needs correction.

parameters.name: include_tables
parameters.description: Array of table names to consider for context (defaults to all tables in current database).

name: prompt_query
description: The prompt_query pragma allows you to ask questions about your data in natural language. This feature translates your plain English questions into SQL, executes the query, and returns the results. MotherDuck analyzes your database schema, generates the appropriate SQL, and executes the query on your behalf. This is a read-only operation and does not allow queries that modify the database.
short_description: Translates a natural language question into a SQL query and executes it, returning the results.
usage_example: PRAGMA prompt_query('what are the top domains being shared on hacker_news?')

parameters.name: question
parameters.description: The natural language question about your data.

name: prompt_schema
description: Analyzes and describes the contents of the current database in plain English. This function helps understand the structure, purpose, and relationships between tables without manual inspection. It can be used to get a high-level overview of the entire database or a specific subset of tables. The analysis process involves schema extraction, data sampling, relationship detection, domain recognition, and finally, generating a human-readable summary.
short_description: Analyzes and describes the contents of a database in plain English.

parameters.name: include_tables
parameters.description: Array of table names to consider for analysis.

name: prompt_sql
description: Generates SQL queries from natural language questions. It analyzes the database schema and content to translate a plain English request into a valid SQL query. The scope of the schema analysis can be controlled by specifying which tables and columns to include, which is useful for focusing on relevant data in large databases and improving performance. This function is helpful for users less familiar with SQL and for experienced users working with new schemas.
short_description: Generates a SQL query from a natural language question based on the database schema.
usage_example: CALL prompt_sql('what are the top domains being shared on hacker_news?');

parameters.name: natural_language_question
parameters.description: Your query in plain English describing the data you want to analyze.

parameters.name: include_tables
parameters.description: Specifies which tables and columns to consider for query generation. Accepts an array of table names (e.g., ['table1', 'table2']) or a map of tables to columns (e.g., {'table1': ['col1', 'col2']}). When not provided, all tables in the current database are considered.

name: quantile_cont
description: Calculates the interpolated quantile for a given position. The position must be between -1 and 1. The function determines the value by linear interpolation between adjacent values in the sorted dataset. Values of 'pos' between -1 and 0 correspond to counting backwards from 1. More precisely, quantile_cont(x, -y) is equivalent to quantile_cont(x, 1 - y). If the position is provided as a list of floats, the function returns a list of the corresponding interpolated quantiles.
short_description: Calculates the interpolated quantile for a given position (-1 to 1).

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The column over which to compute the quantile.

parameters.name: pos
parameters.description: The quantile position or a list of positions, between -1.0 and 1.0 inclusive.

name: quantile_disc
description: Calculates the discrete quantile for a given position. This function returns the value from the input set that corresponds to the specified quantile. It determines the value by finding the first element in the sorted set whose cumulative distribution is greater than or equal to the given quantile. This corresponds to Type 1 in the Hyndman & Fan taxonomy. If the position is provided as a list of values, the function returns a list of the corresponding discrete quantiles.
short_description: Calculates the discrete quantile, returning the value from the input set corresponding to a specified position.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE scores(score INT); INSERT INTO scores VALUES (70), (80), (80), (90), (100); SELECT quantile_disc(score, 0.5) AS median, quantile_disc(score, [0.25, 0.75]) AS quartiles FROM scores;

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The input column over which to compute the discrete quantile.

parameters.name: pos
parameters.description: A single quantile or a list of quantiles to compute. Each quantile must be between 0.0 and 1.0.

name: quarter
description: Extracts the quarter of the year (1-4) from a date, timestamp, or interval. This is a dedicated function for extracting the quarter part, similar to using `date_part('quarter', ...)`.
short_description: Extracts the quarter of the year (1-4) from a date, timestamp, or interval.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The date, timestamp, or interval from which to extract the quarter.

name: query
description: A table function that parses and executes the SQL query provided as a string. This function only accepts constant strings as input. Be cautious, as this function can execute arbitrary queries, which may alter the state of the database.
short_description: Parses and executes a SQL query string, returning the result as a table.

parameters.name: query_string
parameters.description: The SQL query to be parsed and executed. Must be a constant string.

name: query_table
description: A table function that returns a table by its name. It can also return the union of multiple tables. When multiple table names are provided as a list, the function combines their rows. An optional boolean parameter can be used to specify that the union should be performed by column name (`UNION ALL BY NAME`) rather than by position.
short_description: Returns a table by name, or a union of multiple tables.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE t1 AS SELECT 1 AS a, 'foo' AS b; CREATE TABLE t2 AS SELECT 2 AS a, 'bar' AS b; SELECT * FROM query_table(['t1', 't2']);

parameters.name: tbl_name
parameters.description: The name of the table to query.

parameters.name: tbl_names
parameters.description: A list of table names to be combined into a single result set.

parameters.name: by_name
parameters.description: If set to true, the function performs a `UNION ALL BY NAME`, aligning columns by name instead of position.

name: radians
description: Converts a numeric value from degrees to radians.
short_description: Converts an angle from degrees to radians.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The angle in degrees to be converted to radians.

name: random
description: Returns a random number `x` in the range `0.0 <= x < 1.0`.
short_description: Returns a random number between 0.0 (inclusive) and 1.0 (exclusive)

name: range
description: Creates a list of values in a specified range. The range starts at an inclusive 'start' value and increments by a 'step' value until it reaches the exclusive 'stop' value. The function supports both numeric and temporal (TIMESTAMP, TIMESTAMPTZ) ranges. For numeric ranges, if only one argument is provided, it is treated as the 'stop' value, with 'start' defaulting to 0 and 'step' to 1.
short_description: Creates a list of values within a specified range, with an exclusive stop value.

parameters.name: stop
parameters.description: The exclusive upper bound of the range. The range starts at 0 and increments by 1.

parameters.name: start
parameters.description: The inclusive lower bound of the range.

parameters.name: stop
parameters.description: The exclusive upper bound of the range.

parameters.name: step
parameters.description: The increment between values in the range.

parameters.name: start
parameters.description: The inclusive lower bound of the temporal range.

parameters.name: stop
parameters.description: The exclusive upper bound of the temporal range.

parameters.name: step
parameters.description: The interval to step by.

name: range
description: Generates a table containing a single column with a sequence of values. The function can generate numeric (BIGINT) or temporal (TIMESTAMP, TIMESTAMPTZ) sequences. For numeric ranges, the sequence starts at a given value (defaulting to 0) and increments by a step (defaulting to 1), continuing as long as the values are less than the stop value. For temporal ranges, the start, stop, and step (as an INTERVAL) must all be specified. The stop value is exclusive in all cases.
short_description: Generates a table containing a sequence of numeric or temporal values.
usage_example: SELECT * FROM range(TIMESTAMP '1992-01-01', TIMESTAMP '1992-03-01', INTERVAL '1' MONTH);

parameters.name: stop
parameters.description: The exclusive upper bound of the range. The range starts at 0 with a step of 1.

parameters.name: start
parameters.description: The inclusive lower bound of the range.

parameters.name: stop
parameters.description: The exclusive upper bound of the range.

parameters.name: step
parameters.description: The increment between values in the range. Defaults to 1.

parameters.name: start
parameters.description: The inclusive lower bound of the temporal range.

parameters.name: stop
parameters.description: The exclusive upper bound of the temporal range.

parameters.name: step
parameters.description: The increment between values in the range.

name: rank
description: The rank of the current row with gaps; same as the row_number of its first peer. If an ORDER BY clause is specified, the rank is computed within the frame using the provided ordering instead of the frame ordering.
short_description: The rank of the current row with gaps; same as row_number of its first peer.
usage_example: SELECT rank() OVER (ORDER BY sales) FROM transactions;

parameters.name: ORDER BY ordering
parameters.description: If an ORDER BY clause is specified, the rank is computed within the frame using the provided ordering instead of the frame ordering.

name: rank_dense
description: The rank of the current row without gaps; this function counts peer groups.
short_description: The rank of the current row without gaps; this function counts peer groups.
usage_example: SELECT rank_dense() OVER (ORDER BY sales) FROM transactions;

name: read_avro
description: Reads data from one or more Apache Avro files. The function can handle local files, remote files (e.g., from HTTP or S3), and glob patterns. It automatically translates the Avro schema to a DuckDB schema, simplifying Avro's union types for nullability and flattening top-level records into columns for easier querying.
short_description: Reads data from Apache Avro files and returns a table.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path or a list of paths to the Avro file(s) to read. Can also be a glob pattern.

parameters.name: filename
parameters.description: If set to true, an additional 'filename' column is added to the result, containing the path of the file for each row.

parameters.name: union_by_name
parameters.description: Whether to union columns by name (currently not supported for Avro).

parameters.name: hive_partitioning
parameters.description: Whether to read the file as a Hive-partitioned dataset.

parameters.name: hive_types
parameters.description: A struct defining the column names and types for Hive-partitioned data.

parameters.name: hive_types_autocast
parameters.description: Whether to automatically cast Hive-partitioned columns to the specified types.

name: read_blob
description: Reads the content from a file (or multiple files via a list or glob pattern) and returns it as a table with a single column of type BLOB. This is useful for loading binary data directly into the database.
short_description: Reads the content of a file into a single-column table as a BLOB.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the file(s) to read. This can be a single filename, a list of filenames, or a glob pattern.

name: read_csv
description: Reads data from one or more CSV files. This table function is capable of automatically detecting the CSV dialect (delimiter, quote character, etc.), data types, and presence of a header row. It supports reading from single files, multiple files via a list, or using glob patterns. Numerous options are available to manually override the auto-detection for parsing complex or non-standard CSV files, including specifying column names and types, handling date and timestamp formats, and managing parsing errors by skipping lines or storing them for later analysis.
short_description: Reads data from one or more CSV files, automatically inferring the schema and file options.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to a CSV file or a list/glob pattern for multiple files.

parameters.name: delim
parameters.description: Delimiter character used to separate columns within each line. Alias: 'sep'.

parameters.name: header
parameters.description: Specifies if the first line of each file contains the column names.

parameters.name: filename
parameters.description: Add path of the containing file to each row, as a string column named 'filename'.

parameters.name: normalize_names
parameters.description: Normalize column names by removing non-alphanumeric characters.

parameters.name: ignore_errors
parameters.description: Ignore any parsing errors encountered.

parameters.name: columns
parameters.description: Column names and types, as a struct (e.g., {'col1': 'INTEGER', 'col2': 'VARCHAR'}). Using this option disables auto detection of the schema.

parameters.name: quote
parameters.description: String used to quote values.

parameters.name: union_by_name
parameters.description: Align columns from different files by column name instead of position.

parameters.name: types
parameters.description: Column types, as either a list (by position) or a struct (by name). Aliases: 'dtypes', 'column_types'.

parameters.name: skip
parameters.description: Number of lines to skip at the start of each file.

parameters.name: escape
parameters.description: String used to escape the 'quote' character within quoted values.

parameters.name: auto_detect
parameters.description: Auto detect CSV parameters.

parameters.name: encoding
parameters.description: Encoding used by the CSV file.

parameters.name: strict_mode
