parameters.description: Enforces the strictness level of the CSV Reader. If true, an error is thrown on any issue; if false, the parser attempts to read structurally incorrect files.

parameters.name: all_varchar
parameters.description: Skip type detection and assume all columns are of type VARCHAR.

parameters.name: nullstr
parameters.description: String(s) that represent a NULL value. Alias: 'null'.

parameters.name: null_padding
parameters.description: Pad the remaining columns on the right with NULL values when a line lacks columns.

parameters.name: comment
parameters.description: Character used to initiate comments. Lines starting with a comment character are ignored.

parameters.name: sample_size
parameters.description: Number of sample lines for auto detection of parameters. Set to -1 to scan the entire file.

parameters.name: store_rejects
parameters.description: Skip any lines with errors and store them in the rejects table.

parameters.name: new_line
parameters.description: New line character(s).

parameters.name: compression
parameters.description: Method used to compress CSV files. By default this is detected automatically from the file extension.

parameters.name: max_line_size
parameters.description: Maximum line size, in bytes. Alias: 'maximum_line_size'.

parameters.name: names
parameters.description: Column names, as a list. Alias: 'column_names'.

parameters.name: dateformat
parameters.description: Date format used when parsing dates.

parameters.name: rejects_scan
parameters.description: Name of the temporary table where information on faulty scans is stored.

parameters.name: rejects_table
parameters.description: Name of the temporary table where information on faulty lines is stored.

parameters.name: decimal_separator
parameters.description: Decimal separator for numbers.

parameters.name: rejects_limit
parameters.description: Upper limit on the number of faulty lines per file that are recorded in the rejects table. Setting this to 0 means that no limit is applied.

parameters.name: timestampformat
parameters.description: Timestamp format used when parsing timestamps.

parameters.name: auto_type_candidates
parameters.description: Types that the sniffer uses when detecting column types. The VARCHAR type is always included as a fallback option.

parameters.name: hive_partitioning
parameters.description: Interpret the path as a Hive partitioned path.

parameters.name: force_not_null
parameters.description: Do not match values in the specified columns against the NULL string.

parameters.name: allow_quoted_nulls
parameters.description: Allow the conversion of quoted values to NULL values.

parameters.name: buffer_size
parameters.description: Size of the buffers used to read files, in bytes. Must be large enough to hold four lines and can significantly impact performance.

parameters.name: parallel
parameters.description: Use the parallel CSV reader.

parameters.name: thousands
parameters.description: Character used to identify thousands separators in numeric values.

name: read_json
description: Reads data from one or more JSON files, returning the content as a table. The function can automatically infer the schema (column names and types) and the format of the JSON file (array of objects, newline-delimited, etc.). It supports reading from a single file, a list of files, or a glob pattern. Various options can be provided to control parsing behavior, such as specifying column types, date/timestamp formats, and compression methods.
short_description: Reads data from one or more JSON files, automatically inferring the schema and returning the result as a table.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the JSON file(s). This can be a single file, a list of files, or a glob pattern.

parameters.name: filename
parameters.description: Whether or not an extra 'filename' column should be included in the result. This is now added automatically as a virtual column and this option is kept for compatibility.

parameters.name: auto_detect
parameters.description: Whether to automatically detect the names of the keys and data types of the values.

parameters.name: union_by_name
parameters.description: Whether the schemas of multiple JSON files should be unified by name.

parameters.name: ignore_errors
parameters.description: Whether to ignore parse errors (only possible when format is 'newline_delimited').

parameters.name: columns
parameters.description: A struct that specifies the key names and value types contained within the JSON file (e.g., {key1: 'INTEGER', key2: 'VARCHAR'}). If auto_detect is enabled, these will be inferred.

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: Specifies how to read the JSON from a file.

parameters.name: sample_size
parameters.description: Option to define the number of sample objects for automatic JSON type detection. Set to -1 to scan the entire input file.

parameters.name: maximum_object_size
parameters.description: The maximum size of a JSON object (in bytes).

parameters.name: compression
parameters.description: The compression type for the file. By default, this will be detected automatically from the file extension.

parameters.name: hive_partitioning
parameters.description: Whether or not to interpret the path as a Hive partitioned path. If not provided, it is auto-detected.

parameters.name: maximum_depth
parameters.description: Maximum nesting depth to which the automatic schema detection detects types. Set to -1 to fully detect nested JSON types.

parameters.name: MD_RUN
parameters.description: Controls where the function is executed. `LOCAL` runs locally, `REMOTE` runs in MotherDuck, and `AUTO` (the default) runs remotely for remote file paths (s3://, https://, etc.).

parameters.name: records
parameters.description: Specifies whether the JSON contains records that should be unpacked into individual columns.

parameters.name: map_inference_threshold
parameters.description: Controls the threshold for number of columns whose schema will be auto-detected; if JSON schema auto-detection would infer a STRUCT type for a field that has more than this threshold number of subfields, it infers a MAP type instead. Set to -1 to disable MAP inference.

parameters.name: dateformat
parameters.description: Specifies the date format to use when parsing dates.

parameters.name: field_appearance_threshold
parameters.description: The JSON reader divides the number of appearances of each JSON field by the auto-detection sample size. If the average over the fields of an object is less than this threshold, it will default to using a MAP type with value type of merged field types.

parameters.name: maximum_sample_files
parameters.description: The maximum number of JSON files sampled for auto-detection.

parameters.name: timestampformat
parameters.description: Specifies the timestamp format to use when parsing timestamps.

name: read_json_objects
description: Reads a JSON file and returns a table with a single column named 'json'. Each object found in the file is treated as a separate row. This function is useful for processing raw JSON objects before further parsing. The 'format' parameter controls how objects are identified within the file: 'unstructured' reads top-level JSON objects, 'newline_delimited' reads standard NDJSON, and 'array' (the default) reads each element from a top-level JSON array.
short_description: Reads a JSON file, treating each object as a row in a single 'json' column.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: Path to the JSON file(s). Can be a single file, a list of files, or a glob pattern.

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: Specifies how to read the JSON from a file.

parameters.name: maximum_object_size
parameters.description: The maximum size of a JSON object (in bytes).

parameters.name: compression
parameters.description: The compression type for the file. By default this will be detected automatically from the file extension.

parameters.name: ignore_errors
parameters.description: Whether to ignore parse errors (only possible when 'format' is 'newline_delimited').

parameters.name: hive_partitioning
parameters.description: Whether or not to interpret the path as a Hive partitioned path.

parameters.name: filename
parameters.description: Whether or not an extra 'filename' column should be included in the result.

parameters.name: maximum_sample_files
parameters.description: The maximum number of JSON files sampled for auto-detection.

name: read_json_objects_auto
description: Reads JSON objects from one or more files, automatically detecting the JSON format. This function is an alias for `read_json_objects` with the `format` parameter set to 'auto'. It returns a table with a single column named 'json' of type JSON, where each row contains one JSON object from the input file(s).
short_description: Reads JSON objects from a file, automatically detecting the format.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the JSON file(s). This can be a single file path, a list of paths, or a glob pattern.

parameters.name: compression
parameters.description: The compression type for the file. By default this will be detected automatically from the file extension (e.g., t.json.gz will use gzip, t.json will use none).

parameters.name: filename
parameters.description: Whether or not an extra 'filename' column should be included in the result. Since DuckDB v1.3.0, the filename column is added automatically as a virtual column and this option is only kept for compatibility reasons.

parameters.name: hive_partitioning
parameters.description: Whether or not to interpret the path as a Hive partitioned path. Defaults to being auto-detected.

parameters.name: ignore_errors
parameters.description: Whether to ignore parse errors (only possible when the detected format is 'newline_delimited').

parameters.name: maximum_sample_files
parameters.description: The maximum number of JSON files sampled for auto-detection.

parameters.name: maximum_object_size
parameters.description: The maximum size of a JSON object (in bytes).

name: read_ndjson_objects
description: Reads a file in newline-delimited JSON (NDJSON) format, where each line is a separate JSON object. This function is an alias for `read_json_objects` with the `format` parameter implicitly set to `newline_delimited`. It returns a table with a single column named 'json' containing each parsed JSON object.
short_description: Reads a newline-delimited JSON (NDJSON) file, returning each object as a row.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the JSON file(s). Can be a single file, a list of files, or a glob pattern.

parameters.name: hive_partitioning
parameters.description: Whether to interpret the path as a Hive partitioned path.

parameters.name: ignore_errors
parameters.description: Whether to ignore parse errors.

parameters.name: filename
parameters.description: Whether to include a column with the filename in the result.

parameters.name: compression
parameters.description: The compression type for the file. Options are 'none', 'gzip', 'zstd', and 'auto_detect'.

parameters.name: maximum_object_size
parameters.description: The maximum size of a JSON object in bytes.

parameters.name: union_by_name
parameters.description: Whether the schemas of multiple JSON files should be unified by name.

name: read_parquet
description: Reads data from one or more Parquet files. The function can take a single file path, a list of file paths, or a glob pattern to read multiple files as a single table. It supports various options to control how the data is read, such as handling Hive partitioning, unifying schemas by name across different files, and reading from remote sources like HTTP(S) or S3. DuckDB automatically applies projection and filter pushdown for efficient querying, reading only the necessary columns and row groups from the Parquet files.
short_description: Reads data from one or more Parquet files, supporting local paths, globs, and remote URLs.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the Parquet file(s). Can be a single path, a list of paths, or a glob pattern.

parameters.name: filename
parameters.description: If true, includes a `filename` column in the result. This is kept for compatibility; the `filename` virtual column is now available by default without this option.

parameters.name: hive_partitioning
parameters.description: If true, interprets the file path as a Hive partitioned dataset, extracting partition values from the directory structure.

parameters.name: union_by_name
parameters.description: If true, unifies schemas of multiple files by column name instead of by position. Columns not present in a file will be filled with NULL.

parameters.name: MD_RUN
parameters.description: Specifies where the function should be executed in a MotherDuck environment. 'LOCAL' runs it on the local DuckDB instance. 'REMOTE' runs it on MotherDuck's cloud infrastructure. 'AUTO' (the default) decides based on the file path, running remotely for remote sources (e.g., s3://, https://) and locally for local files.

parameters.name: binary_as_string
parameters.description: If true, loads binary columns as strings. Useful for legacy Parquet files that do not correctly set the UTF8 flag for strings.

parameters.name: file_row_number
parameters.description: If true, includes a `file_row_number` column in the result, indicating the row number within each file.

parameters.name: hive_types
parameters.description: A struct specifying the data types for Hive partition columns, e.g., `{'year': 'DATE'}`.

parameters.name: encryption_config
parameters.description: Configuration for reading encrypted Parquet files.

parameters.name: hive_types_autocast
parameters.description: If true, automatically detects and casts data types for Hive partition columns (e.g., for dates, timestamps, and bigints).

parameters.name: schema
parameters.description: Allows reading a Parquet file with a specified schema, enabling column renaming, adding, deleting, reordering, or casting. Field IDs are required. Cannot be used with `union_by_name=true`.

name: read_text
description: Reads the content from a source file and returns it as a VARCHAR in a single-column table. The source can be a single filename, a list of filenames, or a glob pattern. The file content is validated to be valid UTF-8. If the file contains invalid UTF-8, an error is thrown, suggesting the use of `read_blob` instead.
short_description: Reads the content of a text file into a single-column table.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the text file(s), which can be a single path, a list of paths, or a glob pattern.

name: read_xlsx
description: Reads data from an Excel (.xlsx) file. This function provides several options to control the import process, such as specifying the sheet, a cell range, and how to handle headers and data types. Type inference is performed automatically based on cell formats and content, but can be overridden.
short_description: Reads data from an Excel (.xlsx) file, with options to control the import process.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the .xlsx file to read.

parameters.name: sheet
parameters.description: The name of the sheet in the xlsx file to read. Defaults to the first sheet.

parameters.name: header
parameters.description: Whether to treat the first row as containing the names of the resulting columns. The default is automatically inferred.

parameters.name: range
parameters.description: The range of cells to read, in spreadsheet notation (e.g., 'A1:B2'). If not specified, the range is inferred automatically.

parameters.name: all_varchar
parameters.description: Whether to read all cells as containing VARCHARs.

parameters.name: ignore_errors
parameters.description: Whether to ignore errors and silently replace cells that can't be cast to the corresponding inferred column type with NULLs.

parameters.name: empty_as_varchar
parameters.description: Whether to treat empty cells as VARCHAR instead of DOUBLE when trying to automatically infer column types.

parameters.name: stop_at_empty
parameters.description: Whether to stop reading the file when an empty row is encountered. If an explicit 'range' option is provided, this is false by default, otherwise true.

name: regexp_escape
description: Escapes special patterns to turn a string into a regular expression, similar to Python's `re.escape` function. This is useful when you need to match a literal string that may contain characters with special meaning in regular expressions, such as '.', '*', or '+'.
short_description: Escapes special characters in a string for use in a regular expression.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The input string to escape.

name: regexp_extract
description: Extracts a substring that matches a regular expression pattern. It can either return a specific capturing group as a string or return multiple capturing groups as a struct with specified names. If no match is found, it returns an empty string or a struct with empty string values, respectively. The function's behavior can be modified with an optional options string.
short_description: Extracts a substring matching a regular expression, returning a specific capturing group or a struct of named groups.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to search within.

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.description: The regular expression pattern to match.

parameters.name: group
parameters.description: The capturing group to extract. Group 0 refers to the entire match.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: A string of single-character flags to modify the regex behavior.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to search within.

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.description: The regular expression pattern to match.

parameters.name: name_list
parameters.description: A list of names to assign to the corresponding capturing groups, which will be returned as a struct.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: A string of single-character flags to modify the regex behavior.

name: regexp_extract_all
description: Finds all non-overlapping occurrences of a regular expression pattern within a string and returns a list of the values from a specified capturing group. Optional flags can be provided to control matching behavior, such as case-insensitivity.
short_description: Finds all non-overlapping regex matches in a string and returns a list of values from a specified capturing group.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to search within.

parameters.name: regex
parameters.description: The regular expression pattern to match.

parameters.name: group
parameters.description: The capturing group to extract. Defaults to 0, which extracts the entire match.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: A string of single-character flags to modify the regex behavior (e.g., 'i' for case-insensitive, 's' for non-newline sensitive).

name: regexp_full_match
description: Returns true if the entire input string matches the provided regular expression pattern. This is in contrast to `regexp_matches`, which checks if the pattern is contained anywhere within the string. Optional flags can be provided to modify the matching behavior, such as for case-insensitivity.
short_description: Returns true if the entire string matches the regular expression.
usage_example: SELECT regexp_full_match('anabanana', '(an)*'); -- Returns false because the pattern does not match the entire string

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to match.

parameters.name: regex
parameters.description: The regular expression pattern.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: A string of single-character flags to modify the regex behavior. Possible options include 'c' (case-sensitive), 'i' (case-insensitive), 'l' (literal matching), 'm'/'n'/'p' (newline-sensitive), and 's' (non-newline sensitive).

name: regexp_matches
description: Returns true if the input string contains a match for the regular expression pattern, and false otherwise. Unlike the SIMILAR TO operator, this function does not require the entire string to match the pattern; it only checks for the presence of the pattern within the string. Special tokens like `^` and `$` can be used to anchor the regular expression to the start and end of the string, respectively. An optional string of flags can be provided to modify the matching behavior.
short_description: Checks if a string contains a match for a regular expression pattern.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to search within.

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.description: The regular expression pattern to match.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: A string of single-character flags that change the function's behavior. 'c' for case-sensitive, 'i' for case-insensitive, 'l' for literal matching, 'm'/'n'/'p' for newline-sensitive, and 's' for non-newline-sensitive matching.

name: regexp_replace
description: If the input string contains a substring matching the regular expression pattern, this function replaces the matching part with the replacement string. By default, only the first occurrence is replaced. To replace all occurrences, the 'g' (global) flag can be provided in the optional options string. The replacement string can also use backreferences (e.g., '\1', '\2') to include parts of the matched string (capturing groups) in the result.
short_description: Replaces substrings matching a regular expression with a replacement string.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to search and replace within.

parameters.name: pattern
parameters.description: The RE2 regular expression pattern.

parameters.name: replacement
parameters.description: The string to substitute for each match. Backreferences \1 through \9 can be used to insert text from the corresponding parenthesized group.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: A string of single-character flags that change the function's behavior.

name: regexp_split_to_array
description: Splits a string into an array of substrings using a regular expression as a delimiter. An optional string of flags can be provided to modify the matching behavior.
short_description: Splits a string into an array using a regular expression delimiter.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to be split.

parameters.name: regex
parameters.description: The regular expression to use as a delimiter.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: A string of flags to modify the regular expression matching behavior. Possible flags include 'c' (case-sensitive), 'i' (case-insensitive), 'l' (literal), 'm'/'n'/'p' (newline-sensitive), and 's' (non-newline sensitive).

name: regexp_split_to_table
description: Splits a string based on a regular expression delimiter and returns the resulting substrings as a table, with each substring in its own row. An optional `options` string can be provided to control the matching behavior, such as case sensitivity.
short_description: Splits a string by a regular expression and returns a row for each part.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to be split.

parameters.name: regex
parameters.description: The regular expression pattern to use as a delimiter.

parameters.name: options
parameters.description: A string of flags to modify the regular expression's behavior (e.g., 'i' for case-insensitive).

name: regr_avgx
description: Calculates the average of the independent variable for non-NULL pairs, where x is the independent variable and y is the dependent variable.
short_description: Calculates the average of the independent variable (x) for non-NULL pairs.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The dependent variable.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The independent variable.

name: regr_avgy
description: Calculates the average of the dependent variable for non-NULL pairs, where 'x' is the independent variable and 'y' is the dependent variable. This is a statistical aggregate function used in linear regression analysis.
short_description: Calculates the average of the dependent variable (y) in a linear regression model.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE points(x DOUBLE, y DOUBLE); INSERT INTO points VALUES (1, 2), (2, 3), (3, 5), (4, 4); SELECT regr_avgy(y, x) FROM points;

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The dependent variable.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The independent variable.

name: regr_count
description: A statistical aggregate function that counts the number of input pairs where neither the dependent variable (y) nor the independent variable (x) is NULL. It is typically used in linear regression analysis.
short_description: Counts the number of non-NULL input pairs for linear regression

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The dependent variable.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The independent variable.

name: regr_intercept
description: Calculates the y-intercept of the univariate linear regression line for a set of non-null data pairs. The first argument represents the dependent variable (y), and the second argument represents the independent variable (x).
short_description: Calculates the y-intercept of the linear regression line

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The dependent variable.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The independent variable.

name: regr_r2
description: Calculates the coefficient of determination (R-squared), which is the squared Pearson correlation coefficient between two variables. It is used in linear regression where 'y' is the dependent variable and 'x' is the independent variable.
short_description: Calculates the coefficient of determination (R-squared) for a linear regression.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The dependent variable.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The independent variable.

name: regr_slope
description: Calculates the slope of the linear regression line for a set of non-null data pairs. The first parameter represents the dependent variable (y), and the second represents the independent variable (x).
short_description: Calculates the slope of the linear regression line.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE points(x DOUBLE, y DOUBLE); INSERT INTO points VALUES (1, 2), (2, 4), (3, 6.1); SELECT regr_slope(y, x) FROM points;

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The dependent variable.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The independent variable.

name: regr_sxx
description: Calculates the sample variance of the independent variable for non-NULL pairs, which includes Bessel's bias correction. In this function, x represents the independent variable and y represents the dependent variable.
short_description: Calculates the sample variance of the independent variable (x) for non-NULL pairs in a regression analysis.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE points(y DOUBLE, x DOUBLE); INSERT INTO points VALUES (2, 1), (4, 2), (5, 3), (4, 4), (5, 5); SELECT regr_sxx(y, x) FROM points;

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The dependent variable.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The independent variable.

name: regr_sxy
description: Calculates the sample covariance between two variables, which includes Bessel's bias correction. It operates on non-NULL pairs of the dependent variable (y) and the independent variable (x).
short_description: Calculates the sample covariance between two variables

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The dependent variable.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The independent variable.

name: regr_syy
description: Calculates the sample variance of the dependent variable for non-NULL pairs, where x is the independent variable and y is the dependent variable. This function includes Bessel's bias correction.
short_description: Calculates the sample variance of the dependent variable in a linear regression.

parameters.name: y
parameters.description: The dependent variable.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The independent variable.

name: remove_iceberg_table_properties
description: Removes one or more specified properties from an Iceberg table. The table is identified by its fully qualified name, and the properties to be removed are provided as a list of strings.
short_description: Removes specified properties from an Iceberg table.

parameters.name: table
parameters.description: The fully qualified name of the Iceberg table from which to remove properties.

parameters.name: property_list
parameters.description: A list of property keys to remove from the table's metadata.

name: repeat
description: Repeats a string, list, or blob a specified number of times.
short_description: Repeats a string, list, or blob a specified number of times.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: The string, list, or blob to be repeated.

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: The number of times to repeat the input.

name: repeat
description: The `repeat` table function generates a table by repeating a given value a specified number of times. Each repetition of the value is returned as a separate row in a single-column table.
short_description: Generates a table by repeating a value a specified number of times, with each repetition as a row.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The value to be repeated in each row.

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: The number of rows to generate.

name: repeat_row
description: A table function that generates a table with a specified number of rows. The columns of the table are defined by the constant values provided as arguments, and the number of rows is specified by the `num_rows` parameter. Each value provided becomes a separate column in the output table.
short_description: Generates a table by repeating a single row of specified values a given number of times.

parameters.name: varargs
parameters.description: One or more constant values to be repeated in each row. Each value will form a separate column in the resulting table.

parameters.name: num_rows
parameters.description: The number of identical rows to generate.

name: replace
description: Replaces all occurrences of a specified substring (the 'source') with another substring (the 'target') within a given string.
short_description: Replaces all occurrences of a substring with another substring.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The input string to modify.

parameters.name: source
parameters.description: The substring to be replaced.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: The substring to replace the source substring with.

name: reservoir_quantile
description: Calculates an approximate quantile using reservoir sampling. This method is suitable for large datasets where an exact calculation would be too slow or memory-intensive. The size of the sample used for the approximation can be specified, with a default of 8192.
short_description: Calculates the approximate quantile using reservoir sampling.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The column of values for which to compute the approximate quantile.

parameters.name: quantile
parameters.description: The quantile to compute, a value between 0 and 1. Can be a single value or a list of values.

parameters.name: sample_size
parameters.description: The size of the reservoir sample used for the approximation.

name: reverse
description: Reverses the order of characters in a given string.
short_description: Reverses the order of characters in a string

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to be reversed.

name: right
description: Extracts the right-most characters from a string.
short_description: Extracts the right-most characters from a string.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string from which to extract characters.

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: The number of characters to extract.

name: right_grapheme
description: Extracts the specified number of right-most grapheme clusters from a string. A grapheme cluster is a user-perceived character, which may be composed of multiple Unicode code points (e.g., an emoji with a skin tone modifier).
short_description: Extracts the right-most grapheme clusters from a string.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string from which to extract grapheme clusters.

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: The number of grapheme clusters to extract.

name: round
description: Rounds a numeric value to a specified number of decimal places. The number of decimal places can be negative to round digits to the left of the decimal point. If the number of decimal places is not provided, the number is rounded to the nearest integer.
short_description: Rounds a number to a specified number of decimal places.

parameters.name: number
parameters.description: The numeric value to round.

parameters.name: decimal_places
parameters.description: The number of decimal places to round to. Can be negative to round to the left of the decimal point. If not provided, rounds to the nearest integer.

name: round_even
description: Rounds a number to a specified number of decimal places using the 'round half to even' rule, also known as banker's rounding. This method rounds to the nearest value, with ties being rounded to the nearest value with an even least significant digit. For example, 2.5 is rounded to 2, while 3.5 is rounded to 4. The number of decimal places can be negative to round digits to the left of the decimal point.
short_description: Rounds a number to a specified number of decimal places using the 'round half to even' (banker's rounding) rule.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The numeric value to be rounded.

parameters.name: decimal_places
parameters.description: The number of decimal places to round to. Negative values are allowed and round digits to the left of the decimal point.

name: row
description: The row function creates an unnamed STRUCT, also known as a tuple, from a list of input values. This is useful for converting multiple columns or expressions into a single struct column. When used this way, the keys of the struct will be empty strings. The function is variadic, accepting one or more arguments of any type. If multiple expressions are used to create a struct, the `row` function is optional, e.g., `SELECT (x, y)` is equivalent to `SELECT row(x, y)`.
short_description: Creates an unnamed STRUCT (tuple) from a list of values.
usage_example: SELECT row(x, x + 1, y) FROM (SELECT 1 AS x, 'a' AS y) AS s;

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The first value to be included in the struct.
Additional values to be included in the struct.

name: row_number
description: The number of the current row within the partition, counting from 1. If an ORDER BY clause is specified, the row number is computed within the frame using the provided ordering instead of the frame ordering.
short_description: The number of the current row within the partition, counting from 1.
usage_example:
SELECT row_number() OVER (PARTITION BY region ORDER BY time)
FROM sales;

parameters.name: ORDER BY ordering
parameters.description: If an ORDER BY clause is specified, the row number is computed within the frame using the provided ordering instead of the frame ordering.

name: rpad
description: Pads a string on the right with a specified character until it reaches a certain length. If the original string is longer than the target length, it will be truncated on the right.
short_description: Pads a string on the right to a specified length with a given character

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to pad.

parameters.name: count
parameters.description: The desired length of the resulting string.

parameters.name: character
parameters.description: The character to use for padding.

name: rtree_index_dump
description: Returns all the nodes within a specified R-tree index. This function is useful for debugging, profiling, or inspecting the internal structure of the index.
short_description: Returns all nodes within a specified R-tree index for inspection.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE points(p GEOMETRY); INSERT INTO points VALUES (ST_Point(1,1)), (ST_Point(2,2)); CREATE INDEX p_idx ON points USING RTREE(p); SELECT * FROM rtree_index_dump('p_idx');

parameters.name: index_name
parameters.description: The name of the R-tree index to inspect.

name: rtree_index_scan
description: Represents the R-tree index scan operator, which is automatically used by the query optimizer to accelerate spatial queries. This function is not intended to be called directly by users. An R-tree index scan is triggered when a `WHERE` clause contains a supported spatial predicate function (like `ST_Intersects`, `ST_Within`, etc.) on a column with an R-tree index.
short_description: Internal operator for accelerating spatial queries using an R-tree index; not for direct use.

name: rtrim
description: Removes any occurrences of a specified set of characters from the right side of a string. If the characters to remove are not specified, it defaults to removing trailing whitespace.
short_description: Removes specified characters from the right side of a string

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to trim.

parameters.name: characters
parameters.description: The set of characters to remove from the right side of the string. Defaults to whitespace.

name: second
description: Extracts the second component from a temporal value. This is a dedicated function for extracting the 'second' date part.
short_description: Extracts the second component from a temporal value.

parameters.name: date
parameters.description: The temporal value from which to extract the second component.

name: sem
description: Calculates the standard error of the mean for a set of values. NULL values are ignored in the calculation.
short_description: Calculates the standard error of the mean.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE measurements (val DOUBLE); INSERT INTO measurements VALUES (3), (3), (9); SELECT sem(val) FROM measurements; -- Returns 1.6329931618554523

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The input expression over which to calculate the standard error of the mean.

name: set_bit
description: Sets the bit at a specified index within a bitstring to a new value (0 or 1). The first (leftmost) bit is indexed at 0. This function returns a new bitstring with the modified bit.
short_description: Sets the nth bit in a bitstring to a new value

parameters.name: bitstring
parameters.description: The input bitstring.

parameters.name: index
parameters.description: The zero-based index of the bit to set.

parameters.name: new_value
parameters.description: The new value for the bit, which should be either 0 or 1.

name: set_iceberg_table_properties
description: Sets one or more properties on a specified Iceberg table. This function is used to modify the table's metadata properties, such as write behavior modes.
short_description: Sets properties on the specified Iceberg table.

parameters.name: table
parameters.description: The fully qualified name of the Iceberg table.

parameters.name: properties
parameters.description: A map containing the key-value pairs of the properties to set.

name: setseed
description: Sets the seed to be used for the random function.
short_description: Sets the seed for the random number generator.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The seed value to initialize the random number generator.

name: sha1
description: Computes the SHA-1 hash of the input string or blob, returning the result as a hexadecimal VARCHAR.
short_description: Computes the SHA-1 hash of a string or blob.

parameters.name: input
parameters.description: The string or blob to be hashed.

name: sha256
description: Computes the SHA-256 hash of the input string or blob, returning the result as a hexadecimal string.
short_description: Computes the SHA-256 hash of a string or blob.

parameters.name: value
parameters.description: The string or blob value to be hashed.

name: show
description: The SHOW statement provides a convenient way to display various database metadata. It serves as a more readable alternative to certain PRAGMA commands. For example, `SHOW TABLES` is equivalent to `PRAGMA show_tables`.
short_description: Displays database metadata (e.g., tables, databases) as an alternative to PRAGMA commands.

parameters.name: object_specifier
parameters.description: Specifies what metadata to show. This is typically a keyword like TABLES or DATABASES.

name: show_databases
description: Lists all attached databases. This pragma is equivalent to the `SHOW DATABASES` statement.
short_description: Lists all attached databases.

name: show_tables
description: A pragma statement that lists all tables in the current schema.
short_description: Lists all tables in the current schema.

name: show_tables_expanded
description: Lists all tables, providing extra information for each, similar to the output of the DESCRIBE statement.
short_description: Lists all tables with extra information, similar to DESCRIBE.

name: sign
description: Returns the sign of a numeric value. The function returns -1 for negative numbers, 0 for zero, and 1 for positive numbers.
short_description: Returns the sign of the input value as -1, 0, or 1.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The numeric value to check the sign of.

name: signbit
description: Returns whether the signbit is set for a given number. This is a way to check if a number is negative, including negative zero.
short_description: Returns true if the sign bit is set (i.e., the number is negative)

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The input number.

name: sin
description: Computes the sine of a given number, where the number is treated as an angle in radians.
short_description: Computes the sine of an angle in radians.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The input number, representing an angle in radians.

name: sinh
description: Computes the hyperbolic sine of a number.
short_description: Computes the hyperbolic sine of a number

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The input number.

name: skewness
description: Calculates the statistical skewness of a set of input values. Skewness is a measure of the asymmetry of the probability distribution of a real-valued random variable about its mean. A positive skew indicates a tail on the right side of the distribution, while a negative skew indicates a tail on the left side.
short_description: Calculates the statistical skewness of a set of values

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The set of values over which to calculate the skewness.

name: sniff_csv
description: Runs the CSV sniffer on a file to automatically detect its properties, such as the delimiter, quoting rules, column types, and whether a header row is present. It returns a table with a single row containing the detected properties. This function is useful for inspecting how DuckDB will interpret a CSV file before actually reading it with `read_csv`. The detection is performed on a sample of the file, and the sample size can be configured.
short_description: Automatically detects and returns the properties of a CSV file, such as delimiter, column types, and header.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the CSV file to be sniffed.

parameters.name: sample_size
parameters.description: The number of rows to sample for sniffing. Set to -1 to sample the entire file.

name: split_part
description: Splits a string by a specified separator and returns the part at a given 1-based index. If the index is out of bounds, it returns an empty string. If any of the arguments are NULL, the function returns NULL.
short_description: Splits a string by a separator and returns the part at a given index.

parameters.name: string
parameters.description: The string to be split.

parameters.name: separator
parameters.description: The separator to split the string by.

parameters.name: index
parameters.description: The 1-based index of the part to return.

name: sql_auto_complete
description: Attempts to autocomplete a given partial SQL query string. This function is part of the autocomplete extension and provides suggestions for completing SQL syntax, function names, table names, etc. It returns a table containing the suggested completions and the starting position of the token being completed.
short_description: Attempts autocompletion on the given query string.

parameters.name: query_string
parameters.description: The partial SQL query string to autocomplete.

name: sqlite_attach
description: Attaches a SQLite database file, allowing DuckDB to directly read from and write to its tables. This function is deprecated; it is recommended to use the `ATTACH` statement with `TYPE sqlite` instead.
short_description: Attaches a SQLite database file for direct querying (deprecated).
usage_example: SELECT * FROM sqlite_attach('sakila.db'); -- This function is deprecated. Use ATTACH 'sakila.db' (TYPE sqlite); instead.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the SQLite database file.

name: sqlite_scan
description: Reads data from a specified table within a SQLite database file. This function is part of the SQLite extension, which allows DuckDB to query SQLite databases directly. The recommended and more versatile way to interact with SQLite files is by using the ATTACH statement.
short_description: Reads data from a table within a SQLite database file.

parameters.name: database_path
parameters.description: The path to the SQLite database file.

parameters.name: table_name
parameters.description: The name of the table to read from within the SQLite database.

name: sqrt
description: Returns the square root of a given number. Note that for some special values, DuckDB throws errors rather than returning `NaN` for values outside their ranges of definition.
short_description: Computes the square root of a number.

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The number for which to calculate the square root.

name: st_affine
description: Applies an affine transformation to a geometry. The transformation is applied to all vertices of the geometry. For the 2D variant, the transformation matrix is defined as: | a b xoff | | d e yoff | | 0 0 1 |. For the 3D variant, the transformation matrix is defined as: | a b c xoff | | d e f yoff | | g h i zoff | | 0 0 0 1 |.
short_description: Applies an affine transformation to a geometry.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Coefficient of the 3D transformation matrix.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Coefficient of the 3D transformation matrix.

parameters.name: c
parameters.description: Coefficient of the 3D transformation matrix.

parameters.name: d
parameters.description: Coefficient of the 3D transformation matrix.

parameters.name: e
parameters.description: Coefficient of the 3D transformation matrix.

parameters.name: f
parameters.description: Coefficient of the 3D transformation matrix.

parameters.name: g
parameters.description: Coefficient of the 3D transformation matrix.

parameters.name: h
parameters.description: Coefficient of the 3D transformation matrix.

parameters.name: i
parameters.description: Coefficient of the 3D transformation matrix.

parameters.name: xoff
parameters.description: X-axis translation offset.

parameters.name: yoff
parameters.description: Y-axis translation offset.

parameters.name: zoff
parameters.description: Z-axis translation offset.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry.

parameters.name: a
parameters.description: Coefficient of the 2D transformation matrix.

parameters.name: b
parameters.description: Coefficient of the 2D transformation matrix.

parameters.name: d
parameters.description: Coefficient of the 2D transformation matrix.

parameters.name: e
parameters.description: Coefficient of the 2D transformation matrix.

parameters.name: xoff
parameters.description: X-axis translation offset.

parameters.name: yoff
parameters.description: Y-axis translation offset.

name: st_area
description: Computes the area of a geometry. The area is calculated in the units of the geometry's spatial reference system. For geometries that are not polygons or collections containing polygons (like points or linestrings), the function returns 0.0.
short_description: Computes the area of a geometry.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry.

name: st_area_spheroid
description: Returns the area of a geometry in meters, using an ellipsoidal model of the earth. The input geometry is assumed to be in the EPSG:4326 (WGS84) coordinate system, with [latitude, longitude] axis order, and the area is returned in square meters. This function uses the GeographicLib library, calculating the area using an ellipsoidal model of the earth. This is a highly accurate method for calculating the area of a polygon taking the curvature of the earth into account, but is also the slowest. Returns 0.0 for any geometry that is not a POLYGON, MULTIPOLYGON or GEOMETRYCOLLECTION containing polygon geometries.
short_description: Returns the area of a geometry in meters, using an ellipsoidal model of the earth

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry. Assumed to be in EPSG:4326 (WGS84) coordinate system with [latitude, longitude] axis order.

name: st_asgeojson
description: Returns the geometry as a GeoJSON fragment. This does not return a complete GeoJSON document, only the geometry fragment. To construct a complete GeoJSON document or feature, you can use the DuckDB JSON extension in conjunction with this function. This function supports geometries with Z values, but not M values; M values are ignored.
short_description: Returns the geometry as a GeoJSON fragment.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry object.

name: st_ashexwkb
description: Returns the geometry as a HEXWKB string.
short_description: Returns the geometry as a HEXWKB string

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry object.

name: st_assvg
description: Converts a geometry into an SVG fragment or path. The result is a string representing an SVG path element that can be used in an SVG document. The function allows specifying whether the path coordinates should be relative or absolute and sets the maximum number of digits for the coordinates. Points are formatted as cx/cy for absolute coordinates or x/y for relative coordinates.
short_description: Converts a geometry into an SVG fragment or path.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry.

parameters.name: relative
parameters.description: Specifies whether the path should be relative (true) or absolute (false).

parameters.name: precision
parameters.description: The maximum number of digits to use for the coordinates.

name: st_astext
description: Returns the geometry as a WKT (Well-Known Text) string.
short_description: Returns the geometry as a WKT string

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry.

name: st_aswkb
description: Returns the geometry as a WKB (Well-Known-Binary) blob.
short_description: Returns the geometry as a WKB (Well-Known-Binary) blob

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry.

name: st_azimuth
description: Returns the azimuth (a clockwise angle measured from north) of two points in radians.
short_description: Returns the azimuth (a clockwise angle measured from north) of two points in radians.

parameters.name: origin
parameters.description: The origin point.

parameters.name: target
parameters.description: The target point.

name: st_boundary
description: Returns the 'boundary' of a geometry.
short_description: Returns the boundary of a geometry

name: st_buffer
description: Returns a buffer around the input geometry at a specified distance. This is a planar operation and does not take the curvature of the earth into account. The function allows for customization of the buffer's appearance through parameters controlling the number of segments used to approximate curves, the style of end caps, the style of joins between segments, and the mitre limit for sharp corners.
short_description: Returns a buffer around the input geometry at a specified distance.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry.

parameters.name: distance
parameters.description: The target distance for the buffer, using the same units as the input geometry.

parameters.name: num_triangles
parameters.description: Represents how many triangles will be produced to approximate a quarter circle. The larger the number, the smoother the resulting geometry.

parameters.name: cap_style
parameters.description: Specifies the style for the end caps of the buffer.

parameters.name: join_style
parameters.description: Specifies the style for the joins between segments of the buffer.

parameters.name: mitre_limit
parameters.description: Only applies when join_style is 'JOIN_MITRE'. It is the ratio of the distance from the corner to the mitre point to the corner radius.

name: st_buildarea
description: Creates a polygonal geometry by attempting to 'fill in' the input geometry. Unlike ST_Polygonize, this function does not fill in holes.
short_description: Creates a polygonal geometry by attempting to 'fill in' the input geometry.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry.

name: st_centroid
description: Returns the centroid of a geometry.
short_description: Returns the centroid of a geometry

parameters.name: geom
parameters.parameter_data_type: GEOMETRY | POINT_2D | LINESTRING_2D | POLYGON_2D | BOX_2D | BOX_2DF
parameters.description: The input geometry.

name: st_collect
description: Collects a list of geometries into a single collection geometry. The type of the returned collection depends on the types of the input geometries. If all inputs are points, a MULTIPOINT is returned. If all are linestrings, a MULTILINESTRING is returned. If all are polygons, a MULTIPOLYGON is returned. For a mix of geometry types, a GEOMETRYCOLLECTION is returned. Empty and NULL geometries are ignored. If all inputs are empty or NULL, an empty GEOMETRYCOLLECTION is returned.
short_description: Collects a list of geometries into a single collection geometry.

parameters.name: geoms
parameters.description: A list of geometries to be collected.

name: st_collectionextract
description: Extracts geometries from a GeometryCollection into a typed multi-geometry. If the input is a GeometryCollection, the function returns a multi-geometry determined by the optional 'type' parameter (1 for MultiPoint, 2 for MultiLineString, 3 for MultiPolygon). If 'type' is omitted, it returns a multi-geometry matching the highest surface dimension of the contained geometries (e.g., a MultiPolygon if any polygons are present). The function processes nested GeometryCollections recursively. If the input is not a GeometryCollection, it is returned unchanged.
short_description: Extracts specific geometry types from a GeometryCollection.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry, typically a GeometryCollection.

parameters.name: type
parameters.description: The type of geometry to extract. 1 for MultiPoint, 2 for MultiLineString, 3 for MultiPolygon. If not provided, the function extracts the geometry type with the highest surface dimension.

name: st_concavehull
description: Computes the 'concave' hull of an input geometry. This function generates a polygon that contains all points of the source geometry. The level of concavity is controlled by a ratio parameter, where 1.0 produces the convex hull and 0 produces the most concave hull possible. You can also specify whether the resulting polygon is allowed to have holes.
short_description: Computes the concave hull of a geometry, with adjustable concavity.
usage_example: SELECT ST_AsText(ST_ConcaveHull(ST_Collect([ST_Point(0,0), ST_Point(1,1), ST_Point(0,1), ST_Point(1,0), ST_Point(0.5, 0.2)]), 0.5, false));

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry from which to compute the concave hull.

parameters.name: ratio
parameters.description: A value that dictates the level of concavity. A ratio of 1.0 returns the convex hull, while a ratio of 0 indicates the most concave hull possible.

parameters.name: allowHoles
parameters.description: A boolean value indicating whether the output polygon can contain holes. Set to true to allow holes.

name: st_contains
description: Returns true if the first geometry contains the second geometry. In contrast to ST_ContainsProperly, this function will also return true if the second geometry is contained strictly on the boundary of the first. A geometry always contains itself, but does not contain itself properly.
short_description: Returns true if the first geometry contains the second geometry

parameters.name: geom1
parameters.description: The first geometry.

parameters.name: geom2
parameters.description: The second geometry.

name: st_containsproperly
description: Returns true if the first geometry 'properly' contains the second geometry. In contrast to ST_Contains, this function does not return true if the second geometry is contained strictly on the boundary of the first. A geometry always contains itself, but does not properly contain itself.
short_description: Returns true if the first geometry properly contains the second, not just on its boundary.

parameters.name: geom1
parameters.description: The first geometry.

parameters.name: geom2
parameters.description: The second geometry.

name: st_convexhull
description: Returns the convex hull enclosing the geometry.
short_description: Computes the convex hull enclosing a geometry.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry.

name: st_coverageinvalidedges
description: Identifies and returns the invalid edges within a polygonal coverage. Invalid edges are those not shared by exactly two polygons. The function returns NULL if the input does not form a valid polygonal coverage or if all edges are valid. An optional tolerance can be specified for determining if edges are shared.
short_description: Returns invalid edges in a polygonal coverage, which are edges not shared by two polygons.

parameters.name: geoms
parameters.description: A list of geometries forming the polygonal coverage.

parameters.name: tolerance
parameters.description: The tolerance for considering edges as shared.

name: st_coverageinvalidedges_agg
description: An aggregate function that returns the invalid edges in a polygonal coverage. Invalid edges are those that are not shared by two polygons. This is useful for validating topological correctness in datasets. It returns NULL if the input is not a polygonal coverage or if the input is valid. An optional tolerance can be provided.
short_description: Returns the invalid edges of a coverage geometry.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The set of input geometries to check for invalid coverage edges.

parameters.name: tolerance
parameters.description: The tolerance for considering edges shared.

name: st_coveragesimplify
description: Simplifies the edges in a polygonal coverage, preserving the coverage by ensuring that there are no seams between the resulting simplified polygons. By default, the boundary of the coverage is also simplified, but this can be controlled with the optional 'simplify_boundary' parameter.
short_description: Simplifies the edges in a polygonal coverage while preserving topology
usage_example:
CREATE TABLE coverage_polygons AS SELECT column0 AS geom FROM VALUES (ST_GeomFromText('POLYGON((0 0, 5 10, 10 0, 0 0))')), (ST_GeomFromText('POLYGON((10 0, 15 10, 20 0, 10 0))'));

SELECT ST_AsText(ST_CoverageSimplify(list(geom), 1.0)) FROM coverage_polygons;

parameters.name: geoms
parameters.description: A list of geometries forming the polygonal coverage.

parameters.name: tolerance
parameters.description: The simplification tolerance value.

parameters.name: simplify_boundary
parameters.description: If true, the boundary of the coverage is also simplified.

name: st_coveragesimplify_agg
description: Simplifies a set of geometries that form a polygonal coverage. This process preserves the coverage, ensuring no seams or gaps are created between the resulting simplified polygons. A tolerance value controls the degree of simplification. Optionally, you can specify whether to simplify the boundary of the overall coverage.
short_description: Simplifies a set of geometries while maintaining coverage.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The set of geometries to simplify.

parameters.name: tolerance
parameters.description: The simplification tolerance value.

parameters.name: simplify_boundary
parameters.description: If true, the boundary of the coverage is also simplified.

name: st_coverageunion
description: Unions all geometries in a polygonal coverage into a single geometry. This may be faster than using ST_Union, but may use more memory.
short_description: Unions all geometries in a polygonal coverage into a single geometry.

parameters.name: geoms
parameters.description: A list of geometries forming a polygonal coverage.

name: st_coverageunion_agg
description: Aggregates a set of geometries into a single unioned geometry while maintaining coverage. This function is designed for polygonal coverages and may be faster than `ST_Union_Agg`, though it might consume more memory.
short_description: Unions a set of geometries while maintaining coverage

parameters.name: geometry
parameters.description: The input geometry to be included in the union.

name: st_coveredby
description: Returns true if the first geometry is 'covered by' the second geometry.
short_description: Checks if the first geometry is covered by the second geometry

parameters.name: geom1
parameters.description: The first geometry.

parameters.name: geom2
parameters.description: The second geometry.

name: st_covers
description: Returns true if the first geometry 'covers' the second geometry.
short_description: Returns true if the first geometry covers the second geometry

parameters.name: geom1
parameters.description: The first geometry.

parameters.name: geom2
parameters.description: The second geometry.

name: st_crosses
description: Returns true if the first geometry crosses the second geometry.
short_description: Returns true if two geometries cross each other

parameters.name: geom1
parameters.description: The first geometry.

parameters.name: geom2
parameters.description: The second geometry.

name: st_difference
description: Returns the "difference" between two geometries.
short_description: Returns the geometric difference between two geometries

parameters.name: geom1
parameters.description: The first geometry.

parameters.name: geom2
parameters.description: The second geometry.

name: st_dimension
description: Returns the topological dimension of a geometry. For POINT and MULTIPOINT geometries, it returns 0. For LINESTRING and MULTILINESTRING, it returns 1. For POLYGON and MULTIPOLYGON, it returns 2. For a GEOMETRYCOLLECTION, it returns the maximum dimension of the contained geometries, or 0 if the collection is empty.
short_description: Returns the topological dimension of a geometry (0 for points, 1 for lines, 2 for polygons).

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry.

name: st_disjoint
description: Returns true if the geometries are disjoint.
short_description: Returns true if two geometries are disjoint (have no points in common)

parameters.name: geom1
parameters.description: The first geometry.

parameters.name: geom2
parameters.description: The second geometry.

name: st_distance
description: Returns the planar distance between two geometries. The distance is calculated in the coordinate system of the geometries. Z coordinates are ignored in the calculation.
short_description: Returns the planar distance between two geometries

parameters.name: geom1
parameters.description: The first input geometry.

parameters.name: geom2
parameters.description: The second input geometry.

name: st_distance_geos
description: Returns the planar distance between two geometries.
short_description: Returns the planar distance between two geometries.

name: st_distance_sphere
description: Returns the haversine (great circle) distance between two geometries. This function only supports POINT geometries. The distance is returned in meters. The input is expected to be in WGS84 (EPSG:4326) coordinates, using a [latitude, longitude] axis order.
short_description: Returns the haversine (great circle) distance between two point geometries in meters.

parameters.name: geom1
parameters.description: The first point geometry.

parameters.name: geom2
parameters.description: The second point geometry.

name: st_distance_spheroid
description: Returns the distance between two geometries in meters using an ellipsoidal model of the earth's surface. The input geometry is assumed to be in the EPSG:4326 (WGS84) coordinate system, with [latitude, longitude] axis order. This function uses the GeographicLib library to solve the inverse geodesic problem, calculating the distance between two points using an ellipsoidal model of the earth. This is a highly accurate method for calculating the distance between two arbitrary points taking the curvature of the earth's surface into account, but is also the slowest.
short_description: Returns the distance between two geometries in meters using an ellipsoidal model of the earth's surface.
usage_example:
-- Note: the coordinates are in WGS84 and [latitude, longitude] axis order
-- Whats the distance between New York and Amsterdam (JFK and AMS airport)?
SELECT st_distance_spheroid(
st_point(40.6446, -73.7797),
st_point(52.3130, 4.7725)
);
----
5863418.7459356235
-- Roughly 5863km!

parameters.name: p1
parameters.description: The first point geometry.

name: st_dwithin_spheroid
description: Returns a boolean indicating if two POINT_2D geometries are within a target distance in meters, using an ellipsoidal model of the Earth's surface. The input geometries are assumed to be in the EPSG:4326 (WGS84) coordinate system, with latitude, longitude axis order. This function uses the GeographicLib library to solve the inverse geodesic problem, providing a highly accurate method for calculating the distance between two points by taking the Earth's curvature into account. This method is the most accurate but also the slowest.
short_description: Checks if two points are within a specified distance in meters, using a highly accurate ellipsoidal model of the Earth.

parameters.name: p1
parameters.description: The first point geometry. Assumed to be in WGS84 (EPSG:4326) with [latitude, longitude] axis order.

parameters.name: p2
parameters.description: The second point geometry. Assumed to be in WGS84 (EPSG:4326) with [latitude, longitude] axis order.

parameters.name: distance
parameters.description: The target distance threshold in meters.

short_description: Flips the X and Y coordinates of a geometry.

name: st_force2d
description: Forces the vertices of a geometry to have X and Y components. This function will drop any Z and M values from the input geometry, if present. If the input geometry is already 2D, it will be returned as is.
short_description: Forces the vertices of a geometry to have only X and Y components, dropping any Z and M values.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry.

name: st_force3dm
description:
Forces the vertices of a geometry to have X, Y and M components. The behavior depends on the input geometry's existing components:
- If the input geometry has a Z component but no M component, the Z component will be replaced with the new M value.
- If the input geometry has an M component but no Z component, it will be returned as is.
- If the input geometry has both a Z component and an M component, the Z component will be removed.
- Otherwise, if the input geometry has neither a Z or M component, the new M value will be added to the vertices of the input geometry.
short_description: Forces the vertices of a geometry to have X, Y and M components.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry.

parameters.name: m
parameters.description: The M value to apply to the vertices.

name: st_force3dz
description: Forces the vertices of a geometry to have X, Y, and Z components. The behavior depends on the input geometry's existing components. If the input has an M component but no Z component, the M component is replaced with the new Z value. If it already has a Z component but no M component, it is returned unchanged. If it has both Z and M components, the M component is removed. If it has neither Z nor M components, the new Z value is added to all vertices.
short_description: Forces the vertices of a geometry to have X, Y, and Z components.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry.

parameters.name: z
parameters.description: The Z value to apply to the geometry's vertices.

name: st_force4d
description: Forces the vertices of a geometry to have X, Y, Z, and M components. If the input geometry has a Z component but no M component, the new M value will be added. If it has an M component but no Z component, the new Z value will be added. If it has both Z and M components, it is returned as is. If it has neither, both the new Z and M values will be added.
short_description: Forces the vertices of a geometry to have X, Y, Z, and M components

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry.

parameters.name: z
parameters.description: The Z value to add to the geometry's vertices.

parameters.name: m
parameters.description: The M value to add to the geometry's vertices.

name: st_hilbert
description: Encodes X and Y coordinates into a Hilbert curve index. The function can take explicit X and Y coordinates, a geometry object, or a box object. When a geometry or box is provided, its center point is used for encoding. The encoding is performed relative to a specified bounding box. If no bounding box is provided for a geometry input, the index is mapped to the full range of a single-precision float.

name: st_read
description: Reads and imports a variety of geospatial vector file formats using the GDAL library. This table function allows querying spatial data from files as if they were DuckDB tables. It also provides 'replacement scans' for common formats like ESRI ShapeFile (.shp), GeoPackage (.gpkg), and FlatGeoBuf (.fgb), allowing direct querying of these files. Various optional parameters are available to control the reading process, such as specifying a layer, applying spatial filters, or passing driver-specific options.
short_description: Reads and imports various geospatial file formats using the GDAL library.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the file to read.

parameters.name: layer
parameters.description: The name of the layer to read from the file. If NULL, the first layer is returned. Can also be a layer index (starting at 0).

parameters.name: open_options
parameters.description: A list of key-value pairs that are passed to the GDAL driver to control the opening of the file. E.g., the GeoJSON driver supports a FLATTEN_NESTED_ATTRIBUTES=YES option to flatten nested attributes.

parameters.name: keep_wkb
parameters.description: If set, the table function will return geometries in a wkb_geometry column with the type WKB_BLOB (which can be cast to BLOB) instead of GEOMETRY. This is useful if you want to use DuckDB with more exotic geometry subtypes that DuckDB spatial doesnt support representing in the GEOMETRY type yet.

parameters.name: sequential_layer_scan
parameters.description: If set to true, the table function will scan through all layers sequentially and return the first layer that matches the given layer name. This is required for some drivers to work properly, e.g., the OSM driver.

parameters.name: spatial_filter
parameters.description: If set to a WKB blob, the table function will only return rows that intersect with the given WKB geometry. Some drivers may support efficient spatial filtering natively, in which case it will be pushed down. Otherwise the filtering is done by GDAL which may be much slower.

parameters.name: spatial_filter_box
parameters.description: If set to a BOX_2D, the table function will only return rows that intersect with the given bounding box. Similar to spatial_filter.

parameters.name: sibling_files
parameters.description: A list of sibling files that are required to open the file. E.g., the ESRI Shapefile driver requires a .shx file to be present. Although most of the time these can be discovered automatically.

parameters.name: allowed_drivers
parameters.description: A list of GDAL driver names that are allowed to be used to open the file. If empty, all drivers are allowed.

name: st_read_meta
description: Reads metadata from one or more geospatial files using the GDAL library. This function is a companion to ST_Read, but instead of reading the file's contents, it scans and returns metadata. The most detailed information is found in the 'layers' column, which is a complex nested structure of STRUCT and LIST types.
short_description: Reads metadata from geospatial files using the GDAL library.
usage_example:
-- Find the coordinate reference system authority name and code for the first layers first geometry column in the file
SELECT
    layers[1].geometry_fields[1].crs.auth_name as name,
    layers[1].geometry_fields[1].crs.auth_code as code
FROM st_read_meta('../../tmp/data/amsterdam_roads.fgb');

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path or a list of paths to the geospatial file(s).

name: st_readosm
description: Reads compressed OpenStreetMap data directly from a .osm.pbf file. This function uses multithreading and zero-copy protobuf parsing, making it significantly faster than the standard ST_Read() OSM driver. It outputs raw OSM data (Nodes, Ways, Relations) without constructing geometries. Geometries like POINTs can be constructed from the output, and more complex geometries like LINESTRINGs and POLYGONs can be built by joining nodes and references in SQL, though this can be memory-intensive. The function also provides a 'replacement scan', allowing direct querying of .osm.pbf files as if they were tables.
short_description: Reads compressed OpenStreetMap data directly from a .osm.pbf file.
usage_example:
SELECT *
FROM ST_ReadOSM('tmp/data/germany.osm.pbf')
WHERE tags['highway'] != []
LIMIT 5;

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the .osm.pbf file to read.

name: st_readshp
description: Reads a Shapefile without relying on the GDAL library.
short_description: Reads a Shapefile without relying on the GDAL library.

parameters.name: file_path
parameters.description: The path to the Shapefile (.shp) to read.

parameters.name: geom

parameters.description: The grid size to which the coordinates will be rounded. For example, a precision of 0.01 will round coordinates to two decimal places.

name: st_tileenvelope
description: Generates tile envelope rectangular polygons from specified zoom level and tile indices. This is used in MVT generation to select the features corresponding to the tile extent. The envelope is in the Web Mercator coordinate reference system (EPSG:3857). The tile pyramid starts at zoom level 0, corresponding to a single tile for the world. Each zoom level doubles the number of tiles in each direction, such that zoom level 1 is 2 tiles wide by 2 tiles high, zoom level 2 is 4 tiles wide by 4 tiles high, and so on. Tile indices start at [x=0, y=0] at the top left, and increase down and right. For example, at zoom level 2, the top right tile is [x=3, y=0], the bottom left tile is [x=0, y=3], and the bottom right is [x=3, y=3].
short_description: Generates a rectangular polygon representing a tile envelope from a specified zoom level and tile indices.

parameters.name: tile_zoom
parameters.description: The zoom level of the tile.

parameters.name: tile_x
parameters.description: The x-index of the tile.

parameters.name: tile_y
parameters.description: The y-index of the tile.

name: st_touches

short_description: Returns true if two geometries touch at their boundaries

parameters.name: geom1

name: st_transform
description: Transforms a geometry between two coordinate systems. The source and target coordinate systems can be specified using any format that the PROJ library supports. An optional boolean parameter can be used to force the input and output geometries to be interpreted as having an [easting, northing] coordinate axis order, which is useful when dealing with coordinate systems like WGS84/EPSG:4326 that are defined with a [latitude, longitude] axis order but are often used with [longitude, latitude]. DuckDB's spatial extension includes its own copy of the PROJ database, so available coordinate systems may differ from other GIS software.
short_description: Transforms a geometry between two coordinate systems.

parameters.name: geom
parameters.description: The input geometry to transform.

parameters.name: source_crs
parameters.description: The source coordinate system, specified in a format supported by the PROJ library.

parameters.name: target_crs
parameters.description: The target coordinate system, specified in a format supported by the PROJ library.

parameters.name: always_xy
parameters.description: If true, forces the input and output geometries to be interpreted as having an [easting, northing] coordinate axis order, regardless of the CRS definition.

short_description: Calculates the sample standard deviation of a set of values.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE measurements(val INT); INSERT INTO measurements VALUES (3), (3), (9); SELECT stddev_samp(val) FROM measurements; -- 3.4641016151377544

parameters.name: x
parameters.description: The input values.

name: strftime
description: Converts a temporal value (date, timestamp, or timestamp with time zone) to a string according to a specified format string. The format string uses specifiers like '%Y' for year and '%m' for month to define the output structure.

parameters.name: temporal_value

parameters.name: format
parameters.description: A string of format specifiers that defines the output format. See the date/time documentation for a list of all specifiers.

name: string_agg
description: Concatenates the non-null string representations of all input values into a single string. A separator can be specified, which will be placed between the concatenated values. If no separator is provided, a comma (',') is used by default. The order of concatenation can be controlled using an `ORDER BY` clause within the function call, as this is an order-sensitive aggregate function.
short_description: Concatenates string values from a column into a single string, with an optional separator.
usage_example: CREATE TABLE tbl AS SELECT s FROM range(1, 4) r(s); SELECT string_agg(s, ', ' ORDER BY s DESC) AS countdown FROM tbl;

parameters.name: expression
parameters.description: The expression or column whose values will be concatenated.

parameters.name: separator
parameters.description: The separator string to insert between values. Defaults to ',' if not provided.

name: substring
